File.Name: 9405a_9331-100.jpg
Notes: 48909915 Tired but willing Dunn Middle School students enjoying the view looking west from the High Mount (1581 feet) on the flank of El Montanon Peak on the east end of Santa Cruz Island. The islands really are a paradise for hiking and natural history - like going back in time to early California. (Santa Cruz Island, 31 March 2009)

File.Name: 9405b_9331-075.jpg
Notes: 48909583 Beginning our trek up 1808 foot high El Montanon peak - the highest point on the east end of Santa Cruz Island. It was a wonderful (though strenuous!) hike through the grassland and up into the native vegitation along the rugged summit ridge. (Santa Cruz Island, 31 March 2009)

File.Name: 9405c_9331-019.jpg
Notes:
48909181 This cat-sized Island Fox (Urocyon littoralis) visited our campsite on Santa Cruz Island! I guess it's a female fox - a vixen - and it probably lost it's right ear in a sexual encounter with her mate. (Santa Cruz Island, 30 March 2009)

I remember that these little Island Foxes were once common, but they became scarce and endangered because of the influx of Golden Eagles on the islands.

It's been a grand bit of biological engineering! Originally there were fish-eating Bald Eagles on the islands. They disappeared in the 1960's due to a build up of the pesticide DDT in the marine food chain. Golden Eagles took their place, but they prey on land critters - postly wild pigs and foxes. When the pigs were finally removed from the islands, the eagles focused on the foxes, and the fox population dropped to almost nothing. The Golden Eagles are now relocated, Bald Eagles have been reintroduced, and the foxes are coming back.

File.Name: 9405d_9330-042.jpg
Notes: 48908588 I almost stepped on this beautiful Horned Lark (Eremophila alpestris) while hiking through the grasslands on Santa Cruz Island. It's an adult female bird, and you can see the little "horns" above its eyes. (Santa Cruz Island, 1 April 2009)

File.Name: 9405e_9401-029.jpg
Notes: 48908274 This is an Early Onion (Allium praecox) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family hiding in the grasslands on the east end of Santa Cruz Island. The grasslands are mostly introduced annuals, but there are some interesting natives mixed in. (Santa Cruz Island, 1 April 2009)

File.Name: 9405f_9401-025.jpg
Notes:
48932815 Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) collecting some soft fluff for her nest from a native Cattail (Typha domingensis?) seed head in our little "pond". (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2009)

We're flying up to Portland, Oregon early Monday morning to visit our grandkids over spring break. I'll be back next weekend with new photos of the twins!

File.Name: 9406a_9405-012.jpg
Notes: 48932786 Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) collecting some soft fluff for her nest from a native Cattail (Typha domingensis?) seed head in our little "pond". (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2009)

File.Name: 9406b_9405-010.jpg
Notes: 48932763 Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) collecting some soft fluff for her nest from a native Cattail (Typha domingensis?) seed head in our little "pond". (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2009)

File.Name: 9406c_9405-009.jpg
Notes:
49100397 My perfect grandkids Kelvin and Ottokar love the little fishies that hang over their changing table. They are starting to swat at them to make them move! (Portland, Oregon, 8 April 2009)

Mostly it was feed, pee, poop, and sleep - you know how babies are, though they are also building motor skills and getting the idea that they can change their environment by acting. It's interesting how unaware of each other the twins seem, but I'm sure that will change. It was a good visit, and we managed to give their parents Ewan and Roz some time for a break. Our next visit will be at home in June for the Live Oak Music Festival!

File.Name: 9409a_9408-150.jpg
Notes: 49099707 Happy Grandma Julie holding our new grandson Ottokar (?) in the garden. Little Otto has a birthmark on the back of his head. We just got back from a short but wonderful visit with our twin grandkids Kelvin and Ottokar in Portland, Oregon. We had a couple of perfect spring days before the usual rain settled in. (Portland, Oregon, 7 April 2009)

File.Name: 9409b_9407-059.jpg
Notes: 49099425 Self-portrait of Treebeard with my new grandson Kelvin (?) shot against the bathroom mirror. I flipped the photo left-right so the words on the camera would read right. (Portland, Oregon, 8 April 2009)

File.Name: 9409c_9408-160.jpg
Notes:
49103488 It sounds strange to say, but I love flies, and all the details of flies! I found this unknown Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native California Figwort (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae family this morning. It looks like a Robber Fly (family Asilidae) except that it lacks the bristles on the front legs. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2009)

Busy times - We got back from Portland last night, and we're off on an adventure tomorrow with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to the Chimineas Ranch/Caliente Range in the Santa Barbara backcountry! I'll be back late, so probably no photos tomorrow.

File.Name: 9410a_9410-165.jpg
Notes: 49102704 I believe this is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) - in April, close enough - resting on a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata, family Lamiaceae) in the garden, with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2009)

File.Name: 9410b_9410-205.jpg
Notes: 49102226 Giant Crane Fly (Holousia grandis or kin) in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hiding in the native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the woods today. It was a gloomy day, and few insects were on the wing. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2009)

File.Name: 9410c_9410-184.jpg
Notes:
49168442 I guess this is the Coast Horned Lizard (Phrynosoma blainvillii) - aka a "Horny Toad". I haven't seen them for years in the coastal mountains, but we found four of them in 15 minutes on a road stop in the Chimineas Unit of the Carrizo Plains Ecological Reserve. It's all about the ants. The sand-colored lizards don't like the invading Argentine Ants (Linepithema humile). (Chimineas Ranch, Cuyama Valley, 11 April 2009)

I have many more photos of our time at the Chimineas Ranch. I'll save them for later.

File.Name: 9411a_9411-142.jpg
Notes: 49167558 Talk about "cryptic camouflage" - this colorful Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) is a master! (Chimineas Ranch, Cuyama Valley, 11 April 2009)

File.Name: 9411b_9411-027.jpg
Notes:
49166566 Pretty little Fly (order Diptera) perched on a flower of Hillside Daisy (Monolopia lanceolata in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. I guess it's a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae). (Chimineas Ranch, Cuyama Valley, 11 April 2009)

We went for a drive/hike on the Chimineas Unit of the Carrizo Plains Ecological Reserve with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society guided by botanist George Butterworth. Oh my, the flowers - but I was most interested in the insects!

File.Name: 9411c_9411-019.jpg
Notes:
49170865 A pretty Crab Spider (Thomisidae) folded up on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

I spent yesterday at the Chimineas Unit of the Carrizo Plains Ecological Reserve with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society guided by botanist George Butterworth - but today's photo are always better! I'll post more photos from the Chimineas Ranch trip soon.

File.Name: 9412a_9412-181.jpg
Notes: 49170498 Pretty peaches 'n cream colored Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly resting in the woods. No wonder it's tired - I chased it for about 15 minutes! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

File.Name: 9412b_9412-172.jpg
Notes: 49170243 I guess this is an immature Katydid (family Tettigoniidae - or some other kind of Long-Horned Grasshopper (family Tettigoniidae) - in the order Orthoptera). Yes, it was hard to spot this critter! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

File.Name: 9412c_9412-157.jpg
Notes:
49203757 The native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) don't only destroy - they also create life, though it may not be the life we want! These ants seem to be tending both the Scale Insects (Coccoidea) and the Aphids (family Aphididae) in the order Homoptera on a Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. I reckon those gray slugs on the branch are immature scale insects. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

I understand that ants tend aphids to obtain sweet honeydew, but why would they tend scale insects?
CommonNames: Ants; Scale Insects; Aphids; Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn

File.Name: 9413a_9413-221.jpg
Notes: 49202338 Native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) using the same strategy as in my [Previous] photo - this time on an unfortunate earthworm. The strategy is to stretch out their prey and then cut it into pieces alive - hence most of the ants have it by the nose. These nasty critters might be the Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale). (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

File.Name: 9413b_9412-191.jpg
Notes:
49201859 The native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are getting frisky. These ants are literally tearing a caterpillar Lepidoptera apart. They have a strategy to stretch out their prey and then cut it into pieces. The nasty critters might be the Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale). (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

This is a packed photo - it also shows new sexual galls of the Two Horned Gall Wasp on the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) catkin in the front.

File.Name: 9413c_9413-276.jpg
Notes: 49239298 This beautiful Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly showed up on a flowering Peach tree (Prunus persica) in the garden on Monday. It's the first one I've seen this year. It seems small for a Western Tiger Swallowtail - maybe the first generation of the butterflies after winter is smaller? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9414a_9413-291.jpg
Notes: 49238571 This is a tiny "Garbage line spider" (genus Cyclosa, an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae) which leaves a trail of its past victims in a vertical line down its perfect web. It's adding a new victim (a tiny fly) to the garbage line. I shot this spider in "high-key" style against the overexposed cloudy sky - I didn't have any choice! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9414b_9413-288.jpg
Notes: 49237730 A perfect "Garbage line spider" (genus Cyclosa, an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae) which leaves a trail of its victims in a vertical line down the web (not visible here). It has an interesting characteristic bottle-shaped extension to its abdomen. This spider was rather large for it's kind. The slightest breeze would ruin the photo - I waited for the moment between the breezes. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9414c_9413-251.jpg
Notes:
49277213 I'm still noticing insect galls after my science fair project a few weeks ago! This is a galled leaf of Big-berry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family, with one of the (I guess)Manzanita Fold Gall Aphids (Tamalia cowenii) that causes the gall sitting on top of it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

Nothing is stranger than these insect galls. Galls are the abnormal growths in plants caused mostly by certain specialized insects which lay their eggs that are nutured by the abnormal plant growth they initiate. It's a neat trick - to "infect" a plant and trick it into providing food and shelter!
CommonNames: Big-berry Manzanita; Heath; Manzanita Fold Gall Aphids

File.Name: 9415a_9412-188.jpg
Notes: 49276619 A little Snakefly (order Neuroptera, family Raphidiidae, genus Agulla) insect resting on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family in the woods. They feed on small soft-bodied insects like aphids. If you have a garden, they are your friends! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)
ScientificName: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: 9415b_9413-243.jpg
Notes: 49276258 Strange little Mugwort Weevil - at least that's what it should be called. It's a tiny weevil beetle in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occurs on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. They have a strange elephant-like face! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

File.Name: 9415c_9412-198.jpg
Notes:
49311689 Remarkable new flowers of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home on the burn site of the "Gap Fire" last summer. This plant is a "fire-follower" that only appears after wildfires. The var. inezianus plants of the species are endemic to our mountains - they have a greenish gland and are much larger than the usual species. This is only a small part of the inflorescence. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

I got this photo on the burn site of the "Gap Fire" last summer. The fire-follower plants are finally starting to appear. I'm glad, because I'm leading a hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society next month to look for fire-follower plants!

You can see what the wildfire looked like from my home here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37369803
(and [Next] from there).

File.Name: 9416a_9413-267.jpg
Notes: 49310966 This is a pretty flower of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This is another fire-follower plant growing after the "Gap Fire" last summer. Note the little Beetle (order Coleoptera) on the flower petal. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9416b_9413-254.jpg
Notes:
49310514 Pretty little Suncup flowers - I guess Camissonia intermedia - in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) plant family. As this prostrate stem keeps growing, new yellow flowers will open and the old flowers will turn red with age as they are left behind. The seeds will develop in the "inferior ovary" below the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

I got this photo on the burn site of the "Gap Fire" last summer. The fire-follower plants are finally starting to appear. You can see what the wildfire looked like from home here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37369803
(and [Next] from there).

File.Name: 9416c_9413-256.jpg
Notes:
49343649 Like a butterfly in the woods! Intense colors of a native Larkspur - I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family - growing in the woods along our driveway. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

The colors of the flowers are so intense that I can't capture them with my digital camera. They look deep indigo blue to the eye, but my camera always picks up the maroon violet colors that I don't see.

File.Name: 9417a_9412-209.jpg
Notes: 49343402 Elegant new flowers of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflowers in the Iris (Iridaceae) family growing in the woods near home. It's our only native Iris. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9417b_9413-249.jpg
Notes: 49343268 Elegant new flowers of Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) family with the natural lighting just right. This native hides in the woods and is not common. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2009)

File.Name: 9417c_9412-169.jpg
Notes:
49343050 Another photo showing the remarkable endemic flowers of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home on the burn site of the "Gap Fire" last summer. This shot shows the habitat and size of these beautiful plants. See my [Previous] photo.

The burn area is still closed to hiking, and I'm trying to obey the rules. I got this photo along West Camino Cielo Road near the fire line. It was probably only singed in a backfire, but that was enough to bring out these fire-follower plants. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

File.Name: 9417d_9413-265.jpg
Notes:
49372938 It's a tender moment between two little Mugwort Weevils - at least that's what they should be called. They are tiny weevil beetles in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occur on the leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. They have strange elephant-like faces with the antennae down below the eyes. Here they are making babies. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

It was breezy today, but that's better than the gale-force winds we've had all week! It's starting to warm up and feel like the drought year it is. The rains have been well-spaced to hide the fact, but we've only had 55% normal rainfall this season. It will be an interesting summer...

File.Name: 9418a_9418-031.jpg
Notes:
49372388 This is the business end of a perfect little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) that I found in the woods today. It looks like it's trying to suck the dry grass stem it's perched on - or maybe it's trying to hide from the photographer? (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

Or is it actually eating the dried grass stem as a kind of "displacement activity"?

File.Name: 9418b_9418-012.jpg
Notes: 49372081 Just a Fly (order Diptera) perched on a Salvia (in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) leaf in the garden. I guess it's a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae. It sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)
CommonNames: Fly; Mint; Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly

File.Name: 9418c_9418-005.jpg
Notes: 49413112 It seems early in the year to find this beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, but here it is! These are the butterflies that emerge from the caterpillars on plants in the Scrophulariaceae family. This is the first one I've captured this year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9419a_9419-074.jpg
Notes: 49412782 This is not quite sharp where it matters, but I love the sky reflected in the beating wings of this Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. It seems early in the year to find these remarkable diurnal (daytime) moths. It caught me by surprise today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Wild Sweet Pea; Pea

File.Name: 9419b_9419-061.jpg
Notes:
49412258 Look close little fly, the flowers sometimes have teeth!

This pretty little didn't look close enough, and it became lunch for this white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the woods.

Spider and fly are perched on a native flower of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae family growing in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Wild Cucumber; Chilicothe; California Manroot

File.Name: 9419c_9419-106.jpg
Notes: 49448258 Pretty golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of a Euphorbia (family Euphorbiaceae) in the garden - maybe it's the "Mediterranean Spurge" (Euphorbia characias ssp. wulfenii). You can see the fly's vacuum cleaner-like proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Euphorbia; Euphorbiaceae; Euphorbia characias; wulfenii
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Euphorbia; Euphorbiaceae; Euphorbia characias; wulfenii

File.Name: 9420a_9419-048.jpg
Notes:
49447573 Little wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a little wild flower of California Figwort aka the well-named Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

Bee and flower seem made for each other. See the perfect fit between the bee's abdomen and the flower's pistil - sticking out like a tongue on the right side of the flower. I guess it's a Megachilidae bee that collects pollen on its abdomen instead of in baskets on its hind legs.
CommonNames: Bee; California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: 9420b_9418-041.jpg
Notes:
49446844 I knew even with its wings closed that this was a different Checkerspot butterfly than my [Previous] photo, because it has dark antennae rather than golden yellow. Little differences like that become important when you study local butterflies!

This is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly hanging onto the weedy brome grasses that devil us in our woods - probably Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) or kin. It's the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9420c_9419-072.jpg
Notes:
49481130 A little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for breakfast on a single flower of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

Are these spiders really color-shifters?
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Blue Dick; Brodiaea; Lily

File.Name: 9421a_9419-079.jpg
Notes:
49480544 Me and my shadow...

Handsome Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) perched on a leaflet of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

Maybe it's the same little bee that's working the flowers of California Figwort (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in my [Previous] photo? The long antennae match!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Wild Sweet Pea; Pea; California Figwort; Figwort
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 9421b_9419-059.jpg
Notes:
49480118 So many butterflies, but I rarely find caterpillars!

Here one that I did find on a tangle of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. It's stretched out to look like a twig. No wonder I rarely find them! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9421c_9412-194.jpg
Notes: 49548107 I guess this critter is a strange little Treehopper (family Membracidae in the order Homoptera) insect perched near some buds of Styrax redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9422a_9413-242.jpg
Notes: 49549782 Perfect new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The plants are very glandular, like little pinheads. These are native plants, but this photo is from our garden. Yes, the hummingbirds like them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9423a_9419-056.jpg
Notes: 49549485 New flowers of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home. These flowers are fire-followers, but a few plants were flowering on a hillside that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - and not burned. Some of the fire-followers really need fire, but some just need clearing to let the sun in. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2009)

File.Name: 9423b_9419-084.jpg
Notes:
49581104 I found this handsome brown-spored Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family mushroom growing in the garden under the groundcover Bellflowers in the Campanulaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

I reckon the mushrooms are just about done for the year, though I found a few last weekend. We had temperatures in the high 90s (38 degrees Celsius!) this week, and the woods are already getting summer-dry. Fortunately it's cooled off for the weekend.
ScientificName: Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Campanulaceae
associatedTaxa: Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Campanulaceae

File.Name: 9424a_9413-225.jpg
Notes: 49580533 I guess this late-season mushroom is Volvariella speciosa in the Pluteacea family. It looks like an amanita, but it doesn't have a ring and it has pink spores. I didn't collect the mushroom, so I'm really not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

File.Name: 9424b_9412-185.jpg
Notes: 49580141 This late-season mushroom is called "The Blusher" - Amanita rubescens, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods last weekend. The blushing reddish colors are distinctive. It has white warts on the cap to show that it really is an amanita, but the "indistinct volva" is hardly reconizable on the bulbous base. It may be edible, but I'm not interested in eating white amanitas - whether they blush or not! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2009)

File.Name: 9424c_9418-024.jpg
Notes:
49618047 Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly unrolling its proboscis to enter a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family in the canyon today. Note the pollen from previous flowers on its coiled proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

It was so windy today, I was lucky to get any photos. I waited for those calm moments between the gusts, and was rewarded. It's been an interesting weather week - temps near 100 degrees (38 degrees Celsius) early this week, but today it's cold and windy. That's our spring!

File.Name: 9425a_9425-056.jpg
Notes: 49617284 Mating pair of tiny Weevil beetles (family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

File.Name: 9425b_9425-035.jpg
Notes:
49616734 I'm not an entomologist, but I usually know at least which ORDER an insect is. But I'm not sure about this critter I captured by the creek today. Maybe it's a moth-like Caddisfly (order Trichoptera)? The insect is perched on a branch of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

It was so windy today, I was lucky to get any photos!

File.Name: 9425c_9425-060.jpg
Notes:
49653100 This Lady Beetle (aka "Ladybug") - family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera - seems like it's made for this native wildflower of Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home. Other Lady Beetles aren't tall enough to catch the pollen like these do. Maybe it's a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata)? I guess it's native! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

I got this photo - and all my photos today - on the regenerating burn of the "Gap Wildfire" last summer. It was windy again, but I waited for those still moments between the gusts.

File.Name: 9426a_9426-093.jpg
Notes: 49651134 This beautiful Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum in the Papaveraceae family) is the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns. I found this one today growing on the site of the "Gap Wildfire" last summer. I rarely find it even when I'm looking and it "should" be there! It's a real poppy - it has nodding buds, crinkly petals, and a seed pod shaped like a skinny Papaver somniferum pod. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

File.Name: 9426b_9426-116.jpg
Notes: 49650374 Perfect Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha or Plagiobothrys) in the Borage (Boraginaceae) plant family. You need a microscope to tell these flowers apart, and I really don't care. I captured this butterfly today on the regenerating burn of the "Gap Wildfire" last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

File.Name: 9426c_9426-112.jpg
Notes:
49684896 This pretty adult Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect has just emerged from its larval skin, which is visible between its legs in the photo. The head of the snakefly reminds me of the tripod aliens from the old scifi movie "The War of the Worlds"!

See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

File.Name: 9427b_9426-123.jpg
Notes:
49683809 Another photo of the Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata) in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera - working new flowers of the fire-follower Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. (Whew - that's a lot of Latin!)

Note the intimate encounter between the pollen-bearing stamen of the flower and the insect. These particular lady beetles are just the right size and VW shape to pollinate the flower. It a great example of evolution in action - or nature's design if you prefer! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

The female pistils are interesting too. They start with their three arms held high, but they drop into receptive position as the pollen gets used up. This is an example of protandry - male function comes before female function. (The opposite situation is protogyny, but it's not so common.)

(San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

It's a too-common weedy grass (family Poaceae) growing along the road near our mailbox. If you choose to sow your wild oats, this is what you've sown - please sow them somewhere else! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2009)

File.Name: 9428c_9426-083.jpg
Notes:
49758145 Perfect Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha or Plagiobothrys) in the Borage (Boraginaceae) plant family. I've been working on school photos for hours. I'm tired!

File.Name: 9429a_9426-113.jpg
Notes:
49787355 "Gotta sing! Gotta dance!"

It was windy last Saturday morning, so I put on my "big glass" (a humble Tamron 200-500 mm zoom) and set out to photograph the House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that are nesting in the houses we put out on our upstairs porch. We have at least three nesting pairs this year.

This bird is perched on an old Adirondack chair with peeling paint, singing it's brains out! The female wren is sitting inside her nest, but the dutiful male brings her food and surrounds her with near-constant sound to remind her of his presence. It's really very sweet. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

File.Name: 9430a_9425-006.jpg
Notes: 49786018 Pretty little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird perched on a succulent on our downstairs porch. The wrens are nesting again on the upstairs porch where we put out houses for them. Soon we will have baby wrens! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

File.Name: 9430b_9425-010.jpg
Notes: 49785851 Little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird perched on a succulent on our downstairs porch. The wrens are nesting again on the upstairs porch where we put out houses for them. Soon we will have baby wrens! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

File.Name: 9430c_9425-014.jpg
Notes:
49824372 This is one of the many tiny moths - the "microlepidoptera" - perched for the day on a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. It is one of the "bird poop" moths that looks something like a bird dropping. It's really quite beautiful for being so small. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

*Sigh* If I had another lifetime to live, I would really like to learn the little moths!

File.Name: 9501a_9425-040.jpg
Notes:
49823943 Just when I thought the mushrooms were all done for the year, we have a huge clump of these Shaggy Parasol Mushrooms (Lepiota rachodes) coming up in a pile of wood chips from our tree work last year. They are usually edible and tasty, though these 'shrooms were dried out and tough. Beware the look-alike Chlorophyllum molybdites mushroom with greenish spores! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

Even better, we have a bit of late-season rain coming in tonight. We've already had some drops from the approaching storm. I hope we get more than a few drops!

File.Name: 9501b_9425-068.jpg
Notes: 49823258 I can't produce a photo like yesterday's House Wren every day, but this is also pretty cool. It's a wild bird eggshell that I found in the woods last weekend. There's still a bit of yolk attached to the shell. I hope the baby emerged healthy and strong and wasn't eaten by the Blue Jays or other preditors! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2009)

File.Name: 9501c_9425-074.jpg
Notes:
49855062 Perfect newly-emerged Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly catching some morning sun to warm up on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

It's another butterfly that looks like it wants to be a museum specimen! What is that shiney spot (or two) on the thorax about where an insect pin would go?

File.Name: 9502a_9502-025.jpg
Notes: 49854510 This Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly is perched on the weedy Brome grasses in the canyon. These moth-like butterflies are so common I usually forget to photograph them, but they have their beauty, especially with the sun behind. Note the soccer ball eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

File.Name: 9502b_9502-028.jpg
Notes:
49854260 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly - the first I've been able to photograph this year. It's resting on a prickley leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family in the canyon. I can't tell the skipper butterflies apart - I won't even try. It was working the white flowers a few minutes ago. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

These are summer-time butterflies. Summer is almost here!

File.Name: 9502c_9502-036.jpg
Notes:
49890171 Another mating pair of Mugwort Weevils - at least that's what they should be called. They are tiny weevil beetles in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occur on the leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. They have strange elephant-like faces with the antennae down below the eyes. Here they are making babies - at least they're in the position to do so. Something seems to be missing! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

The winds never let up today, making photos impossible. Just as well. I'm in school slide show mode now. Along with these "Treebeard Photos", I also take Dunn Middle School photos. My annual DMS slide show extravaganza is due on May 28. Yipes!

File.Name: 9503a_9502-032.jpg
Notes: 49889077 This white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has caught breakfast while perched on some kind of Lavendar in the garden. It sure looks like an earwig, but I hope we don't have them at home! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

File.Name: 9503b_9502-050.jpg
Notes:
49888390 Caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly munching a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The white sunbust in the background is a Wild Blackberry flower. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

These caterpillars prefer plants in the Scrophulariaceae family, but how do they tell? The adult butterflies are starting to appear, see my photo at:

File.Name: 9504b_9502-029.jpg
Notes:
49925722 "Ouch - be gentle!"

This is a graphic shot of the *uhm* fang-like genitalia of the male Mugwort Weevil in mating position. At least that's what the beetles should be called. They are tiny weevil beetles in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occur on the leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. They've been going at it in the mugwort leaves for two weeks now! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

Insect genitalia are usually heavily "sclerotised" - hardened, like a beetle's shell - so they preserve well and are useful for taxonomy, especially at species level. There's even a "Phalloblaster" - aka a "vesica everter" - designed to "inflate the genitalia with a stream of pressurised alcohol to create the same shape as when the insect was alive".

All I can say is "Ouch - be gentle!

File.Name: 9504c_9418-028.jpg
Notes:
49960134 Elegant new wildflower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) growing on the dry side of our garden at home. It's a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. They are a "tender trap" - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

There's another wildfire in the Santa Barbara foothills tonight - the Jesusita Fire, named for the popular Jesusita trail to Inspiration Point. The fire is downwind from us - and what winds! - so I'm not too worried, but we have friends in the fire path and under evacuation orders ronight. I wish you well!

It's another dry year - only 50-60% of normal rainfall. *Sigh* another summer of worry!

The power is starting to flicker. I'll post one photo while I can!

File.Name: 9505a_9502-056.jpg
Notes:
49998081 I got home from school today and didn't want to go anywhere, but my son Ryan had to go to Santa Barbara and he got this photo of the Jesusita Fire burning this afternoon in the Santa Barbara foothills as the winds were starting to kick up. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2009)

You can see more of Ry's photos (including this one) at: http://www.flickr.com/photos/rkummel/.

It's a scary night with fire in the mountains. We live upwind of this fire, so I reckon we're good.

File.Name: 9506a_ryan-dcs026.jpg
Notes:
50027302 It seems silly to post a photo of a lizard when the mountains are on fire and homes are burning, but it's what I have. I "Twittered" a report that there was a new fire closer to home, but it turned out to be just a rumor. We're still ok.

This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on a sandstone rock in the garden before the fire started. It's a male showing its brilliant mating colors. A female was nearby, but I couldn't capture them together. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2009)

I'll bet a lot of these lizards have been roasted by the Jesusita Fire! The winds have died down tonight, so I hope things are better for the firefighters. I'm thinking of you Susan and Dick in Mission Canyon...

File.Name: 9507a_9502-057.jpg
Notes: 50062928 The Jesusita Fire flared up last night and turned towards our home on San Marcos Pass. We started packing and backing up hard-drives. This is what we saw when we finally went up for a look around 11 pm. That's my son Ry standing in the glow of the fire. This photo is natural light, using the car roof as my camera support. It's a 12 second exposure at f/3.5. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2009)

File.Name: 9508a_9507-125.jpg
Notes:
50062591 The Jesusita Fire flared up last night and turned towards our home on San Marcos Pass. This is how it looked in the morning - not good!

We live in a north-facing canyon and can't see anything from home, so I drove up East Camino Cielo road about 1/2 mile towards the Painted Cave community for a view. *Sigh* - a fire official came by and asked me politely but firmly to leave. It's been hard to get action shots of this fire that threatens us. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2009)

I thought the fire was just beyond the ridge line threatening Painted Cave and then us. But it's not as close as it looks.

File.Name: 9508b_9508-142.jpg
Notes:
50062198 The Jesusita Fire flared up last night and turned towards our home on San Marcos Pass. A crew of firefighters came by our house around noon and advised us to get out while we can. They call it "mandatory evacuation" - they can't make you leave, though they can keep you from going back once you do leave.

We packed our stuff in the truck that we had already piled up downstairs last night. Everyone should go through an evacuation order just to decide what's really important! It's really not so much. My guitar, fly fishing rod, a few boxes of papers and old photos, the lamp that grammy made - you know, the stuff that really can't be replaced. Terabyte-size portable hard drives make the computer and photo stuff a lot easier! The big painting didn't make the final cut. We ended up with a modest truck load. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2009)

The truck is still parked outside. We haven't actually gone anywhere and don't intend to unless the fire really gets close. Fortunately the winds have finally died down tonight.

File.Name: 9508c_9508-179.jpg
Notes:
50094730 I feel like I'm under house arrest! I've been warned not to leave our canyon on San Marcos Pass, but we can't see any of the wildfire action from home. We're supposed to evacuate, and we're all ready - the truck is packed and pointing downhill - but we're staying put. The fire news is all good. I don't want to jinx it by being too confident... The skies are clear and beautiful. I'm too jittery to work on the school slideshow, so I guess I'll take some photos! The skies are clear and beautiful. I'm too jittery to work on the school slideshow, so I guess I'll take some photos!

I can't see anything from home, but the fire news is all good. I don't want to jinx it by being too confident...

This is a Long-Horned Grasshopper (family Tettigoniidae, in the order Orthoptera) in the garden this morning, with perfect backlight. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2009)

File.Name: 9509a_9509-008.jpg
Notes:
50094356 There have been many different air tankers - aka fire bombers - dropping fire retardant on the Jesusita Fire that is threatening our home. Here is another one flying low over the native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge just above our house. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2009)

I'm essentially grounded by the all the fire activity on San Marcos Pass. I can't see any bit of the fire from home, but at least I can photograph the bombers that fly overhead!

This is airplane number 22 of the CDF fleet. I believe it is another Lockheed P-3a Orion aircraft.

I can't see any bit of the fire from home, but at least I can photograph the bombers! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2009)

This is airplane number 17 of the CDF fleet. I believe it is a Lockheed P-3a Orion aircraft.

File.Name: 9509c_9508-172.jpg
Notes:
50126997 Are these mating Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) in the garden today? Mentally check off the alternatives: it's not one jumping spider eating another, it's not a spider giving live birth - they must be mating! I had no idea that any spiders have this kind of intimate encounter to produce their eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2009)

I have another photo sequence of "spider love" at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19193969
and [Next] from there - legs akimbo, but not very *ahm* mammal-like like this.

It's another strange day grounded at home with no view of the action over the hill. The Jesusita Fire has been quiet today, I think, but I can't see anything from home in the canyon. We're evacuated and ready to leave at any moment. Life goes on. I've been working on the school slideshow today. I'll try to get to school tomorrow, but I'm worried I'll be cut off from home once I leave.

File.Name: 9510a_9510-125.jpg
Notes:
50162436 Here is another tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the garden yesterday. This one is munching a really tiny fly for breakfast. I love how these little spiders sit up and take notice of me, monster (in size) that I am! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2009)

The Jesusita Fire continues to lay low. I went to school today and drove home past the "Road Closed" signs on East Camino Cielo road. This might be another wildfire checked off under our belts!

File.Name: 9511a_9510-117.jpg

50194605 This critter is a little Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera. This one is perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. That tiny head contains enough of a brain to survive and mate! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2009)

The Jesusita fire continues to lay low despite renewed sundowner winds tonight. That's a really good sign. Despite this, we're still under "mandatory evacuation orders" along East Camino Cielo road. I've been driving to school and ignoring the "Road Closed" signs. I haven't unpacked the truck yet!

It's my Dunn Middle School slideshow time! As well as these "Treebeard Photos", I also take lots of photos of my school events. I'm working on the DMS slideshow - 1800+ photos in 26 segments - due next Thursday!

50425472 These gangly Crane Flies (family Tipulidae in the order Diptera) seem so clumsy when they get in our homes or in our hair, but they are graceful in their natural habit, hanging from the grass stalks or a spider web like this one. I guess its tiny smooth feet keep it from getting stuck in the web. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2009)

I didn't know they have such beautiful emerald eyes!

File.Name: 9519a_9516-016.jpg
Notes:
50461213 The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - are converging in the canyon again! These lady beetles usually converge in mid-winter, so I'm surprised to find them now at the beginning of summer - but they were also here last summer about the same time. Obviously the number of dots is not important for idenfying the species - but I reckon the pattern of white bars on the thorax is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2009)

I had the day off to work on the school slideshow, but of course I took a short walk in the canyon with my camera this morning. The slideshow is coming along...

I've been hitting the archives lately while I continue to work on the Dunn Middle School end-of-the-year slide show. It's a huge job. This year I have almost 2000 photos (mostly mine) arranged in 24 segments, with music for each segment - due Thursday night at 7:00 P.M.! It's coming along well.

Notes:
50699458 Beautiful dandelion seed head - but this is a native Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris heterophylla or A. grandiflora in the Asteraceae family). Fly away little seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2009)

The Dunn Middle School slideshow is done - at least I'm done! I work with my good friend Nick Vincent, an old-school 16 mm film guy. I sort the photos and put them in order, and then Nick and I turn them into beautiful sequences using Final Cut Pro on his Mac. Nick still has to "render" the beast - turn it into video - which will take a few hours of quiet crunching time on his computer, and then burn the final DVD in realtime. We're good for showtime tomorrow night!

We try to hold the slide show under an hour to get the best DVD quality - and darned if it didn't come in at 59 minutes and 46.2 seconds. That's based on my pre-sort of 1,942 slides. Damn, we're good!

The funny part is that I haven't seen any of it - we work in short bits, frame by frame. I've only seen a few seconds at a time, mostly speeded up. It will be a new show for me too tomorrow night!

File.Name: 9527a_9516-027.jpg

50759478 Handsome Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera. My Dunn Middle School slide show happened tonight, and it went very well. They "surprised" me with a slideshow tribute about my 26 years at the school. (I'm semi-retiring this year.) It was touching and amazing to see all the old photos. I'll talk with my director/friend Nick Vincent to see if we can get it on YouTube.

This is my first new photo in almost two weeks, but there will be many more to come. We had the Dunn Middle School graduation today, including Linda's touching tribute to me. I'm semi-retiring after 26 years at the middle school, but I'll continue taking school photos and doing afternoon elective classes - the fun stuff! And I'll have more time for these "Treebeard Photos".

File.Name: 9602b_9602-079.jpg
Notes:
50893969 "Osmeterium goes down..." (as Homer says; see my [Previous] photo!)

This is the same fat caterpillar as my [Previous] photo of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family.

I took this photo a few seconds after my [Previous] photo. The "osmeterium" disappears that fast! You can see where it went. I read that all swallowtail caterpillars have these structures. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2009)

The fennel is introduced, and it's a bad weed in the mountains. These native butterflies also use other native Apiaceae) species like like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia, but they seem to prefer the invasive fennel. We try to control the weeds in our canyon, but we always leave a few fennel plants for the caterpillars, and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia

File.Name: 9602c_9602-078.jpg
Notes:
50893164 "Osmeterium goes up..." (as Homer says; see my [Next] photo!)

Fat caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family growing in the canyon.

This caterpillar is showing its "Osmeterium" - the orange "thingie" at the top of it's head! It looks like a gummy bear kind of thing. It disappeared after a few seconds. The first time I saw this I almost jumped out of my skin - it appears that fast! It's said to smell bad, but I've never noticed. You can coax the caterpillar to show its "thingie" by pushing down on it's head with a grass stem, but this photo is unrehearsed. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2009)

File.Name: 9604a_9604-155.jpg
Notes:
50956715 We uncovered this beautiful Ensatina salamander (Ensatina eschscholtzii ssp. eschscholtzii in our woodpile today. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2009)

It was dirty after being disturbed, so we put it in a glass of water for a bath and a chance to rehydrate. It seemed to dig the water, sitting perched on the glass for some time. It's amazing that these salamanders can survive our dry California summer.

It's a "lungless salamander" that "breathes" through pores in its skin. It's important not to handle them too much.

The is the classic "ring species". Different subspecies of ensatina salamanders live in "the mountains surrounding the Californian Central Valley. The complex forms a horseshoe shape around the mountains, and though interbreeding can happen between each of the 19 populations around the horseshoe, the [i]Ensatina eschscholtzii[/i] subspecies on the western end of the horseshoe cannot interbreed with the [i]Ensatina klauberi[/i] on the eastern end. As such it is thought to be an example of incipient speciation." (Says Wikipedia.)

File.Name: 9604b_9604-158.jpg
Notes:
50956379 This is a native Ensatina salamander (Ensatina eschscholtzii ssp. eschscholtzii that we uncovered in our woodpile today. I believe it is ssp. eschscholtzii because its tail is a bit constricted and it clearly has 5 toes on its hindlegs. It was dirty after being disturbed, so we put it in a glass of water for a bath and a chance to rehydrate. It seemed to dig the water, sitting perched on the glass for some time. It's amazing that these salamanders can survive our dry California summer. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2009)

See my [Next] photo for more info.

File.Name: 9604c_9603-138.jpg
Notes:
50955352 I believe these are the mud nests of a solitary Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (genus Eumeninae, family Vespidea, order Hymenoptera) lining a branch in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2009)

Wikipedia says that "the adult wasp typically collects beetle larvae, spiders or caterpillars and, paralyzing them, places them in the cell to serve as food for a single wasp larva. As a normal rule, the adult wasp lays a single egg in the empty cell before provisioning it." I don't think I'd recognize this wasp if it stung me.

File.Name: 9606a_9605-167.jpg
Notes:
51044993 In with the food... (See my [Previous] photo.)

The little House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) on the front porch are hatching a second brood this year. They work tirelessly to bring food to the babies inside. It appears to be a caterpillar this time.

I got this photo on Thursday during our unexpected rain. It was terrible photo conditions - the bird was in dark shadow with a bright background. There was nothing to do except crank up the ISO and shoot with wide aperture and a slow shutter speed - but it's a sweet moment.

51044796 ... and out with the poop! (See my [Next] photo.)

The little House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) on the front porch are hatching a second brood this year. They work tirelessly to bring food - caterpillars and crickets and such - to the babies, and every few trips they fly off with one of these neat little "poop sacks". Beats changing diapers! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2009)

51044475 The handoff...

A hard working little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird passing a caterpillar to its mate in the nest box on our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2009)

I got this photo on Thursday during our unexpected rain. It was terrible photo conditions - the bird was in dark shadow with a bright background. There was nothing to do except crank up the ISO and shoot with wide apertureand a slow shutter speed - but it's a sweet moment.

File.Name: 9607a_9607-064.jpg
Notes:
51064949 Furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the air working new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains. I got this photo looking straight down on this cute fly. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

I drove up East Camino Cielo road to the "castle" today for the first time since the Jesusita fire. I was shocked by the "heavy hands" of the hot shot crews while fighting the fire. Several of my favorite photo places have been destroyed needlessly.

I suppose I can't complain - they were making fire lines to save my house! - but it's disturbing that the work was done by fire crews from somewhere else, with no thought for the local environment. There are local weeds that I call "forest service weeds" - like Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) - because they always follow Forest Service work.
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Trask's Yerba Santa; Waterleaf; Yellow Star Thistle
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Centaurea solstitialis

51102701 I captured this little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) near the river today. It's so blue that it looks black! It's pissed because I set my tripod up on its favorite rock. It kept flying away, but it kept coming back to the same place. It was just a matter of waiting... (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 June 2009)

The "June Gloom" is extending into the mountains this year. It's been cloudy all week. I'm ready for some sun! (But not tomorrow - I have to clean out my classroom at school - 26 years accumulation of stuff!)

File.Name: 9608b_9607-036.jpg
Notes:
51101949 Interesting ant-like "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) working the flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family in our mountains. It has an interesting "7th wheel" - an extra leg - on its head to guide its slender proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

I should be able to figure this out, but give me a day. Hmm - long 3-segmented beak, 4-segmented antennae, large pronotum, no ocelli... I'll figure this critter out and report back!

File.Name: 9608c_9607-074.jpg
Notes: 51101113 This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is perched on a flower cluster of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains. It's set like a mouse trap! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

With the long-exterted stamens and pistil, the flower seems to be expecting a much larger insect! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

I spent the day clearing out my classroom at school. I'm semi-retiring this year - no longer teaching science in the mornings, but I will still teach elective classes in the afternoons. It was pretty emotional dumping a 26 year accumulation of "stuff" - mostly junk, but good junk with much history!

"Doc" Macomber - who retired a few years before me as the high school chemistry teacher - came by in the middle of it all and said "It looks like a career."

*Sigh* I'll be done with it tomorrow.

File.Name: 9609b_9607-017.jpg
Notes:
51133861 Brilliant new summer flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delpinum cardinale) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family captured on a foggy day. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

These remarkable summer flowers are not common in our mountains. I only know a few places where they grow. You have to appreciate that our summers are dry in the Santa Barbara mountains. Most of the wildflowers are already done. That makes the summer flowers very interesting and special!

File.Name: 9609c_9607-070.jpg

The open flowers look like solemn hooded priests in robes with arms raised to celebrate life! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2009)

This is a fire-follower plant growing in a place in the mountains that didn't burn, but it was cleared for a fire break during the fire madness last year. For some of these plants, new sunlight on the soil is enough to trigger growth.

File.Name: 9612b_9611-144.jpg
Notes:
51229316 There was a rave of these coin-sized Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe Largus californicus?) - going on at the base of an interesting plant in the woods yesterday. For some reason known only to them, more than a dozen of these bugs were congregating at the base of this particular plant. Most of them quickly scattered as I placed my tripod, but I managed to capture a few that were entangled with mating. I came back again later, and they were still going at it. (Maybe they didn't know which way to run? ;=) (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2009)

I believe the interesting plant is Clustered Broomrape (Orobanche fasiculata> in the Broom-rape (Orobanchaceae) family. It has no chlorophyll - it grows as an obligate root parasite and has a fungal connection with another flowering plant nearby - probably native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family in this case. Ain't nature grand!

I guess this is the pupal stage of the same Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that I've been showing in [Previous] photos. You can see the silk webbing on the twig. There's magic happening inside! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2009)

I'm pretty sure the head of the butterfly is down below - but is that the head of the caterpillar gripping the stem above?? I think I see eyes on the stump.

The unseasonably cool and gloomy June weather has slowed down the butterflies, as well as all our summer insects. File.Name: 9615b_9614-045.jpg
Notes:
51331762 I finally got a photo of the elusive Yucca Moth (family Prodoxidae in the order Lepidoptera) working a flower on a giant flower spike of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei in the Lily Liliaceae) family)! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2009)

Or did I? I think this might really be a Bogus Yucca Moth (genus Prodoxus in the same moth family) - and so are most of the photos that come up on Google Images. For one thing, Wayne Armstrong says that our real yucca moths are dark:
   http://waynesword.palomar.edu/ww0902c.htm

Here's one account I found on the web of yucca moths (at http://amos.indiana.edu/library/scripts/yucca.html):

"Female yucca moths have a few days to deposit approximately one hundred eggs. So they fly around, scattering their eggs onto various yucca flowers. A female typically injects about three to five eggs into one yucca flower's ovary. In the process, she benefits the flower by pollinating it.

"Here's where this relationship gets more complicated though. When the moth's larvae hatch they feed on about twenty of the yucca flower's three hundred seeds. However, if too many larvae hatch inside one flower, the plant will abort the flower and the larvae will die. Therefore, it's to the advantage of the moths to be sure the flowers they deposit their eggs in do not become overloaded with eggs. Thus, the moths have developed communication through scent.

"When a female moth lands onto a flower she uses her antennae to inspect the flower for the scents of previous female visitors. If one or more moths have already deposited eggs in that flower this visitor will either reduce the number of eggs she lays or move onto another flower. When she does decide to lay her eggs onto a particular flower she drags her abdomen about the surface of the flower in order to leave her own scent as a warning to future visitors."

Bogus Yucca Moths also hang out in yucca flowers, but they are not involved in pollination. Kind of like freeloaders...

Ah, but which brodiaea is it? The Clifton Smith "Flora of the Santa Barbara Region" (my bible) calls it Brodiaea jolonensis, but its probably Brodiaea terrestris ssp. kernensis instead. See the interesting webpages by Wayne Armstong, starting with:

   (Follow the links.)

It's almost too easy to get good photos of these butterflies. There are dozens of them in the garden right now, and they are relatively docile. What's missing this year - after a gloomy June - is diversity of butterflies and all insects. Hopefully the new warm weather will change that.

File.Name: 9628a_9628-034.jpg
Notes:
51778621 Push mama push!

Yep, it's another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. But look near the bottom of the photo below the head and antennae - this butterfly is ovipositing, laying her precious golden eggs! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2009)

The host plant is native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. These butterflies feed on many plants, but they only trust their eggs to the scrophs.

The eggs will soon hatch and form little caterpillars that spin creepy webs on the host plants, like tent caterpillars. The caterpillars then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. They are undoubtedly our most common butterfly right now.
Locality: Scrophulariaceae) family. These butterflies feed on many plants

File.Name: 9629b_9629-043.jpg
Notes:
51824348 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) - on the right in the photo - is working flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family growing on the dry side of our garden. We rescued this shrub from a washout in the road years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2009)

These bees have to work for their reward! The lower lip of the flower stands up, and the bee has to bend it down with the weight of its body to get to the sweet nectar. After a few presses, the flowers hang open and different bees can get inside.

It seems that this flower is adapted for a much larger bee!

These slow-moving bugs are so tiny they look like bits of detritus on the leaves under the oaks, but they are alive and effective preditors. Maybe you can see its sucking proboscis folded under its head.

Since kauka_93 asked yesterday, let me just say that I take all the photos on this flog. By choice and providence, I live in a rich natural area. I get out with my camera every day that I can to document the life in the woods - mostly in our one canyon on San Marcos Pass in the Santa Barbara mountains. Like I said, it's a rich natural area!

The flowers seem built for much larger insects, but I usually see these little skippers visiting them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2009)

File.Name: 9630c_9628-019.jpg
Notes:
51855568 If to were drive Highway 154 over San Marcos Pass in summer, the last thing in the world you would think of is wild orchids - but here they are.

Here are flowers of the uncommon native Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family. Not to brag, but they are growing on the dry side of our garden, about 15 feet from our front door. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2009)

You can see that the pedicel - the stem of the flower - is rotated 180 degrees in the process known as resupination. These flowers also have a long nectar spur. They are beautiful and elegant up close like this, but from a distance they look more like asparagus stalks than anything exotic!

File.Name: 9701a_9701-120.jpg
Notes:
51894766 The cold ice-pick in the neck...

This is an unfortunate Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera) that has fallen prey to an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. I guess it thought the new white flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family were safe. It was wrong. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

When you see an insect hanging at a weird angle from any plant, always investigate!

I went exploring up East Camino Cielo road along the summits of our mountains today.

File.Name: 9701b_9701-126.jpg
Notes:
51893640 Uncommon male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

It almost looks like a White (Pierinae) butterfly, but the hooked antennae and the fact that it perches with wings open give it away as a skipper.

Coyote Mint is a beautiful flower that comes at a crucial time of the year, given our dry summers - it's the summer food plant for many butterflies.

It's an interesting stumper why Spring flowers flower later in the high mountains, but Summer flowers flower sooner!
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint; White
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Pierinae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Pierinae

File.Name: 9701c_9701-139.jpg
Notes:
51892773 Beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly resting on a twig of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae family. Ribes species of gooseberries and currants are its host plant. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

I went exploring up East Camino Cielo road along the summits of our mountains today.
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Canyon Gooseberry

File.Name: 9702a_9701-151.jpg
Notes:
51945037 Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the flowers of Buck Lotus (Lotus crassifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. This interesting perennial only grows along the summits of the mountains. It has characteristic flat sprays of leaves. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

Look close at the butterfly and note the pollen on its hindwing. This butterfly has been snuggling with native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers!
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Buck Lotus; Pea; Humboldt Lily
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum

File.Name: 9702b_9701-130.jpg
Notes: 51944610 Furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) hovering in the air while working new summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 9702c_9701-102.jpg
Notes:
51944432 Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2009)

We have two dark "spread-wing skippers" (subfamily Pyrginae) with white trim on the trailing edge of the hindwing. The Mournful Duskywing is mostly brown and the less common Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) is charcoal black.
CommonNames: Mournful Duskywing; Canyon Sunflower; Funereal Duskywing; Erynnis funeralis
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Pyrginae
associatedTaxa: Erynnis funeralis; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Pyrginae

File.Name: 9703a_9703-171.jpg
Notes:
51958863 Remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a native flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum). The genera Sphecomyia and Spilomyia are both strong wasp mimics, but neither has such a long abdomen. I'm not sure what it is - but what color! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2009)

Heh - that's a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the background of the photo. They're everywhere right now!
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; California Thistle; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Sphecomyia; Spilomyia; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Sphecomyia; Spilomyia; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: 9703b_9703-173.jpg
Notes:
51957887 Another California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly visiting new summer flowers of the native succulent Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2009)

This butterfly is a male, but it's hiding its glory behind closed wings. You can see a hint of the iridescent "dog face" on the upper wings showing through with the backlight. I'll keep trying to get a photo.

File.Name: 9703c_9703-160.jpg
Notes:
51957422 Another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly - this one is working new flowers of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden this morning. It's a healing plant. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2009)

If you look close you'll notice this butterfly is missing one of its tails. It's an occupational hazard for these large butterflies as they dodge birds.

File.Name: 9704a_9701-136.jpg
Notes:
51998027 Red oh-so-red flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delpinum cardinale) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) plant family growing near Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road. I didn't so any saturation enhancement on this photo - nothing helped. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

Today I've posted Red, White, and Blue flowers for our U.S. Independence Day. It's a good day to think about our revolutionary heritage...

File.Name: 9704b_9704-023.jpg
Notes:
51997383 White flowers of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family - they hold a surprise for unsuspecting pollinators!

This is a fierce-looking Ambush Bug in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera waiting for prey. It lives up to its name. This one has unusual greenish colors. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2009)

File.Name: 9704c_9611-154.jpg
Notes:
51996584 Blue summer flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing on dry ground along the road at Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2009)

It's a "penstemon" - you can see the infertile fifth stamen laying along the bottom of the flower as a "staminode". The pistle is visible in the lower-right flower on top.

It's strange - the flowers LOOK blue, but they come out magenta in photos. Is it something to do with UV?

File.Name: 9705a_9705-039.jpg
Notes:
52036233 Ted Adams standing by a remarkable lime kiln that dates back to maybe 1905 on San Marcos Pass in the Painted Cave community near the pump house. It's all dry-fit sandstone blocks - a pretty good trick to make a sphere out of blocks as any lego fan will tell you! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2009)

If you heat limestone hot enough, you get concrete. They apparently mined the limestone from the travertine build-up by old springs. Rumor is that they used this cement to build the original road to Carpenteria. It seems wrong to bring cement DOWN from the mountains, but maybe that's the way it was until the railroad arrived.

Painted Cave is a few miles east of us on San Marcos Pass. It's been a mountain community for many years. We did a local history hike with the San Marcos Pass Mountain Goats (my name) in the Painted Cave area, down by the pump house. I feel humble - I'd never seen this before.
Locality: my name) in the Painted Cave area

File.Name: 9705b_9705-067.jpg
Notes:
52035123 I guess anarchists have always lived in our mountains! Graffiti used to take more time when they were literally carved in stone. This one along the original Painted Cave road says:

"Free roads
   Free air
   And -
   some day
   Free land"

I'm sure it dates back to the turn of the last century - ca. 1900 beet- check the lichens. Why would someone take the effort to carve this message in stone? And with real estate prices today, it's a hope lost.
Locality:

File.Name: 9705c_9705-071.jpg
Notes:
52033843 Ted Adams posing by a mossy rock at the Painted Cave community with an elegantly carved inscription: "May 22/97" - that's 1897! Below that (if you can see it) is carved the names "L and S Lewis" which seems to be added later. There are Lewis Falls across the mountains below Knapp's Castle. I reckon the main inscription commemorates finishing the road. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2009)

Painted Cave is a few miles east of us on San Marcos Pass. It's been a mountain community for many years. We did a local history hike with the San Marcos Pass Mountain Goats (my name) in the Painted Cave area, down by the pump house. I feel humble - I'd never seen this before.
Locality: my name) in the Painted Cave area

File.Name: 9706a_9706-107.jpg
Notes:
52075187 There's a lot happening in this photo - look close!

The main attraction is a pair of coin-sized Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe Largus californicus?) - going at it in the flowers of a Toyon aka California Holly shrub (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) plant family.

There's also a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the lower left - obviously disturbed by the bugs!

Finally, that Bordered Plant Bug in the foreground is wearing it's destiny. Look close - it has two white eggs on it's forehead, probably the parasites that will become it's fate. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2009)
CommonNames: Bordered Plant Bugs; Toyon; California Holly; Rose; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Largidae; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: 9706b_9706-092.jpg
Notes:
52074230 This odd Beetle (order Coleoptera) seems to have a proboscis like Hemiptera bugs! It spent all day working a flower head of an Onion (Allium cepa) flower cluster in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2009)

That "thing" can't be a proboscis. It must be a "tongue" or "ligula" of some sort. I don't know what it is!

File.Name: 9706c_9706-102.jpg
Notes: 52073204 Pretty little Beetle (order Coleoptera) munching on pollen from a Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) plant family. I guess it's a Long-horned Beetle (family Cerambycidae). (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2009)
CommonNames: Beetle; Toyon; California Holly; Rose; Long-horned Beetle

File.Name: 9707a_9707-117.jpg
Notes:
52118133 Exhibit A shows a beautiful immature Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's tiny - only a few mm across. It's related to the infamous vinchuca "kissing bugs", but I'm pretty sure it's prey are other insects, not humans. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2009)

What's remarkable is that it's an almost perfect mimic of the immature Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) that I show two photos [Back] in exhibit C. Both have spider-like legs and long black-and-white speckled antennae. But the close shot in exhibit B in my [Previous] photo shows that it's something else!

I admit this critter fooled me. At first I thought "what a tiny katydid nymph" and didn't realize I had something else until I got it on my computer screen.

Heh - it's also got a neat pair of faux "eye-spots" on the tip of its abdomen!

File.Name: 9707b_9707-115.jpg
Notes:
52117036 Exhibit B shows the mouthpart of an insect that is anything but a cow-like herbivore!

This is a very tight crop of a different photo of the Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera in my [Next] photo. It has an armor-tipped spike of a proboscis for subduing its prey and sucking its juices. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2009)

File.Name: 9707c_9704-017.jpg
Notes:
52116438 Exhibit C is a beautiful nymph of a Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a leaf in the woods. The nymphs have these amazing spider colors before they become large dull-green cattle-like insects that munch leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2009)

I have many photos of the adults, for example here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/41312235

File.Name: 9708a_9706-110.jpg
Notes:
52163673 This is a much larger coin-sized member of the Assassin Bug clan in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's probably a Bee Assassin (Apiomerus crassipes or maybe A. spissipes) or kin, but I didn't get a good look at it. You can see it's mighty proboscis. This assassin in hiding on a branch of Toyon aka California Holly shrub (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2009)

All my photos today are Assassin Bugs, but they look very different!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Bee Assassin; Toyon; California Holly; Rose
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus crassipes; A. spissipes; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus crassipes; A. spissipes; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: 9708b_9708-148.jpg
Notes:
52163327 A different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. This one is also tiny, only a few mm long. It's a well-named Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) perched on a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) plant family. We try to control this weed in our canyon, but we always keep a few for the swallowtail butterflies. You can clearly see its dagger-like proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2009)

It's a challenge not to over-expose photos of these tiny insects. I barely made it with this one!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Thread-legged Bug; Fennel; Parsley
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Emesinae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Emesinae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: 9708c_9708-140.jpg
Notes:
52162989 Here is another photo of the remarkable spider-like immature Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's tiny - only a few mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2009)

I went back to the same Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf this morning, and there it was! I guess it doesn't travel very far.

See my [Previous] two photos for more info.

File.Name: 9709a_9709-175.jpg
Notes:
52199143 You can see right through the wings of this Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2009)

It's a challenge to photograph these diurnal (daytime) moths - they hover like little hummingbirds and constantly move from flower to flower.
