File.Name: 9709b_9709-181.jpg
Notes:
52198723 This is an uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterfly working native flowers of its host plant - Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 July 2009)

Both butterfly and flower are uncommon. The plant is remarkable choosing to flower in midsummer, given our summer-dry conditions. And the butterfly is remarkable for choosing this plant as its host plant. It won't take much change for both to disappear.
CommonNames: Callippe Fritillary; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 9709c_9701-115.jpg
Notes:
52198111 I believe this is a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly working white flowers of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. It has "white frosting beyond the postmedian line on the hindwing" - as my butterfly book says. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2009)

We have several of these plain brown hairstreaks - including the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) and the Gold Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) - but I think I finally have this one right.
CommonNames: Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat; Hedgerow Hairstreak; Gold Hunter's Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium tetra; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum
associatedTaxa: Satyrium tetra; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum

File.Name: 9710a_9710-188.jpg
Notes:
52235749 Beautiful Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working the new flowers of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden today. A bird got a good part of its left hindwing, but missed the butterfly itself - as intended. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2009)

This butterfly looked so brilliant that I thought it might be something exotic, but I think it checks out as a Western Tiger Swallowtail.

File.Name: 9710b_9706-098.jpg
Notes: 52235503 Beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a Dahlia flower in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2009)

File.Name: 9710c_9710-200.jpg
Notes: 52235286 Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly perched on a flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden today as the sun gets low. I haven't seen these pretty butterflies for quite awhile. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2009)

File.Name: 9711a_9710-197.jpg
Notes:
52272243 "Beautiful fly" seems like an oxymoron, but in this case it's true!

This beautiful fly is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) despite the absence of bristles. Studying the pictures at bugguide.net, I'm pretty sure this critter is genus Gymnosoma. The adult flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2009)

These flies with the big red eyes are active on a horticultural Goldenrod (Solidago) in the garden.

File.Name: 9711b_9711-011.jpg
Notes: 52271548 This Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) is so blue that it's almost black. This is the working end of the damselfly - its body goes on for a long ways to the right. What amazing eyes, and what a remarkable "rotor system" it has - like a helicopter - for controlling its wings. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2009)

File.Name: 9711c_9711-007.jpg
Notes:
52271141 Even nasty preditors need love!

Here are a pair of Ambush Bugs in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera exchanging genetic material to insure that there will be lots of little ambush bugs in the future. These are probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). Their nuptial bed is a cluster of flowers of the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2009)

You can see the crab-like front legs they use to hold their prey - often much larger than they are. I have a recent photo of an ambush bug in action:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/51894766
CommonNames: Ambush Bugs; Pacific Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 9712a_9712-035.jpg
Notes:
52307352 One minute you're flying free as a butterfly - see my photo from a few days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52199143
   - and the next thing you know, you're hanging like a side of beef in a butcher shop!

This unfortunate critter is a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) that has become prey for a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae). The spider is perched in a native flower head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. It's been a productive perch. I watched it snag two Variable Checkerspot butterflies a few days ago in just over an hour. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2009)

I got this photo at some risk - I was perched on a narrow rock ledge with my tripod in the shade about 6 feet from the ground. I couldn't fuss too much with focus and exposure!

File.Name: 9712b_9712-038.jpg
Notes: 52306378 The Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies are finally getting scarce, and here's one reason why. This Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) has just captured a checkerspot. It seemed to be beginning to remove it's wings, when I made a clumsy move with my tripod and she scurried back to her funnel-like nest. I waited and waited, but one thing you cannot do is to out-wait a spider! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2009)

File.Name: 9712c_9709-154.jpg
Notes:
52305892 This is a really tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a single flower of a cultivated Onion (Allium cepa) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing in the garden. Of course the onion has a flower head with hundreds of flowers - this is just one of them. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2009)

Tiny as it is, this little critter is camera shy! It took me several days of quiet stalking and tripod placing to get this photo without scaring it off!

File.Name: 9713a_9712-016.jpg
Notes:
52348156 Remarkable summer wildflower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily".

It's remarkable looking - but it's also remarkable because it picks the middle of our bone-dry summer drought to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2009)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium

File.Name: 9713b_9704-013.jpg
Notes:
52347683 A perfect flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the canyon. These flowers are always a surprise in the mountains, like a celebrity at a campground music jam. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2009)

I've been trying to get a photo of the swallowtail butterflies on the flowers. I'll keep trying.

File.Name: 9713c_9713-067.jpg
Notes:
52347338 One more photo on the hazards of being a butterfly.

This is an unfortunate Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly hanging like a strange fruit from a flower cluster of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the woods today. The predator that brought it down is in plain view - but it's hard to see. Can you see it now? (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2009)

It's an Ambush Bug in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera. If you still can't see it, look right above the thorax of the butterfly, where the legs are attached. I have another recent photo here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52271141
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 9714a_9714-080.jpg
Notes:
52389250 I guess this is an uncommon native Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family. It's unusually robust because it's growing in a place that burned last summer in the Gap Fire - ashes make good fertilizer.

It's also unusual because the "lip" of the flower is so long. I considered P. michaelii and even P. leptopetala (which is unknown from Santa Barbara County) - but I think it's just a robust form of P. elongata.

It's beautiful - a real orchid in our summer-dry woods! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2009)
ScientificName: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; P. michaelii and even P. leptopetala; P. elongata; real orchid
associatedTaxa: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; P. michaelii and even P. leptopetala; P. elongata; real orchid

File.Name: 9714b_9714-073.jpg
Notes:
52388600 My photos are usually framed so close that you can't really tell what a plant looks like or where it growing. Mea culpa, it's got to do with the 500 pixel limit on Fotolog photos.

Here's a wider shot of an unusually robust plant of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2009)

This is the same plant I've been featuring close-up in my [Previous] photos. I reckon it's so robust because it's growing in a place that burned last summer in the Gap Fire - ashes make good fertilizer. You can see many flowers, and also some seed pods.
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium

File.Name: 9714c_9712-023.jpg
Notes:
52388143 Here's another photo of the remarkable summer wildflower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense.

It's remarkable looking - and it's also remarkable because it's a lily that picks the middle of our bone-dry summer drought to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2009)

Yesterday's flower was from East Camino Cielo, but today's photos are from West Camino Cielo - by the first place where you can see the burn. (Get there soon Lynn - they won't last!)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium

File.Name: 9715a_9715-096.jpg
Notes:
52431589 Tattered Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of native Dunn`s Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) - now included in the Bellflower (Campanulaceae) family.

The plant is uncommon, but only because it's habitat is uncommon - there aren't many flowing streams in midsummer in this drought year. I got this photo where Lewis Creek enters (and becomes, for a bit) the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2009)

File.Name: 9715b_9715-122.jpg
Notes:
52431017 "You get a line and I'll get a pole,
   We're all goin' down to the crawdad hole..."

Crayfish, crawfish, or crawdads - call them what you like - are fresh water decapod crustaceans like little lobsters. Tasty too! I went to the river today and noticed this critter as I was wading in one of the few places that still has water in this drought year. It seemed to have a nest, which it promptly backed into. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2009)

It has remarkable eyes, visible near the bottom of the photo. There is a glimmer of recognition and intelligence there!

File.Name: 9715c_9715-130.jpg
Notes:
52430359 Another photo in my series on the the hazards of being a butterfly...

This is actually a moth - a diurnal or daytime Clearwinged Moth (family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae in the order Lepidoptera) that has fallen prey to a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). It's "hanging out" in a horticultural Goldenrod (Solidago) in the garden that is acting like a natural light tent, turning everything yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2009)

File.Name: 9716a_9716-183.jpg
Notes:
52470097 We had an unexpected visitor to the garden this morning!

This is a White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth working new flowers of the Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden this morning. I think of these as nocturnal or twilight moths, but this one stayed active until after 11 am. I was glad it made an exception! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2009)

They beat their wings so fast, but I believe they vibrate their wings rather than flapping them, like little helicopters. That's why their wings appear transparent, even though they are fully scaled. They seem very much like little hummingbirds, and our hummers were actively chasing it off. Like the hummers themselves, it kept coming back as soon as their attention was elsewhere.

File.Name: 9716b_9716-157.jpg
Notes:
52469715 This is another photo of the White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth that lingered in the garden this morning after its bedtime. The only time it was ever at rest was when it was snuggling into this non-native Gladiolus flower (Iridaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2009)

It's a perfect pollenation photo. You can see the moth making contact with the flower's pistil, and yes, its proboscis really does reach down to the nectary at the base of the flower. Look how its antennae lay back, cat-like!

File.Name: 9717a_9715-134.jpg
Notes:
52504648 You can tell it's time for the kids to move out when...

...they almost bite your head off when you go to feed them!

This parent House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) is bringing food to its almost fledged young - for almost the last time. I got this photo late in the day on Wednesday, and on Thursday (yesterday) they were gone from the nest. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2009)

Human parents will sympathize!
   (I've been there!)

This is the third brood that these wrens have raised this year. They are the only one of the three nesting pairs of birds around home that raised three broods. It's interesting that they didn't sing for these babies. I guess the hormones stopped pumping, but they still knew what to do.
Locality:

File.Name: 9717b_9715-143.jpg
Notes:
52503940 Parent House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bringing a grasshopper to its almost fledged young. I got this photo late in the day on Wednesday, and on Thursday (yesterday) they were gone from the nest. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2009)

Human parents, take warning - suddenly your kids are gone! (But if we're lucky they might bring us a grasshopper once in a while!)

File.Name: 9717c_9716-199.jpg
Notes: 52503381 It was funny - as the hummingbird-like White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth worked the Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) flowers in the garden yesterday - see my [Previous] photos - the hummingbirds grew agitated and protective, and then they started working the flowers also! This is probably a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna). (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2009)

File.Name: 9718a_9718-019.jpg
Notes:
52541300 "Slouching Towards Bethlehem..."

This is a photo of my favorite summer predator - the well-named Ambush Bug in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera, probably a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It just landed on a flower cluster of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. It hasn't had time to hide in ambush position yet, so I got to capture its full armored body. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2009)

These ambush bugs seem to be color shifters, but they have a limited palette of color - white to green to yellow, but no blue.

I went back to my favorite clump of Coyote Mint today. This is on the high divide between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. The Coyote Mint in our canyon are still not flowering yet.

File.Name: 9718b_9718-004.jpg
Notes:
52540548 You can see right through the transparent wings of this Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2009)

It's a challenge to photograph these diurnal (daytime) moths - they hover like little hummingbirds and constantly move from flower to flower. This photo gives the wrong impression - usually the wings appear to be in constant motion! I believe these Hawkmoths actually vibrate their wings instead of flapping them - like little helicopters.
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 9718c_9712-044.jpg
Notes:
52540209 Remarkable green body of a Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterfly working native flowers of what I guess is its host plant - Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2009)

These butterflies are never common, and they run to subspecies. I guess this is an Unsilvered Macaria aka a Laurina Fritillary (Speyeria callippe ssp. macaria form "laurina"). There's nothing in my books about them having green bodies.

File.Name: 9719a_9719-035.jpg
Notes:
52576815 Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. I got this photo with backlight. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2009)

The pointy forewings are distinctive with this species. The males have amazing colors on their upper wings, but that's another story.

File.Name: 9719b_9719-041.jpg
Notes:
52576419 I guess this is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly - it has the iridescent eyespots of the species on its hind wings. It's working composite flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum). I reckon this is a female. You can see a piece of the upper wing near its tail, and it's brown rather than blue. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2009)

These butterflies have been scarce this year - either the drought or the unusually gloomy June may be the reason. Really it's remarkable - most insects only live one season. That means that they completely trust their genome to weather etc. every year! One bad year - or a year that's "different" - could literally wipe them out!

File.Name: 9719c_9718-034.jpg
Notes:
52575771 This odd little MothLepidoptera) has long antennae lying along it's back - they look at first like a shodow of something that's not there. It also has long labial palps raised antennae-like over it's head. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2009)

I'd love to learn the moths - there must be many interesting stories! Maybe in my next life...

File.Name: 9720a_9720-060.jpg
Notes:
52617265 This is a web on a Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing in the canyon - but it's not a spider web!

This is the work of web-spinning caterpillars of the beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that were so common in our mountains a few weeks ago. Look near the bottom of the photo - you can see the social caterpillars on their common web. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2009)

The butterflies feed on many plants, but they only trust their eggs to the scrophs. I've found the caterpillars on several Penstemons (including garden species), monkey flowers, bee plants, and Cordylanthus.

I have a photo of a Variable Checkerspot oviposting on the same kind of plant here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/51778621

The little caterpillars spin creepy webs on the host plants, like tent caterpillars. They usually only cover a branch or two, so they are not too destructive. The caterpillars then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them.

File.Name: 9720b_9718-023.jpg
Notes:
52616242 Like my [Previous] photo, this butterfly is another old friend that I haven't seen for about a year.

This is a Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2009)

I photographed this same butterfly on July 7, 2008 and July 10, 2006 and July 27, 2005 - all pretty close to today's date. It's reassuring that some of the butterflies are on schedule in this drought year!
CommonNames: Checkered White; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 9720c_9718-017.jpg
Notes:
52615843 This tattered yellow Colias Sulfur butterfly is similar to the female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) in my [Previous] photo, but it is subtly different. It doesn't have pointed wing tips, and you can see some dark trim on the wings.

This is a Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2009)

Sometimes history does repeat itself. I photographed this same butterfly on August 4, 2008 and July 31, 2005 - both pretty close to today's date.

File.Name: 9721a_9721-105.jpg
Notes:
52657144 Look out little fly!

Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) about to strike a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera)... but the fly got away. The flower is a horticultural Goldenrod (Solidago, Asteraceae) in the garden today. The spider seems perfectly placed, but she missed this Fly. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2009)

Once I found this crab spider, I sat and waited with camera pre-focused for over an hour for it to capture something. No luck. There were six near misses, but no strikes. Maybe it's harder being a crab spider than it seems.

File.Name: 9721b_9721-112.jpg
Notes:
52656734 Look out little bee!

Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) about to strike a little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera)... but the bee got away. The flower is a horticultural Goldenrod (Solidago, Asteraceae) in the garden today. The spider seems perfectly placed, but she missed this bee. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2009)

File.Name: 9721c_9721-078.jpg
Notes:
52656379 A little humpbacked Fly (order Diptera) working a new summer flower of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. stenophyllus) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. Look - it's blowing a bubble! It's tiny. The flower is about the size of a pencil erasor, and the fly is only about half that. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2009)

I was disappointed with this photo - cool as it is. The slightest breeze is enough to get these plant stems swinging. But there's always tomorrow...

If anyone has any idea what this fly might be, please let me know! It has distinctive behavior bobbing up and down before it lands on a flower.

File.Name: 9722a_9722-125.jpg
Notes:
52693086 Swallowtail Caterpillar - day 3 (Wednesday, today)

This is the caterpillar - now a chrysalis - of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2009)

It's an amazing transformation! After two days, the caterpillar has become a chrysalis - it seems a gigantic change from yesterday! If you look very close, you can see the outlines of antennae and wings on the pupal case. (Head up.)

Here's a stumper - what happened to the pretty patterned green skin of the caterpillar? Did it just become the hard shell of the pupa? I'll have the answer tomorrow!

File.Name: 9722b_9721-072.jpg
Notes:
52692452 Swallowtail Caterpillar - day 2 (Tuesday)

This is the caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2009)

Changes are happening as the caterpillar prepares to pupate. Look how he is now fully strapped in, suspended by the silken sling. The prolegs seem to have retracted. Its colors seem faded, though maybe that's an illusion.

File.Name: 9722c_9720-055.jpg
Notes:
52691987 Swallowtail Caterpillar - day 1 (Monday)

This is the caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family growing in the canyon. (It's a weed, but we always leave a few for the butterflies. Note the plant is stripped!) (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2009)

It's beginning to pupate. It has the typical comma-like shape. And look how the branch is wrapped with webbing. And you can see it positioning a sling with its third true leg - the sling that it will hang from.

How does it wrap the stem and get the sling around its fat body with those stubby legs!?

Note the tiny dark wasp - surely a Parasitic Wasp in the order Hymenoptera. I'll post a closer photo of it tomorrow.
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Fennel; Parsley; Parasitic Wasp
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 9723a_9720-056.jpg
Notes:
52731103 This is a closer crop of the caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly that I featured yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52691987

But it's not the same photo. I took this photo a minute or so after the previous one, and there's a difference in the little wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2009)

This photo shows the wasp with a bulge in it's abdomen that wasn't there in the first photo. (I'm biting my tongue to avoid bad taste jokes!) This is surely a Parasitic Wasp in the order Hymenoptera, though I don't know what kind.

The bulge must be some sort of ovipositor that the wasp will use to get her eggs under the skin of the caterpillar. I hope a butterfly will emerge from this chrysalis and not a mass of wasp parasitoids. Butterfly chrysalises seem so vulnerable.

You can also see a little white capsule that I guess is a fly egg - but I guess that problem is taken care of when the caterpillar sheds its skin to become a chrysalis. (See my [Previous] photo.)

You can finally see it positioning its sling with its third true leg - the sling that it will hang from. That's a pretty good trick with those stubby little legs. How does it wrap the sling around its body?

Unfortunately, I probably won't be here to see what happens. We're heading off to Oregon to visit grandkids and green mountain meadows early next week. I'll miss the emergence.

File.Name: 9723b_9723-133.jpg
Notes:
52729792 This is a close shot of that bit of extra skin that was visible at the base of the chrysalis of the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly in my [Previous] photo.

That original caterpillar has shed his skin like cast-off pajamas! Look close and you can see eyes, legs, and prolegs - the whole skin seems to be there in one piece! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2009)

I reckon this solves the stumper about what happens to the pretty patterned skin of the caterpillar - but it leaves a bigger stumper. The caterpillar was already hanging in its sling before it shed its skin. See my photo from yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52692452

So how the heck does it shed its skin while in the sling? (I'll bet mountain climbers have the same problem!)

File.Name: 9723c_9723-137.jpg

52729222 This is the chrysalis of the other Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. Two caterpillars became chrysalises on the same plant on the same day. This is the other one. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2009)

Now look close at the bottom of the photo and spot that little bit of extra skin. I'll have a close shot in my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 9724a_9722-123.jpg
Notes:
52769431 Pretty little butterfly with a somber name - this is a Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2009)

I love the name. You can see some of the metallic silver threads in the weave of its wings that is this butterfly's namesake.

This is the first one of its kind that I've seen since last July. Our late-season butterflies have arrived.

File.Name: 9724b_9723-142.jpg
Notes:
52769163 This Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly is working a Winter Savory (Satureja montana) flower in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family growing in the garden. Look close at its proboscis - I think it's a bull's eye! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2009)

I know, it's just another dull hairstreak - but this is the first one of its kind that I've seen since last September. Our late-season butterflies have arrived.

File.Name: 9724c_9719-037.jpg
Notes: 52768822 Beautiful female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) working flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2009)

File.Name: 9725a_9725-022.jpg
Notes:
52803217 This is another one of those WTF photos!

It shows another chrysalis of a Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - it's not one of of the ones I watched form, but it's in a neighboring plant of its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2009)

This chrysalis has been destroyed - either something large went in or came out that was not the butterfly.

It's strange, but this Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera kept trying to get into the pupal case and it finally succeeded. (Photo tomorrow.)

Maybe a bird got the chrysalis, and the wasp was looking for table scraps? I really don't know. I guess that's why I love taking natural history photos. After almost 40 years of taking photos in these woods, I still get to say "I don't know!

File.Name: 9727a_9727-079.jpg
Notes:
52872460 The holy grail of local butterfly photographers must surely be to capture the "dog face" of a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. This is close! Actually it looks more like the face of an ogre in my photo. The flower is a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

It's tough because the butterflies always close their wings when they land on a flower, and they seem to spring jet-like without warning into the air. Even my butterfly books fudge this with a museum specimen. I had to put my camera on "automatic burst mode" to get this photo.

It's a beautiful butterfly, with a brilliant iridescent violet sheen that is only revealed in the air when the butterfly takes wing. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why!

File.Name: 9727b_9727-060.jpg
Notes:
52871601 This critter looks like a wasp, but the anatomy is all wrong!

This is actually a moth - a diurnal or daytime Clearwinged Moth (family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae in the order Lepidoptera). It's perched on a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

I only got this glimpse of it before it took off for places unknown. So many of my photos are like that - remarkable critters, but I know nothing about how they live.

File.Name: 9727c_9727-066.jpg
Notes:
52870898 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

Now look closer! The honey bee has one hind leg stuck in the milkweed flower behind. Sometimes they actually have to pull a leg off to get free, but this one managed to free itself by dragging sideways.

Milkweed flowers (family Asclepiadaceae) set a trap for pollinator insects to carry their pollen. The bee's hind leg is stuck in the "horns" of the flower. It was strong enough to pull free, but it probably picked up more milkweed "pollinia" or pollen sacks by doing so. You can see some of those little black "berries" attached to its front legs. Milkweed flowers offer sweet nectar to pollinating insects, but they expect them to carry their pollen from plant to plant in exchange!

For more info, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8108247

File.Name: 9728a_9728-116.jpg
Notes:
52913635 Julie is bravely holding our largest beetle in her hands!

This is a Pine Sawyer aka a Spined Woodborer (Eragates spiculates) beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera. As you can see, it's about the size of Julie's thumb. It's our largest beetle! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2009)

My books say the larvae are grubs in coniferous logs. We live in an oak forest, but there are pines on the hills above us. I reckon this critter is a wanderer.

By some strange chance, I got my first photo of this beetle last year on July 29:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/38592872

Do these insects have a schedule??

File.Name: 9728b_9728-080.jpg
Notes:
52912881 A little golden caterpillar (Lepidoptera) is munching pollen in the heart of a composite flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2009)

It looks like a sea slug munching coral polyps in some tropical ocean!

File.Name: 9728c_9727-072.jpg
Notes:
52912484 Another photo showing the remarkable "dog face" of a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. It's tough because the butterflies always close their wings when they land on a flower, and they seem to spring jet-like without warning into the air. The flowers are dwarf Zinnia flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

I focused a bit above the flowers and put my camera on "automatic burst mode" to get this photo - 60 boring photos, followed by this one keeper. The butterflies take wing that fast!

Sure it's not in focus, but so what? Note that one side of the upper wings is yellow, but the other side is bluish. That's the beautiful iridescence of these butterflies - which gets them the name "flying pansies". (Heh, that common name isn't used much anymore - English speakers will know why!)

File.Name: 9729a_9729-155.jpg
Notes:
52953049 The hummingbirds have been going crazy at the feeder!

I won't try and guess which hummer is which. We have Black-chinned Hummingbirds (Archilochus alexandri) and Anna's Hummingbirds (Calypte anna) - and a single Allen's or Rufous (Selasphorus sasin) or (S. rufus) that is mostly getting shut out of the fray. There are at least 10 hummers fighting over the feeder! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2009)

I reckon we have every hummingbird in the canyon at our feeder right now - a sign of this drought year. The California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) are about to flower, and I hope that will reduce the load. As it is, I have to fill the feeder 3 or 4 times a day! (They come and tell me when it's empty!)
CommonNames: Black-chinned Hummingbirds; Anna's Hummingbirds; Allen's; Rufous; California Fuschia
ScientificName: Archilochus alexandri; Calypte anna; Selasphorus sasin; S. rufus; Epilobium canum

52952563 I believe this is a Sand Wasp (genus Bembix, family Sphecidae, order Hymenoptera) - at least it's in the subfamily Bembicinae. It's working a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. They have remarkable eyes and an impressive proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2009)

These wasps are aggressive and camera-shy. I could only capture it from a distance and do a tight crop.

File.Name: 9729c_9728-122.jpg
Notes:
52951997 This thumb-sized Pine Sawyer aka Spined Woodborer (Eragates spiculates) beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera is about to take flight from Julie's thumb! It was tired from too much handling. It only flew a few feet, and we helped it find a shady plant to rest on. It was gone this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2009)

As with all beetles, the front "wings" are just wing covers. The hind wings do all the work.

File.Name: 9730a_9730-177.jpg

Beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden today. It's got perfect aim. Look close and you'll see that it's feet have picked up some milkweed "pollinia" or pollen sacks! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2009)

This beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly is working the new summer flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2009)

This butterfly is not really gray - it's more of a "blue dun", a color which is important for tying trout flies for fishing. (It's on my mind - we're soon leaving for Oregon!)

File.Name: 9730c_9727-071.jpg
Notes:
Little hummingbird (female Anna's, Calypte anna?) working a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

Don't be fooled - there are "hummingbird flowers" that the hummers prefer, but they will stick their beaks into just about anything! They are curious little critters.

Notes:
53023702 Perfect male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly perched on a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. These butterflies aren't only beautiful on their upper wings! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2009)

We planned to leave for Oregon on Sunday, but we just got word of a wildfire that's moving towards my Dad's house along the North Umpqua River. We're leaving tomorrow morning.

No time for new photos, but here's some good ones from my "Good Stuff" folder.

I'll be back in a couple of weeks.

File.Name: 9824a_9805-088.jpg
Notes:
53863743 A peek at a pair of mating Pond Damselflies in the family Coenagrionidae of the order Odonata.

Damselfly mating is unusual. Males first deposit sperm in a special structure on the underside of the front end of their abdomen. The male then holds the female behind the head with claspers on the tip of his abdomen. The female then loops her abdomen forward and picks up the sperm from the male. It's a fascinating pose. Mating pairs can fly, but awkwardly.

In my photo, the male is above-right and the female is below-left. (Hemlock Lake, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2009)

File.Name: 9824b_9805-092.jpg
Notes:
53862771 I believe this dragonfly is an Eight-spotted Skimmer (Libellula forensis) in the Libellulidae family of the order Odonata. There were lots of these dragonflies along the shore of Hemlock Lake in the Oregon woods. (Hemlock Lake, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2009)

Even with lots of dragonflies, it was hard to get this photo. Dragonflies have amazing eyes that take in everything - including the approaching photographer. I'm sure a close-focusing long telephoto is the answer. I have one, but I never seem to have it when I need it.

File.Name: 9824c_9804-045.jpg
Notes: 53862240 I believe this dragonfly is a female Widow Skimmer (Libellula luctuosa) in the Libellulidae family of the order Odonata. I found it in a dry Oregon meadow during our vacation trip - there was no obvious water source nearby. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2009)

File.Name: 9825a-9820-352.jpg
Notes:
53897748 Not the best photo, but this was probably the most interesting insect I found on our trip to Oregon. It's not a bumble bee, but a fly! Go ahead - examine the head and count the wings.

I believe this critter is in the genus Laphria - the Bee-like Robber Flies - of the family of Robber Flies (Asilidae) in the order Diptera. Looking at BugGuide (http://bugguide.net/node/view/102927) it looks a lot like Laphria virginica, though the geography seems wrong. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 August 2009)

I don't know anything about this fly. This specimen didn't even try to fly when I got close, but it managed to crawl away to parts unknown.

File.Name: 9825b-9803-015.jpg
Notes:
53896911 Here are web-spinning caterpillars of the Fall Webworm Moth (Hyphantria cunea in the family Arctiidae of the Lepidoptera). I guess this is a alder tree, but they were common on many different hardwood trees in the Oregon woods. I never saw them on conifers. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2009)

The adults are a kind of furry white tiger moth, but the larvae are more noticeable. They form "tents" over the leaves of their host trees. Unlike proper tent caterpillars, they form their tents over the actual leaves they will consume. They look kind of creepy, but I believe they don't particularly harm the trees.

We don't have these caterpillars in southern California, but according to Wikipedia, they are one of the few insect pests introduced from North America into other continents.

File.Name: 9825c-9804-049.jpg
Notes:
53895626 Make a circle with your thumb and forefinger - this garden spider is about that big! I guess this is a Yellow Garden Spider (Argiope aurantia) - a kind of Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2009)

Look close at the pattern on the spider's abdomen - do you see a face? These garden spiders hang on their webs in the open all day long, and many of them seem to have "scary faces" to frighten predators.

53933364 Under the volcano...

The view from the porch of Panorama Lodge overlooking the Hood River Valley was spectacular. I performed a wedding ceremony here for two good friends, and we partied and played music for three days and nights.

The birds are American Goldfinches (Carduelis tristis) working Nyjer thistle seed in sock-like feeders hung along the porch of the lodge. The birds love that seed! (Hood River, Oregon, 10 August 2009)

Notes:
53932667 If it smells good, eat it!

This is my son Ewan holding my grandson Ottokar at the Portland Rose Garden. Little Otto knows what he likes and acts appropriately! Otto and his twin brother Kelvin are now past 6 months old. They're starting to crawl. We had a great visit. (Portland, Oregon, 13 August 2009)

File.Name: 9827c_9820-355.jpg
Notes:

I believe this critter is in the genus Laphria - the Bee-like Robber Flies - of the family of Robber Flies (Asilidae) in the order Diptera. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 August 2009)

I was odd that this specimen didn't try to fly when I got close. It managed to crawl away to parts unknown.

File.Name: 9828a_9805-083.jpg
Notes:
54002210 Remarkable "Ping Pong Galls" on leaves of deciduous Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Oregon woods. They are more properly known as the galls of the Speckled Gall Wasp (Besbicus mirabilis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2009)

Oaks make acorns, so these galls must be something else. Galls are fascinating tumor-like plant growths caused by specialized insects and other organisms who manipulate the DNA of their host to provide for their young housed inside. At some time, new wasps will emerge to do it all again and produce a new generation. There's so much to learn in nature!

Interesting trivia: Alfred Kinsey did his PhD work on gall wasps, and in 1930 published a monograph entitled "The Gall Wasp Genus Cynips: A Study in the Origin of Species." Kinsey went on to study human sexual behavior and made a lot of people nervous!
CommonNames: Ping Pong Galls"; Oregon White Oak; Speckled Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus garryana; Besbicus mirabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus garryana; Besbicus mirabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

Notes:
54001645 Little bees (Hymenoptera) working a flower cluster of Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. It was growing on rock ledges by the river. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2009)

This is one of those plants that we do have in the Santa Barbara mountains, but it's just hanging on - like Thimbleberry and Madrone. They belong further north.

The plants were prized for fiber by native Americans. "Cannabinum" sounds like "cannabis", but it's the fiber that's valued.

I took this photo as the last light of sunset was fading and the color in the sky was barely visible to the eye. The exposure is 30 seconds @ f/7 @ ISO 200. The color is smoke from a fire in the mountains behind L.A. 100+ miles away. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, 29 August 2009)

This is the end of my Oregon photos - I've got lots more, but it's time to get back to life in the woods at home. It's been refreshing to pick through these photos. It's been wicked hot at home this week, 105 degrees in the shade - that's 40+ degrees Celsius.

*Sigh* - I go back to school tomorrow, as teacher not student. I'm only working part-time this year, but it's still a struggle on the first day, and I have to get up early. It's been a wonderful summer vacation!

File.Name: 9831a_9827-053.jpg
Notes:
54106722 Is this critter a Walking Stick insect (order Phasmatodea) or a Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera? I captured this photo on the dry flats along the Santa Ynez River. It's prowling on a branch of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 August 2009)

When I started to post these photos, I was sure it was a walking stick - but now I'm pretty sure it's a large Thread-legged bug - possibly Emesaya brevipennis or kin. Its body is about 2 inches long (5 cm). See my [Previous] photo and the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/86407

ScientificName: Phasmatodea; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Emesaya brevipennis
associatedTaxa: Phasmatodea; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Emesaya brevipennis

File.Name: 9831b_9827-050.jpg
Notes:
54106379 Is the critter in my [Next] photo a Walking Stick insect or a big Thread-legged Bug like my [Previous] photo? They look very similar from a distance, but up close they look different. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 August 2009)

Here are closeup photos of the "mouth parts" of the two insects side by side.

The upper insect seems to have two strange multi-segmented mouth parts. But maybe they're too strange to be mouth parts. Assuming its mouth is actually on its head, I don't understand how it would get food from its mandibles to its mouth! I'm pretty sure those are really its front legs.

For comparison, the lower photo shows the very different looking mouth part of a Thread-legged Bug.

Now that I look at these photos, I think the upper photo really shows two front legs held out in front of its body. Its tiny mouthpart may be barely visible in the gap between the "legs" under the head. That means that it's a Thread-legged bug - possibly Emesaya brevipennis or kin. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 9831c_9826-031.jpg
Notes: 54104939 A well-named Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. It's balanced on the edge a leaf of wild blackberry. It look a bit like a walking stick insect, but it's small - only about 1 cm long. Compare my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2009)

File.Name: 9901a_9827-039.jpg
Notes:
54138802 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens has captured a small wasp in the order Hymenoptera). The plant is composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2009)

Usually the late season scale broom is buzzing with pollinators at this time of year, but this year I found few insects working the flowers and many predators. Maybe it's just too darn hot! (It's been 40+ degree Celsius for the past week.)

It's the time for predators like the spiders, ambush bugs, and assassin bugs. Most insect will soon die as winter approaches. The predators are just helping them along!

I'll be gone for a few days on our school camping trip to the Big Sur coast. This year my only official duty is to take photos - of the kids - but I reckon I'll get a few "Treebeard photos" as well!

Notes:
54316024 Always investigate when you see an insect hanging at a weird angle!

This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly which has fallen prey to a dogpile of three Ambush Bugs in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera. I know they're hard to see, but look in the upper right. I guess the upper two ambush bugs are hopeful skinny males and the lower one (with the butterfly) is a gravid female that is large with eggs. The flowering plant is Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Ambush Bugs; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Composite
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Strymon melinus; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae

File.Name: 9906c_9906-078.jpg
Notes:
54315666 I know Ambush Bugs in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera are color-shifters, but this is something else - they NEVER shift this red. Maybe it's a new species (I checked BugGuide) or maybe it has an internal parasite that has changed its color - like a sow bug? Or maybe it got that boiled-lobster color from the record heat we've been having? (Just joking)

This predator is working a new flower cluster of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum in the Polygonaceae family). Flowering now is quite a trick after a drought year and a drought summer and 10 days of 100+ degree weather, but these are tough natives that can handle it. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

File.Name: 9907a_9829-005.jpg
Notes:
54351817 This perfect little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly is working flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. Note the iridescent speckles on the "eyes" along the hind wing. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2009)

Sorry if the colors are a bit off today - my good old CRT monitor is finally dying. I guess it's finally time for an upgrade - after 10 years of constant use!

File.Name: 9907b_9905-023.jpg
Notes:
54351217 This is the only new butterfly I've found this week. I guess it's more interesting that I found any new butterfly at the dry end of a drought year!

She's an uncommon female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a composite Marigold (Tagetes sp.) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2009)

File.Name: 9907c_9906-052.jpg
Notes:
54350891 This handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly is "puddling" - getting moisture or salts from the garden. Sometimes you catch more butterflies by watering bare ground than with flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

I dig the soccer ball eyes and the cryptic colors under the wings!

I rarely find these butterflies visiting flowers. What do they metabolize?

File.Name: 9908a_9827-032.jpg
Notes: 54389028 Beautiful Argiope spider hanging out on the dry flats by the Santa Ynez River, such as it is in this drought year. It's an Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). Maybe it's the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata)? Like many late-season spiders, it seems to have a scary face on its abdomen! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2009)
ScientificName: Argiope; Araneidae). Maybe it's the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata
associatedTaxa: Argiope; Araneidae). Maybe it's the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata

File.Name: 9908b_9906-042.jpg
Notes:
54388707 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey in a composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

Maybe I'm imaging things - but is the spider holding a bit of brown fluff from the flower as bait?
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 9908c_9906-068.jpg
Notes: 54388528 Ferocious looking Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on a flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is another one of those late-season plants that flowers in the dry time. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

File.Name: 9909a_9826-022.jpg
Notes:
54423212 Me and my shadow...

I found this little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the hood of my white truck parked in front of the house. It kept trying to leave, and I kept coaxing it back until I got the photo. I think we were both glad it was over! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2009)

File.Name: 9909b_9827-041.jpg
Notes:
54422951 Here's why knowing the names of all the critters I find in the woods is interesting. Is this spider the same species as the spiders in my [Previous] two photos or is it something different? Connecting male and female is hard enough. This spider has the same bumps on its abdomen, but it is whiter and the "face" on its abdomen is less colorful. Maybe it's the same Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata) as my prvious spiders, maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2009)

You can see a bit of the stabilimentum of this spider. That's the pattern of thick silk on its web. There's some controversy about why these spiders make it. Look it up at Wikipedia.

File.Name: 9909c_9827-035.jpg
Notes: 54422824 Look at this little spider, then look at my [Previous] photo. I'm pretty sure this is the skinny male that goes with the much larger female. It's definitely a Argiope spider - maybe the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata)? It's hanging out on the very dry flats along the Santa Ynez River. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2009)

File.Name: 9910a_9730-183.jpg
Notes: 54455119 Beautiful little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera prowling on a flat spray of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) plant family. This flower is another one of our late-season bloomers that keep the insects alive during our dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

File.Name: 9910b_9905-012.jpg
Notes: 54454898 This is a pretty "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. They don't really stink - they smell like cilantro! The native plant is an interesting Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It has nearly invisible flowers, but they appear late-season at the end of our dry summer and attract the insects. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2009)
CommonNames: Green Stink Bug; Bird's Beak; Figwort

File.Name: 9910c_9906-081.jpg
Notes: 54454474 Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. I don't know what kind of bee this is - so I'll leave it at that. A cloud of these bees was working the baccharis flowers. They move very fast - hard to capture. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Composite

File.Name: 9911a_9911-180.jpg
Notes:
54488840 Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of Bluebeard ("Caryopteris x clandonensis") in the Lamiaceae (or by some accounts Verbenaceae) plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2009)

I like being semi-retired! I still go to school to teach afternoon classes, but I get some time in the morning for the important stuff. Check out all my photos today!
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae; Verbenaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae; Verbenaceae

File.Name: 9911b_9910-141.jpg
Notes: 54488486 Here is an immature "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) resting on a bristly leaf of a composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. I guess it's a "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera, but it only seems to have four antennae segments instead of five. Maybe it's a matter of maturity? (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2009)
CommonNames: True Bug; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Green Stink Bug

File.Name: 9911c_9910-142.jpg
Notes:
54488159 Me and my shadow...

This little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera is in attack pose. It's resting on a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf in the woods beyond the garden. This one is showing its mighty proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2009)

File.Name: 9912a_9912-018.jpg
Notes:
54520745 Mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her precious eggs on a late-season flowering plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

What are they doing guarding eggs now? Will the new spiderlings really survive winter? I guess so.

It was windy today. Each of these photos is the one of many photos that I managed to shoot between the wind gusts. The good news is that our heat wave may finally be breaking.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Bird's Beak; Figwort

File.Name: 9912b_9912-002.jpg
Notes: 54520230 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey in a composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. Note the cast-off carcass of the little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) behind it. This is the fate of most insects as autumn approaches. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: 9912c_9912-025.jpg
Notes: 54519959 This spider-like Assassin Bug nymph in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera has caught a little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera). It's perched on a late-season flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. This is another of the late-bloomers that keep the insects alive during our dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

File.Name: 9913a_9913-043.jpg
Notes:
54554081 This is a giant dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) wasp working new flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. This is our largest wasp, about 2 inches (5 cm) long - one of the biggest wasps in the world. They can straighten out their antennae and curl them back up - neat trick! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 September 2009)

I haven't seen any tarantula spiders out yet this year, but they usually come out of their burrows in the fall to find mates. The wasps battle and try to sting a tarantula to paralyze it. Then they drag the poor spider into their nest and lay an egg on it. Think of the movie "Alien"...
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 9913b_9913-061.jpg
Notes:
54553324 This is an enormous Spiny Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera) near the river today. Well, it SEEMS enormous because it's just a fly - but it's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long. I found this one working the flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 September 2009)

It seems like the "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae!
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Arctiidae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Arctiidae

File.Name: 9913c_9913-048.jpg
Notes: 54552785 Finally there are plenty of Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies working the composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers on the dry wash of the river. This is probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti. They've been conspicuously absent this year. Maybe it was too hot for them? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 September 2009)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Lepidospartum squamatum; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Lepidospartum squamatum; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti

File.Name: 9914a_9912-010.jpg
Notes:
54586971 Here is a male Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with mating colors and a gleam in his eye. It's perched on the concrete retaining wall uphill of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

There was a female nearby and this lizard was making moves, but I guess I ruined the event by sticking my camera into it. Cameras can do that! But I swear the colors of the lizard grew less intense before my eyes. Maybe they can slide those blue scales in and out? Do lizards have chromatophores??

I really wonder why the lizards would be frisky at this time of year, just before the autumnal equinox with summer becoming fall - the very opposite of "lizard weather"? I also saw lizards with mating colors in the spring, so why again? I guess they make one clutch of eggs in the spring to hatch immediately, but they make a backup clutch in the fall to overwinter. That's just my guess.

File.Name: 9914b_9913-052.jpg
Notes:
54585972 Here is one more photo of a giant dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) wasp working new flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 September 2009)

I believe the males have bright orange antennae, but the females have black antennae - so this is a female and my two [Previous] photos are males. The color of these impressive insects isn't really black. It's more iridescent "bluer than blue" - a color so blue that it looks black. (I figure it's kind of like octarine if you dig Terry Pratchett Discworld novels!)
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 September 2009)

It was windy yesterday. I had trouble getting photos, and this insect had trouble just hanging on! Here he is all spread-eagled over the scale broom.

I believe the male wasps have orange antennae and the females have black antennae. See my [Next] photo.

54624610 One of my students has a Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) in his hand. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 September 2009)

I got this photo on my after-school Hike Club to the river last week to jump off the rocks at the swimming hole. It's not the best photo - shade, hand held, zoom lens (rather than my macro lens) - but it's still pretty cool!

Notes:
54624226 The object of his attractions...

This is the female Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) that was causing the male [Previous photos] to get excited and show his mating colors.

She's molting - but I don't think it matters to the male! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

File.Name: 9915c_9912-006.jpg
Notes:
54624000 Here's another photo of the horny male Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with mating colors. It's resting on a pile of White Sage (Salvia apiana) garden scraps. I got this photo before the [Previous] one, and he still has his dewlap puffed out! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

See my [Previous] photo for more info.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; White Sage

File.Name: 9916a_9905-009.jpg
Notes: 54660221 Perfect immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) is resting on a branch of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) plant family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2009)
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; White Sweet Clover; Pea

File.Name: 9916b_9907-098.jpg
Notes:
54660125 The crickets have been singing - here's why!

This is a mating pair of Snowy Tree Crickets (Oecanthus sp. in order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae). I believe that is the male with his wings out, and the demure female is lying prone below him. They either just "did it" or are about to. The flower is a non-native composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2009)

I had an appointment so I had to leave them alone in the early morning shade. I guess they were happy to have some privacy!
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Crickets; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 9916c_9912-033.jpg
Notes: 54659912 Here is a different white-colored Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey in a composite Marigold (Tagetes sp.) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

File.Name: 9917a_9910-132.jpg
Notes: 54699939 I found this plain brown Moth (Lepidoptera) dead in the garden, perched on a cluster of Spearmint (Menthe spicata) flowers in our garden. In a few minutes, it fell off. I guess it was prey to an Ambush Bug in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera which left as I approached. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2009)

File.Name: 9917b_9906-031.jpg
Notes: 54699696 This is a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens - family Stratiomyidae in the insect order Diptera) in the garden. They have amazing patterned eyes, but you can't quite make them out in this photo. I've waited to get a better photo, but I haven't seen this critter since I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2009)

File.Name: 9917c_9828-085.jpg
Notes: 54699444 Here is another cute Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) in the woods. This one is resting on a stump-sprouting burl of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2009)

File.Name: 9918a_9918-178.jpg
Notes: 54719254 A pair of little Wild Bees (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of some kind of late-season composite Aster (Asteraceae) in the garden. This aster grows 8 feet tall, but it's leggy and breaks easily. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2009)

File.Name: 9918b_9917-175.jpg
Notes: 54718989 Here is an exploding seed head of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa - Asclepiadaceae) milkweed in the garden today. There are milkweed seeds everywhere in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2009)

54718636 Here's another photo of the Mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her precious eggs on a late-season flowering plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2009)

Notes:
54748486 Is this the delicate embrace of death?

The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) from my photo twice [Previous] has caught an unfortunate Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera). The flower is an annual composite Marigold (Tagetes). (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

These spiders sit on flowers with perfect camouflage like little wound-up springs ready to attack anything that's the right size. Almost all the summer insects will meet a similar fate. It is their time.

Notes:
54748104 When I got home from my Saturday outing, I watched this fat Crab Spider (Thomisidae) nail this unfortunate Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera). It was perfect timing for me, but not for the bee fly. The flower is an annual composite Marigold (Tagetes). (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

The spiders sit on flowers like little wound-up springs ready to attack anything that's the right size.

Notes:
54747835 Nasty-looking Crab Spider (Thomisidae) caught in the garden with a backlight glow early this morning. The flower is an annual composite Marigold (Tagetes). (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

This spider had a good day hunting. See my [Next] two photos.

Notes:

I've been photographing these little flies for years and never knew what they are. Now I think they might be atypical Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. See BugGuide at:

File.Name: 9920c_9920-053.jpg
Notes:
54778196 Don't get greedy!

The same fat Crab Spider (Thomisidae) I showed yesterday has caught another unfortunate Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) for breakfast this morning. The flower is an annual composite Marigold (Tagetes) in the garden. That's at least 3 flies in 2 days - good hunting for a small spider! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2009)

Note how the spider has twisted the fly's head around.

Does eating all these flies make the spider fatter or does it all go to egg production? There's a lot I don't know about spider physiology!

File.Name: 9921a_9921-074.jpg
Notes:
54811379 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is snuggling into a flower of Bluebeard ("Caryopteris x clandonensis") in the Lamiaceae (or by some accounts Verbenaceae?) plant family in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

Now look closer! The bee's proboscis is coming out of a hole in the flower stem - probably made by a large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin in the order Hymenoptera) as a shortcut into the flower to steal the nectar.

The bee is sucking air, but this shows how long a honey bee's proboscis really is!

Notes:
54810440 This critter looks like a Walking Stick insect (order Phasmatodea) but it's really a Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera - possibly Emesaya brevipennis or kin. Its body is about 1.5 inches long (4 cm). It's scarcely as large around as a broom straw! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

I found this critter on the upstairs porch window screen when I got home from school today. I carried him (or her) downstairs and got the photo on a sage plant. I wonder what habitat it's adapted for? (I guess grass - crane flies are similar.)

Look where the slender antennae meet the body at the bottom of the photo. That's the head with eyes. The front legs extend beyond. They have tiny spines for catching prey. Like all Reduviidae, these critters are predators, though I wonder what they catch?
