File.Name: 9921c_9921-081.jpg
Notes:
54809553 The same fat Crab Spider (Thomisidae) I showed yesterday and the day before has caught yet another unfortunate Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) for breakfast this morning. That's 4 flies in 3 days that I know of!

The spider is perched on the same flower of an annual composite Marigold (Tagetes) in the garden. I guess it's a good perch. How soon will it be satiated? (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

Obviously I can't resist these photos!

File.Name: 9922a_9920-059.jpg
Notes:
54842112 Here are a pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies (look close) getting frisky in the new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. They're not actually "doing it" but they're thinking about it! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 September 2009)

When I got this photo I realized that I haven't seen a Buckeye butterfly all summer - at least I haven't got the photo to prove it. I guess they are a casualty of our drought. We've only had about 1/2 normal rainfall, and this is the dry end of our dry summer.

Todays photos feature a trio of plants that keep the insects alive through the drought:
   Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum),
   Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa), and
   Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii)

All of these are yellow composite flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. There are more!

It continues to be very hot - it was 106 degrees Fahrenheit (41 degrees Celsius) on the upstairs deck when I got home from school today. I feel wilted.

File.Name: 9922b_9919-024.jpg
Notes:
54840993 Handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working a composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is another one of those late-season plants that flowers in the dry time - and this is a drought year! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

This butterfly seems to be missing an antenna club. Life is hard for these critters!
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: 9922c_9919-038.jpg
Notes:
54840468 Pretty Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly - or is it a Lupine Blue? It was alone working a flower of a native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in our mountains. Note the flecks of blue iridescence on the hind wing "eyes". (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

I love these late-season flowers that appear at the end of our desert-dry summer. They are magnets for much insect life.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Lupine Blue; Bush Groundsel; Sunflower

File.Name: 9923a_9919-033.jpg
Notes:
54879462 These interesting native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are crawling over insect galls on this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls make drops of honeydew, and the ants collect it - and maybe the ants keep parasites away? (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

I believe these are the galls of a Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis melliflica) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera.
CommonNames: Ants; Scrub Oak; Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 9923b_9912-027.jpg
Notes:
54879084 The Coast Live Oaks (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon are having a pretty good acorn year despite the local drought. But I've been noticing many acorns falling dark and immature to the ground.

I think I've found the reason why. Note the froth coming from the acorn cup. It's gooey like molasses - and it tastes sweet, like honey! (Sure - I tasted it.)

I think this acorn is the gall-site of a Mottled Acorn Gall (Callirhtiss carmelensis) - or kin - in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The gall wasp larvae is making genetic changes in the oak to produce honey under pressure to force the immature acorn from its cup. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

Ain't nature strange!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oaks; Mottled Acorn Gall
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhtiss carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhtiss carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 9923c_9906-066.jpg
Notes:
54878296 Here are a group of strange oak galls on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family). I guess they are the galls of the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

Oaks make acorns, so these galls must be something else. Galls are fascinating tumor-like plant growths caused by specialized insects and other organisms who manipulate the DNA of their host to provide for their young housed inside. At some time, new wasps will emerge to do it all again and produce a new generation. There's so much to learn in nature!
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 9924a_9921-071.jpg
Notes:
54911024 With the muted early-morning colors, this one is worthy of nikaj_online!

This is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

File.Name: 9924b_9921-079.jpg
Notes: 54910864 I was already focused close on the marigold with a crab spider when this Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) landed on the Zinnia flower right besides it. This is almost full frame. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

File.Name: 9924c_9922-142.jpg
Notes: 54910655 Here are the intimate flower parts of a native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) plant family. You can see the interesting pollen grains of these favorite hummingbird flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2009)

File.Name: 9925a_9925-263.jpg
Notes:
54940514 This handsome little rusty Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) was working an Aster (Asteraceae) flower in the garden this morning. You can see the fly's body through the rusty-haired vestiture. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2009)

The temperature continues to be very hot - 100+ degrees (38+ degrees Celsius) every day it seems. We'll have a hot weekend, then maybe a break next week.
Locality: 38+ degrees Celsius) every day it seems.  We'll have a hot weekend

File.Name: 9925b_9925-266.jpg
Notes:
54940066 The Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) are back on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. And so is a predator Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. I wonder if the aphids tell stories at night of the "Volkswagen beetle from hell"? (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2009)

These perennial plants are not native in California, and I always wonder how these interesting aphids find it year after year.
CommonNames: Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed; Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: 9925c_9924-244.jpg
Notes: 54939450 Red Rock is a legendary local swimming hole on the upper section of the Santa Ynez River known as Paradise. I usually avoid it because it's also a legendary party spot, but I took my after-school Hike Club kids there yesterday for a swim, and we had it almost to ourselves. If you look close, you can see two of my DMS kids climbing the rocks at the bottom of the photo for scale! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2009)

File.Name: 9926a_9926-008.jpg
Notes:
54974440 The upper male butterfly is (literally) making a pass at the lower female!

These are a pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies getting frisky in the flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. They get close, she flutters her wings in anticipation - and some darn photographer gets too close to spoil the moment! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

I went to the river today to look for an uncommon Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly - but I think it was too hot for butterflies. It was 107 today - 42 degrees Celsius - too hot for man or beast. It's expected to cool down on Monday.
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Scale Broom; Great Purple Hairstreak
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Atlides halesus
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Atlides halesus

File.Name: 9926b_9926-022.jpg
Notes:
54973991 I guess this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. These beetles seem to appear of all the late-season Asteraceae. It has short wing covers and a protruding abdomen - barely visible here. It also has a face full of pollen! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

These beetles have interesting natural history and cultural history. The larvae go through more larval instars than most insects. They look for egg cases of grasshoppers (Orthoptera) as a host, and then go through further stages before becoming adults. I don't understand the details.

I've read that these beetles are the source of the original "Spanish Fly" - the stuff of legend when I was in high school in the 1960's. Some of these blister beetles (probably not this one) can secrete defensive fluids containing the chemical cantharidin that cause irritation and blistering of human skin. Sounds horrible.

File.Name: 9926c_9926-018.jpg
Notes: 54973574 Beautiful honeybee-sized Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It has remarkable patterned wings. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

File.Name: 9927a_9927-030.jpg
Notes:
55007619 Beautiful dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) perched on a dead branch in the woods. I think it might be a Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens). The name seems appropriate. It must have wandered quite a distance because there's not a drop of water in our creek at the end of this drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

It's interesting that the spot in its eye is red rather than black. I can't remember ever seeing this.

It was another hot day - 100 degrees (38 Celsius). Tomorrow is supposed to be much cooler, but I wonder when the big cool-down will happen?
Locality: 38 Celsius).  Tomorrow is supposed to be much cooler

File.Name: 9927b_9927-048.jpg
Notes:
55007098 Here is the impressive proboscis of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. It was resting on a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf in the woods just beyond the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

Mighty as it's proboscis looks, these critters are very s_l_o_w. I wonder how they catch anything!

File.Name: 9927c_9926-007.jpg
Notes:
55006661 Here is more love play among the Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies near the river! The female is on the left trying to look nonchalant as she feeds on the flower, but she is quivering in anticipation. (Note that the head is sharp, but the wings are trembling.) They didn't actually "do it" then and there. Butterflies seem to play a lot before they get serious - just like us.

The flowers are a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It was wicked hot when I got this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

File.Name: 9928a_9926-015.jpg
Notes:
55038528 Here is another photo of the remarkable honeybee-sized Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54973574

This is looking eye-to-eyes with the fly. It's hard proboscis is in "UP position". It uses hydraulic pressure inside its head to bring it back down. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

The hot weather cooled down as expected today. I'm so glad it's in the 80s rather than the 100s! More cooling is expected tonight. It finally feels like autumn, but insects are scarce. I'll soon be scrambling for photos to post until the mushrooms come.

File.Name: 9928b_9921-082.jpg
Notes: 55037913 A little tan-colored Fly (order Diptera) resting on a sage leaf in the garden. It seems to have it's proboscis out working a bird dropping on the leaf! I guess these little tan flies are Pomace Flies aka Small Fruit Flies in the family Drosophilidae. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)
CommonNames: Fly; Pomace Flies; Small Fruit Flies

File.Name: 9928c_9927-032.jpg
Notes:
55037458 Amazing eyes of a dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) perched on a dead branch in the woods. I'm pretty sure it's a Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens). This is another photo of the same dragonfly as my [Previous] shot. The dragonflies have favorite perches that they come back to. You can focus close and wait. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

It's interesting that the the spot in its eye is now black instead of red.

File.Name: 9929a_9927-051.jpg
Notes:
55073865 This is just a dead Ant (family Formicidae of the order Hymenoptera) that I noticed on a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

I wonder how it got here, and what brought it down in the middle of this leaf?

It reminds me of the old Bob Dylan song "Only a Hobo" (that I remember more from the bluegrass band Old and in the Way):


   "As I was out walking on a corner one day,
   I spied an old hobo, in a doorway he lay.
   His face was all grounded in the cold sidewalk floor
   And I guess he'd been there for the whole night or more.


   "Only a hobo, but one more is gone
   Leavin' nobody to sing his sad song
   Leavin' nobody to carry him home
   Only a hobo, but one more is gone"

File.Name: 9929b_9920-064.jpg
Notes: 55073116 Here is a rather large Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the order Hemiptera) working the flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

File.Name: 9929c_9920-066.jpg
Notes:
55072543 I believe this beauty is a Harlequin Cabbage Bug (Murgantia histrionica) in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. My books say they prefer Mustard (Brassicaceae) plants, but this one is working a composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

Of course it's bug-eyed!

File.Name: 9930a_9920-056.jpg
Notes: 55141001 I believe this is a female Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a buckwheat twig on the dry flats by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2009)

File.Name: 9a01a_9927-039.jpg
Notes:
55141729 Here is a little caterpillar (order Lepidoptera) on a late-season flowering plant of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The plant barely looks alive after our dry summer, but it still has a few flowers. The caterpillar is a surprise. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

So many butterflies, but I rarely find caterpillars. A Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly was working the flowers.

It's been a busy couple of nights, and I'll be busy with a Natural History Society meeting tomorrow night. Better busy than dead!
Locality:

File.Name: 9a01b_9919-026.jpg
Notes: 55141312 Pretty - but deadly for insects - little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a native flower of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in our mountains. They sit and wait like little wind-up mousetraps on the flowers all set to spring on their prey. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2009)

File.Name: 9a02a_9a02-184.jpg
Notes: 55183806 Remarkable yellowjacket wasp-mimic fly (order Diptera) resting on a cactus in the garden. I guess it's a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It has amazing patterned eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a02b_9a01-128.jpg
Notes: 55183482 At first glance this critter looks like a honey bee - but look closer! It's an interesting Fly (order Diptera) working a late-season composite Aster (Asteraceae) in the garden. It's probably a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae). It perfectly mimics the bee's translucent abdomen. (I hope to find this fly again so I can get a photo with some backlight to bring out the likeness even more.) (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a02c_9a01-124.jpg
Notes:
55183134 Perfect Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a late-season composite Aster (Asteraceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2009)

Look close at the bee - then look at my next photo!

File.Name: 9a03a_9a02-177.jpg
Notes: 55204264 Here is a perfect Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched with Zinnia flowers behind in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a03b_9a03-007.jpg
Notes: 55204048 Here is a beautiful little Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) visiting flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. The adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a03c_9a02-188.jpg
Notes:
55203897 Here is another photo of of the remarkable yellowjacket wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) resting on a cactus in the garden. I guess it's Spilomyia interrupta or kin, see my [Previous] photo. Yes, it has amazing eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2009)

Either it's grooming its wings or it's trying the scratch one of those hard-to-reach places!

File.Name: 9a04a_9a03-024.jpg
Notes:
55235964 Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

It finally feels like autumn. It was cool and windy today. The highs were in the 50s - that's 50 degrees less than it was last weekend!

File.Name: 9a04b_9a03-036.jpg
Notes:
55235681 This Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera is perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near home. You can see its proboscis tucked under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

I checked at BugGuide. It looks a lot like Acholla multispinosa, but that seems to be an east coast creature. (See http://bugguide.net/node/view/36336)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Acholla multispinosa

File.Name: 9a04c_9a03-042.jpg
Notes:
55235295 New flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. These are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear - even before the rain this year. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

Even in this drought year, they know that it's time to flower after the equinox. It feels like a miracle every year when the first new flowers appear. How do they know?

File.Name: 9a05a_9a05-055.jpg
Notes:
55267031 Life and death in the woods!

We had some excitement when I got home from teaching at school today. I believe this is a Cooper's Hawk (Accipiter cooperii) that has brought down a Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) right under our upstairs window. Predator and prey are about the same size. I fumbled for a lens and got this photo looking almost straight down at the birds through the dirty window screen. Not a great photo, but it captures the moment! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2009)

The pigeon suddenly came back to life after I got this photo! The hawk lost it and took off for a nearby Madrone tree, and the pigeon flopped under our grape vine to die. The hawk came back, but I don't think it found the bird. Que lastima. I'll check for a dead pigeon under the grape tomorrow and report.

File.Name: 9a05b_9a03-012.jpg
Notes:
55265923 I first thought this interesting fly (order Diptera) working new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family was a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae). But looking at the photo, I realize it is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). For one thing, it is bristly. For another, it has large "calypters" - those large lobes at the base of the wing. Tachinids are calyptrate but syrphids are acalyptrate. Little differences matter with flies! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

I want to do a photo exhibit on "Families of Flies" for my next DMS science fair project. Expect lots of fly photos between now and next March!
CommonNames: fly; Coyote Bush; Sunflower; Syrphid Flower Fly; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Syrphidae; Tachinidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Syrphidae; Tachinidae

File.Name: 9a05c_9a03-030.jpg
Notes:
55265212 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) is still guarding her precious eggs on a late-season flowering plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

She's been waiting for a while. See my photo from 12 September of the same spider:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54520745

ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 9a06a_9a03-016.jpg
Notes: 55295251 Here is a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) munching on the flowers of a female Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. Heh - the grasshopper has bright red, white, and blue patriotic colors on its hind legs! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a06b_9a03-022.jpg
Notes: 55294877 Here is a perfect Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the late-season composite flowers of a female Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. Look how the bee's antennae guide its proboscis to the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a06c_9a03-039.jpg
Notes:
55294419 There are still a few (very few) butterflies in the woods. This is a female Common Checkered Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working a male - that is, staminate - flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. Coyote bush plants are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This is one of the few male plants I've seen in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

I looked for the dead pigeon under the grape arbor this morning, but there was none to be found. (See my [Previous] photo.) Maybe the hawk came back in the growing darkness and got his kill? I hope so.
CommonNames: Common Checkered Skipper; Coyote Bush; Sunflower

File.Name: 9a07a_9a01-173.jpg
Notes: 55332952 Last week I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Refugio Beach for a low-tide beach walk. We surprised this flock of seagulls into flight. I think I like the shadows as much as the birds! (Refugio Beach, Gaviota Coast, 1 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a07b_9a03-010.jpg
Notes:
55332790 Something is happening to the native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in this drought year. Most of the plants - especially the male, staminate plants of this dioecious species - are not producing real flowers, but only these dry husks of flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

Coyote Bush is our most important late season flower for insects. It's absence is missed.

We always have a summer drought in our Santa Barbara mountains - that's part of our usual Mediterranean climate summer. But this year, our dry summer came after a 50% normal rain winter. The woods - and the insect populations - are hurting...

File.Name: 9a07c_9a03-016.jpg
Notes:
55332234 This a a close crop of my [Previous] photo of a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) munching on the flowers of a native composite female Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2009)

This grasshopper has remarkable mouthparts - the segmented maxillary and labial palps are like little hands that taste and feed food to its mouth! They remind me of Bender's little mini-arms when he played washboard with Beck! (Futurama episode 3ACV13 of course.)

File.Name: 9a08a_9a08-128.jpg
Notes:
55364617 Here is a tiny native Ant (family Formicidae of the order Hymenoptera) working a new female (pistillate) flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

See my [Previous] photo to get an idea of the size of the flower. I shot the photo at about 1.2 magnification, and I still had to crop deep for this image. The little ant's body is so strange!

File.Name: 9a08b_9a08-127.jpg
Notes:
55364210 This is the critter I was trying to capture today - it's a (relatively) huge Spiny Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera). These flies are 16-18 mm long - about 3/4 inch!

They stayed out of reach high in the flowering Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. This is a tight crop without enough detail. Maybe tomorrow! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)
CommonNames: Spiny Tachina Fly; Coyote Bush; Sunflower
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 9a08c_9a08-141.jpg
Notes:
55363911 I was trying for a different photo of the new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family with backlight. These are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear - even before the rain this year.

Look - I captured a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on top of the flowers! I didn't see it when I took the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a09a_9a08-173.jpg
Notes: 55390636 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to "Lizard's Mouth" yesterday to play on the rocks! It's a popular site for rock climbing and hanging out away from authority in the mountains near home. The rocks are fantastic! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a09b_9a08-164.jpg
Notes:
55390415 People keep adding to this memorial for Nicholas Samuel Markowitz who was murdered and buried here in the mountains on August 8, 2000. It's a sad story, told in the movie "Alpha Dog". I don't know who carved the memorial. They did a good job. I reckon it's good to keep the story of his life and death known.

I got this photo near "Lizard's Mouth" - a popular site for rock climbing and hanging out away from authority in the mountains near home. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a09c_9a08-115.jpg
Notes: 55389851 I can't resist another photo of the tiny native Ant (family Formicidae of the order Hymenoptera) working a new female (pistillate) flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a10a_9a10-007.jpg
Notes:
55422142 We had a blessed event in the canyon!

This is a protective mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) standing over her brood of newly hatched spiderlings! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2009)

I've posted several photos of this particular spider before:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55265212
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54718636
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54520745

It was a crummy day for photos - overcast and windy, but tomorrow might be worse. There's a storm coming Monday or Tuesday night. This will be our first rain since June, and our first real rain since April - the real blessed event in our forecast! I wonder what the little spiderlings will do?

File.Name: 9a10b_9a10-013.jpg
Notes:
55421598 It's too late to warn you not to look at this photo if you suffer from Insect Fear! But then you probably wouldn't be looking at my fotolog anyway.

This is a clutch of new spiderlings hatched by a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae). Some of the tiny spiders already have green heads. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2009)

It was overcast and windy most of today. Hoping for the best, I took 140 pictures waiting for that moment of calm between the gusts - but I only kept 14. The photo is not sharp, but what would I focus on anyway?

File.Name: 9a10c_9a10-018.jpg
Notes:
55421244 I believe this strange net-winged creature is a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. Prowling at BugGuide.net, I think it might also be family Issidae and genus Neaethus. See:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/46331

The photos from BugGuide are all from California, so this might be a local critter. I know nothing about it, though I have found it before. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a11a_9a11-022.jpg
Notes:
55444654 Perfect acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia in the Fagaceae plant family) with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2009)

Well maybe not raindrops yet - more like fog drops, but they'll do for now. It was cloudy all day - good for saturated colors, though the wind was a problem. The real rain is forecast for Tuesday/Wednesday. This will be our first rain since June, and our first real rain since April. We need it!

File.Name: 9a11b_9a11-029.jpg
Notes: 55444240 Rodents have been busy eating the new acorns of the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. They seem to be eating the acorns even before they fall! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a11c_9a11-030.jpg
Notes:
55443977 Strange wasp galls on two leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. These are the homes of Crystalline Gall Wasps (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. You can see the actual volcano-like galls of the wasps emerging through the fluff. They seem to be clustered near the edges of the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2009)

Galls are "tumor-like growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in response to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - but why are they so darned beautiful? I did NO color corrections to this photo!

Actually, it's been a poor year for insect galls in our mountains. It's not that the galls have failed in this drought summer, but that the wasps that make them have failed. Many insects put all their eggs into one basket, so an unusually dry year like this one has been hits them hard.
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 9a12a_9a11-034.jpg
Notes:
55483404 I believe this beauty is a genus Polistes paper wasp in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera - but I'm not sure of the species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 October 2009)

We were in a "cloud cap" in the mountains, so I went to the river on Sunday to find some sun. I found the sun, but the wind seemed to grow as I arrived. I only got a few photos before I gave up.

Our storm is approaching! We've already had some showers today - real water from the sky! - and lots more is expected tomorrow. Wish us well.

File.Name: 9a12b_9a11-033.jpg
Notes: 55482567 I guess this is a Green Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Phaenicia sericata or kin, family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera). It's just a trash fly, the kind you don't want to land on your picnic lunch. But it looks pretty visiting working the flowers of this native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 October 2009)
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 9a12c_9a11-037.jpg
Notes:
55481822 I found another mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) protecting her sack of precious eggs by the river yesterday. The plant is a mostly spent Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) on the dry wash along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 October 2009)

These spiders are easy to recognize in silhouette because of their prominent chelicerae or jaws. If they are on a schedule, the eggs will soon hatch! See my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55422142
   and [Previous] to that.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Hairy Golden Aster
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides

File.Name: 9a13a_9927-046.jpg
Notes:
55515763 Beautiful flower of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. The stamens look like hungry little birds! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

The rain came as advertised today. We've had over 3 1/4 inches (almost 9 cm) so far, and more heavy rain is expected tonight. Not bad considering that our last real rain was in April! What do all the insects do in the rain?

I'll be busy on next two nights and probably won't be able to post photos.
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 9a13b_9a10-014.jpg
Notes:
55515042 A large branch of the willow tree by the creek cracked and fell, and the leaves are full of the galls of the Willow Tube Gall Midge fly in the genus Iteomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The galls have little openings at the apex, and only occur on the undersides of the leaves. The willow is an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis in the Salicaceae plant family). (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2009)

Did you spot the little Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - in the upper-left of the photo? They are starting to converge in the canyon again!
CommonNames: Willow Tube Gall Midge; Arroyo Willow; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName:

File.Name: 9a13c_9a08-121.jpg
Notes:
55514456 Here's another photo of the tiny native Ant (family Formicidae of the order Hymenoptera) working a new female (pistillate) flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. The little ant's body is so strange! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2009)

I have more photos of the same ant at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55389851 and
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55364617

File.Name: 9a14a_9a14-039.jpg
Notes:
55611275 Here's a Giant Crane Fly (Holousia grandis or kin) in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging in the composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) by the garden at school on the day after our mighty rain storm that dropped 10 inches of rain on the pass. (Dunn School, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 October 2009)

These flies seem so awkward when they wander into our homes, but they look at home here in the brush. They have such amazing "old philosopher" faces!
ScientificName: Holousia grandis; Tipulidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Holousia grandis; Tipulidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: 9a15a_9a15-042.jpg
Notes:
55611712 On the day after our mighty rain storm that dropped 10 inches of rain at home, I didn't notice many insects - but I did see lots of these tiny Leafhoppers (family Cicadellidae in the order Homoptera). I captured this one grooming its wings on a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf. The lizards were picking them off on the side of our house! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2009)

Notice those comb-like spines on its rear legs. I'll bet their only purpose is to groom their wings.

File.Name: 9a16a_9a16-147.jpg
Notes:
55612289 Here is a perfect Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera with some backlight. It's perched on the edge of a leaf of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", family Labiatae) in the garden this morning. At least I think it's a Drosophila fly. Every hair on it's body probably has a name, and I haven't counted every one! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2009)

We had 10.3 inches of rain on Tuesday night/Wednesday morning - that's 26 cm! That's a good way to end a drought summer. And today it was in the 90s! I'll be looking for the White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii) mushrooms this weekend. This is the perfect weather combination!
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; Waverly Sage; White King Bolete
ScientificName: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Labiatae; Boletus barrowsii
associatedTaxa: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Labiatae; Boletus barrowsii

File.Name: 9a17a_9a17-010.jpg
Notes:
55640438 What the rain brings...

The rain brought a dogpile of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. The ladybug rave is on! Mating is definitely occurring. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2009)

There are no mushrooms yet and few insects after our huge rain last week. I'm willing to give it a few days!

File.Name: 9a17b_9a17-019.jpg
Notes: 55640222 I was worried whether our mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) and her babies survived the major rain we had this week. They did just fine! (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a17c_9a17-036.jpg
Notes:
55639939 Here is a tiny Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) ready to take wing from a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden today. The background in lens flare from my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2009)

I wonder where it's flying to?
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 9a18a_9a17-038.jpg
Notes:
55674137 Native Ant (family Formicidae of the order Hymenoptera) working a flower of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa in the Asclepiadaceae plant family) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

I don't think the ant is going for the nectar in the cups (or "hood") of this interesting flower. It's more interested in the "pollinia" or pollen sacks between the cups.

File.Name: 9a18b_9a18-044.jpg
Notes:
55673806 Perfect Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) resting in the morning sun on a leaf of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", family Labiatae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

Did you know that bees have such hairy eyes? Think about it - there must be an opening sculpted into the bee's exoskeleton for every one of those hairs!

File.Name: 9a18c_9a18-066.jpg
Notes:
55673553 Here's a different Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle - Coccinella septempunctata. Yes, it does have seven spots! It is much more red in color than the orange Convergent Lady Beetles Hippodamia convergens that are converging in our canyon right now. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

This lady beetle is working male (staminate) flowers of the composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). It's dusted with pollen. It's a dirty job, but someone's got to do it!
Locality: Baccharis pilularis). It's dusted with pollen. It's a dirty job
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Seven-spotted Lady Beetle; Convergent Lady Beetles; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Coccinella septempunctata; Hippodamia convergens; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Coccinella septempunctata; Hippodamia convergens; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: 9a19a_9a17-042.jpg
Notes:
55705284 Nasty looking Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera glowering at me from its perch on a milkweed leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

Insects are really getting scarce after our heavy rain last week - despite the mostly warm temperatures. I have to assume that the heavy rainfall - close to 3 inches per hour at its peak - killed many insects outright. I figure that's robbing the spiders of their prey!

(Then again, insects and spiders were already getting scarce as a result of our recently ended drought.)

File.Name: 9a19b_9a18-071.jpg
Notes: 55704792 Impressive Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen wrapping up an unfortunate wasp in the garden. These wasps are common under the eaves of our porch. They make neat orb-webs a foot or two across. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a19c_9a19-079.jpg
Notes:
55704434 I haven't found any mushrooms yet after our big rain last week, but I did notice this strange orange fungus growing over burned soil on the site of the Gap Wildfire last summer. At least it's colored like a chanterelle mushroom! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2009)

Note the whitish edge around the orange center. It's growing like a culture in an agar plate!

File.Name: 9a20a_9a20-102.jpg
Notes:
55733081 I apologize for the brutality of this photo, but I really saw it happen in the woods today.

The same Ambush Bug (in the family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) in my [Previous] photo has captured the same Green Bottle Fly (Phaenicia sericata in the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera) in my photo [Previous] to that! Look for the ambush bug in the upper-right of my photo. It happened right before my eyes, but I wasn't fast enough to capture the moment with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

This really is a brutal photo. The ambush bug has the fly's proboscis in its crab-like fist, and it's sticking its own proboscis into the opening. I guess it injected a venom because the fly quickly became still. Notice that the fly's head is turned completely around.

I guess it's a good reminder - whenever I think insects are scarce in the canyon, look closer!
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera

File.Name: 9a20b_9a20-097.jpg
Notes:
55731255 This little Ambush Bug in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera is perched on a cluster of male (staminate) flowers of the composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

Notice the crab-like fists of this predator!

Keep watching - there really is a story here!

File.Name: 9a20c_9a20-094.jpg
Notes:
55730892 This Green Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Phaenicia sericata or kin, family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera) is working flowers of male (staminate) flowers of the composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

Keep watching - there's a story here!
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Coyote Bush

File.Name: 9a21a_9a20-092.jpg
Notes:
55768118 This is one of my favorite photos! It will take you a moment to figure out what you're looking at...

Look - here are two tiny Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) "doing it" - they are mating while hanging in the air suspended by silken threads. I reckon that's the female on the bottom, because she is larger with eggs. Now look close at her (upper) eyes - isn't she giving a sweet glance to her partner? (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

They are celebrating their nuptials among the male (staminate) flowers of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon.

I'm not sure how I noticed this remarkable scene, except that photo-ops were short, so I was looking very close at the few flowers left in the woods. I'm glad I did!
Locality:
Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon.

I'm not sure how I noticed this remarkable scene

File.Name: 9a21b_9a18-067.jpg
Notes:
55766794 These interesting native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are crawling over insect galls on this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls make drops of honeydew, and the ants collect it - and maybe the ants keep parasites away in exchange? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

I believe these are the galls of a Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis melliflica) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. the similar galls of the Flat-topped Honeydew Gall wasp (Disholcaspis eldoradensis). (*Sigh* - nature is always complicated!)

It's interesting that I have another recent photo of these galls:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54879462
Notice that the ants are different!
CommonNames: Ants; Scrub Oak; Twig Gall Wasp; Flat-topped Honeydew Gall wasp
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis eldoradensis

File.Name: 9a21c_9a20-103.jpg
Notes:
55766000 Here's another photo of the brutal encounter between a Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) and a Green Bottle Fly (Phaenicia sericata in the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera) yesterday. The ambush bug is in the upper-middle of my photo. Even close up, they are hard to see because of their perfect camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

You can see that the ambush bug has the fly's proboscis in its crab-like fist, and it's sticking its own proboscis into fly's face. It soon transfered it's proboscis to the fly's neck, and all was quiet.

Another pollinator goes down. Sooner or later, this seems to be the fate of all the late-season pollinators.
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phaenicia sericata; Calliphoridae; Diptera

File.Name: 9a22a_9a20-084.jpg
Notes:
55796527 This beautiful shaggy-bearded Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen is perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia in the Fagaceae plant family). It had a two-foot orb-web nearby. It looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides - but the markings aren't quite right. I reckon that"s the right genus though. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

I'm going to a wedding near Temecula tomorrow for one of my past students. It's a long drive. I'll be back with more photos on Saturday night.

File.Name: 9a22b_9a20-088.jpg
Notes:
55795569 Beautiful mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) and a few of her tan-colored babies. She's perched on a late-season flowering plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

She's been tending to her babies in the same perch at least since September 12 when I first got a photo of her guarding her nest. That's real dedication and a strong mothering instinct.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54520745

File.Name: 9a22c_9a20-108.jpg
Notes: 55794795 Large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on female flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a23a_9a16-160.jpg
Notes: 55859127 Here is a small Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working the glandular new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family with the sun behind. If you look close, you can see the bee's proboscis entering the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a24a_9a20-115.jpg
Notes: 55859667 This common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is doing a cheat on this flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", family Labiatae) in the garden. Instead of going through the throat of the flower and helping pollination, it is taking a shortcut and going right through the calyx to get to the nectar. Many insects know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a24b_9a16-146.jpg
Notes:
55859414 Here is another strange net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. This one is perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia in the Fagaceae plant family) leaf, which gives a sense of scale. Those bristles on the oak leaves are about 1 mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

Prowling at BugGuide.net, I think it might be family Issidae and genus Neaethus. See:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/46331

The photos from BugGuide are all from California, so this might be a local critter.

File.Name: 9a25a_9a25-012.jpg
Notes:
55884110 This Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera is trying to warm up with the first sunlight (at about 9:30 AM!) in the garden. It's perched on a leaf of Spearmint (Menthe spicata). (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

Morning light is coming later and later to our garden on the north-facing side of the mountains. The darn trees keep growing, which makes it worse every year.

Note how the wasp is supporting its long-petioled abdomen with its wings. It kept repeating this ritual: lift abdomen with wings, wings rise above abdomen, drop wings below abdomen - and repeat.
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Menthe spicata
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Menthe spicata

File.Name: 9a25b_9a25-021.jpg
Notes:
55883666 Little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower head of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is one of our most dependable late-season flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

The spider seems to be pointing the wrong way to catch prey!

File.Name: 9a25c_9a25-024.jpg
Notes:
55883248 Around and 'round...

This large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen has captured a Yellow Jacket-type wasp in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera. I took a dozen photos, and I can see the wasp being turned 'round and 'round. The spider is perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia tree in the Fagaceae plant family). (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides - but the markings aren't quite right. I reckon that's the right genus though.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Yellow Jacket; Coast Live Oak; Cat-Faced Spider

File.Name: 9a26a_9a25-005.jpg
Notes:
55919941 This very large Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) is trying to warm up on a sun-pointing leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) in the canyon. I guess it was an inch long, 2.5 cm, almost too-black to photograph. It has punk hair! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

It takes longer for the BIG insects to warn up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. An insect that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded insects mostly stay small.

File.Name: 9a26b_9a25-018.jpg
Notes:

55918486 Here's another photo of the large whitish Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on female flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2009)

I disturbed it when I got my photo at:

File.Name: 9a27a_9a22-169.jpg
Notes:
55952966 This beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly is perched high in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs porch. I captured this photo with my "big glass" - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens opened all the way. Think of it as a tele-macro photo. My butterfly book describes it like this: "A cross-dresser of sorts, this species is a lady, not an admiral." (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2009)

I haven't seen these butterflies on the ground yet. They seem to be taking the place (along with the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) in my [Previous] photo) of the California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterflies that patrol the skyways and almost never visit flowers. I don't know what they eat.

It's seems odd to see this butterfly now high in the oaks, but I've photographed them before in June, September, November, and December - so I guess this sighting fits in.
Locality:
Adelpha bredowii) butterflies that patrol the skyways and almost never visit flowers. I don't know what they eat.

It's seems odd to see this butterfly now high in the oaks

File.Name: 9a27b_9a25-004.jpg
Notes:
55952065 There's been a regime change with our butterflies since our big rain two weeks ago. All of the summer butterflies have disappeared, but their replacements are here.

This is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched high in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs porch. I captured this photo with my "big glass" - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens opened all the way. Think of it as a tele-macro photo. I haven't seen them on the ground yet. What do they eat? (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

I've seen these butterflies in December through March in previous years, and in August up in Oregon. I guess they migrate. I've never seen them so early in our mountains. Is it an omen?

File.Name: 9a27c_9a25-013.jpg
Notes:
55950967 Another photo of the very large Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) which is trying to warm up on a sunny leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) in the canyon. See my [Previous] photo. I guess it was an inch long, 2.5 cm, almost too-black to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

Tomanshela was right - except for the wings, it looks more like a furry mammal!

File.Name: 9a28a_9a18-056.jpg
Notes:
55985829 Beautiful autumn seeds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family. The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

Well, it's happened. I'm all out of fresh photos of insects in the woods. Partly it's because I was away at Sarah and Justin's wedding on Friday and Saturday, and it's been cold and windy since then - but mostly it's because the seasons really are turning to winter, and insects are getting scarce. It's time to photograph other life in the woods...

File.Name: 9a28b_9a19-076.jpg
Notes: 55985263 Here are flower heads of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is one of our most dependable late-season flowers that is still going strong in our mountains as summer turns into winter. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2009)

File.Name: 9a28c_9a18-055.jpg
Notes:
55984909 Here is a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family growing in the canyon. It is showing both almost ripe fruit and new flower buds that will open soon. The leaves have an intense Bay Laurel taste and are useful for cooking if you don't overdo it. The fruit have an even more intense taste, but they hold tasty nuts that the squirrels love. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2009)

The fruit look like avocado "pups" - and they are indeed related.

File.Name: 9a29a_9a29-122.jpg
Notes:
56017526 I finally found a sign that it rained 10 inches just two weeks ago!

Here is a strange Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from a very old piece of fallen oak in the woods. It looks like little rasta dreds coming out of the wood! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2009)

These are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as these tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log to another.

I've been waiting for mushrooms after our rain, but so far nothing.

File.Name: 9a29b_9a18-018.jpg
Notes:
56017002 Ripe acorns of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. If you look close at the acorn on the right, you can see the exit hole of a weevil or other critter who probably ate the developing acorn. If you look really close at the green leaf in the foreground, you can see tiny galls that are probably the same as my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2009)

Oaks really are the "mother-trees" in our woods - they support lots of life!

File.Name: 9a29c_9a05-043.jpg
Notes:
56016633 Tiny Cynipid Wasp Galls made by tiny wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family) - with exit holes. I have no idea which wasp they are. For scale, the bristles on the oak leaves are about 1 mm long. Just think - some critter spent most of it's life inside these little capsules! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2009)

Critters are scarce in the woods - I'm looking closer!

File.Name: 9a30a_9a30-138.jpg
Notes:
56045941 Here is another photo of the same Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) with long antennae working the glandular new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in my [Previous] photo. I guess it is a Burrowing Bee in the genus Andrena. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2009)

This plant is the first of the new season flowers to bloom. Insects are getting scarce in the woods, but bees - wild and domestic - seem to stand the cold mornings better than other insects.

File.Name: 9a30c_9a30-133.jpg
Notes:
56045332 Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working new glandular flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2009)

This plant is the first of the new season flowers to bloom. Insects are getting scarce in the woods, but honey bees can stand the cold mornings better than most insects!

File.Name: 9a31a_9a31-008.jpg
Notes:
56077445 This is my scary Halloween photo this year!
(I almost forgot, but nature provides!)

But there really nothing scary about it. I was surprised to find this Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera working the flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden this afternoon. Shouldn't they be out collecting caterpillars and spiders? (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2009)

We went to my former student Ryan's Eagle Scout ceremony today. I was surprised to find myself named as one of 3 mentors for his award. I got a nifty pin and a little teary-eyed.

File.Name: 9a31b_9a31-016.jpg
Notes:
56077197 Here is an interesting Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera that I found prowling outside the bedroom window this evening as the sun was getting low. I took this photo with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2009)

I don't think this is a Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta), but if I'm wrong it really is a scary Halloween visitor!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug

File.Name: 9a31c_9a31-012.jpg
Notes: 56076869 Here are lots of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) lined up like cattle on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. I reckon they are as pretty as the flowers, so we leave them be. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2009)

File.Name: 9b01a_9b01-024.jpg
Notes:
56102140 How strange - a suicide pact between Junco birds?

We found these two Dark-eyed Juncos (Junco hyemalis oreganus) aka "Oregon Juncos" trapped dead in a wide-open but broken plexiglass display case early this morning before the sun was up. This photo is just as we found them. I can't imagine how the birds got trapped and died here. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2009)

Think of your favorite romantic suicide deaths...

File.Name: 9b01b_9b01-029.jpg
Notes: 56101400 For those who notice such things, the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) is still watching her young on a now-spent plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. Maybe she'll guard them until she dies? (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2009)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Bird's Beak; Figwort
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 9b01c_9b01-034.jpg
Notes:
56101040 Here is a rather large Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the order Hemiptera) on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It was the only one I saw - just about the only reasonably large insect I saw today. It kept trying to hide - I had to nudge it into a position to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2009)

I figure at this time of year, all insects are interesting! These are the survivors.

File.Name: 9b02a_9b02-061.jpg
Notes:
56139492 I guess it sometimes happens!

I was focused on the Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on the left putting an end to an unfortunate fly (order Diptera). In the 1/320 second of my exposure, a honey bee with loaded pollen-baskets flew through my photo, and here they both are! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

This action is happening on new flowers of (I think) Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain of the river.

I had some extra time this morning thanks to the "Daylight Savings Time" time change. The composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs are just about done, but the Palmer's Goldenbush is in full flower. There's still life at the river!
Locality: Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs are just about done

Notes: 56138957 This spider-like Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera is putting its ice pick-like proboscis to the neck of an unfortunate fly (order Diptera). The action is happening on new composite flowers of (I think) Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae) plant family that's flowering on the dry flood plain of the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b02c_9b02-054.jpg
Notes: 56138619 Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the new flowers of (I think) Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain of the river. The bee has nearly full pollen-baskets on its hind legs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b03a_9b02-072.jpg
Notes:
56200897 Here is another large Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the insect order Hemiptera) working male flowers of a composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) growing by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The females are all done, but the male pollen-bearing plants have new flowers after our early rain three weeks ago. Something is wrong here!

File.Name: 9b04a_9b02-075.jpg
Notes:
56202061 This is probably my favorite predator!

This Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) is prowling among the male (staminate) flowers of a composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) by the river, waiting for prey. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

Names, names, names... The family Phymatidae is now considered to be the subfamily Phymatinae of the family Reduviidae. And the order Hemiptera has now become the suborder Heteroptera of a broader order Hemiptera that includes aphids and cicadas.

I don't really care. The names change, but the critters don't. I'll stick with the names I learned, and let synonymy deal with it!

I missed uploading photos yesterday. We went to visit my Dad in L.A. and got back late.

File.Name: 9b04b_9b02-068.jpg
Notes:
56201287 This Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) is perfectly camouflaged among the male (staminate) flowers of a composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2009)

Look - the ambush bug has picked up some yellow pollen on its claws and mouth to complete the camouflage!

File.Name: 9b05a_9b03-085.jpg
Notes: 56243538 Perfect little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) pausing for a moment while working a new glandular flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. Look close - it's got a forked tongue! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b05b_9b05-098.jpg
Notes:
56243416 Finally! I found this little dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushroom in the woods along a trail near home. Touch it, and it falls apart. Being edible is not an issue with such friable little mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2009)

I've been waiting for mushrooms to appear ever since our drought-busting rain a few weeks ago. I hope this is the first of many.

File.Name: 9b05c_9b03-086.jpg
Notes:
56243198 "Something is happening but you don't know what it is..."

This isn't a very good photo, but it's an interesting moment. This little parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) is ovipositing - laying an egg - on or next to a tiny Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Th aphid looks belly-up. Maybe the wasp has just finished laying its egg inside the aphid? (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2009)

associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 9b06a_9b05-155.jpg
Notes:
56265660 My after-school Hike Club kids are sitting on an emerging sandbar as the tide gets lower and lower at Surf Beach, where the Santa Ynez River meets the ocean. We were skimboarding during the minus low tide yesterday, but the mood turned to a moment of meditation as the sun got low. (Surf Beach, 5 November 2009)

Of course this is the natural light, not a sepia tint.

File.Name: 9b06b_9b05-185.jpg
Notes:
56265359 Here is the rusty train trestle, some old pilings, and a bit of driftwood at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River at Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc. They are catching the perfect light as the sun gets low behind. (Surf Beach, 5 November 2009

I brought my after-school Hike Club kids here yesterday for skimboarding during a minus low tide. Everything was perfect!

File.Name: 9b06c_9b05-112.jpg
Notes: 56265120 A flock of gulls take wing at remote Surf Beach, where the Santa Ynez River meets the ocean in a huge estuary that is protected by the surrounding Vandenberg Air Force Base. I brought my after-school Hike Club kids here yesterday for skimboarding during a minus low tide. Everything was perfect! (Surf Beach, 5 November 2009

File.Name: 9b07a_9b07-014.jpg
Notes:
56297198 This shouldn't be - but here it is...

The Bigberry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are already flowering, and here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the new flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2009)

In past years I've found manzanita flowering in December and January, but never in November. I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain this year.

File.Name: 9b07b_9b07-002.jpg
Notes: 56296919 Gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) in the canyon. This might be the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus). (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2009)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Four-spurred Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus tetracanthus
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus tetracanthus

File.Name: 9b07c_9b05-103.jpg
Notes:
56296765 These critters are so black I assumed they were Beetles (order Coleoptera), but looking at my photo on the screen, I realize they are immature true Bugs (Hemiptera). They are resting on a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2009)

In the upper-left under the bug's antenna is a gall-like folded leaf that contains an insect larva. "Gall-like"? Well, it's not an obvious structure caused by an insect like most galls, but it definitely provides shelter. I guess I'll have to open it to see if it also provides nourishment.

File.Name: 9b08a_9b08-028.jpg
Notes: 56323709 I believe this is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) working a composite flower of native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. That well-placed halo in the background is a dandelion-like seed-head of another flower. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Groundsel; Sunflower

File.Name: 9b08b_9b08-032.jpg
Notes: 56323414 Here is an exposed seed-head of a native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a good anatomy lesson for composite flowers: you can see the pappus of the seeds and a few spent flowers, as well as the receptacle and a few sepal-like phyllaries. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b08c_9b07-021.jpg
Notes: 56322908 Here is the ongoing battle between the ants and the yellow jackets at the hummingbird feeder. Oh yeah - the hummers get some too! I keep moving the feeder around, so it takes the ants awhile to find it again. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b09a_9b08-043.jpg
Notes: 56359760 Here is a beautiful late-season flower of (I think) native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. It may be the Tall Stephanomeria ( S. virgata), but judging by its stature - only a foot tall - it is S. exigua. The flowers start blue like this in the morning, but end up white and fading by early afternoon. The tiny stamens and pistils are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae;  S. virgata; S. exigua
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae;  S. virgata; S. exigua

File.Name: 9b09b_9b08-023.jpg
Notes:
56359356 A Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera trying to get warm in the morning on a spent Zinnia flower in the garden. I believe these are the paper wasps with the long petiole to their abdomen. They are our most common wasps at home. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)

I know, my photo is not quite sharp - it was breezy. It's still pretty cool. Look how the wasp is supporting its abdomen on its wings!

File.Name: 9b09c_9b08-036.jpg
Notes:
56358894 This is the same Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) as my [Previous] photo. It's now resting on a seed head of the same native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family.

The fly is grooming itself. Look close at the front legs... Any contortionist would be proud! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Groundsel; Sunflower

File.Name: 9b10a_9b10-071.jpg
Notes:
56390503 Another pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) - maybe in the family Lygaeidae? This one is perched on a composite flower of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2009)

I went to the Santa Ynez River before school today. It's on the way, only a few miles from home. I live on top of the mountains, and this part of the river is directly below us.

File.Name: 9b10b_9b10-072.jpg
Notes:
56389705 Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the new flowers of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2009)

It's interesting that the pollen in the flowers and on the bee's rump is yellow, but the pollen in its pollen baskets is orange. I have no idea why.

File.Name: 9b10c_9b10-067.jpg
Notes:
56389226 Wild Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) standing in a field of the "bad grass" - Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) - by the Santa Ynez River this morning. Smilo Grass is also known as Millet Mountain Rice and Rice Millet. It's a pretty grass that almost looks native, but it's the worst plant invader in our mountains right now. I've seen it spread hugely near the river and along the roads in the mountains - including our canyon - over the past five years. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2009)

If you live in California, learn this grass and kill it!
CommonNames: Mule Deer; Smilo Grass; Smilo Grass; Millet Mountain Rice; Rice Millet

File.Name: 9b11a_9b10-068.jpg
Notes: 56425829 The flowers of California Brickellbush (Brickellia Californica in the Asteraceae plant family) usually don't look like much, but they're getting a boost from our early rain a month ago. Any flowers are scarce in the woods now, but I can't recall ever seeing insects visiting these late-season flowers. They are kind of pretty. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b11b_9b07-007.jpg
Notes:
56425710 It's strange and disturbing - these caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies are already active on this native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's way too early for them.

These Variable Checkerspots are interesting because they overwinter as caterpillars. Last year I thought it was strange that they were active in January - and here they are in early November! The Manzanita's are also flowering way too early. Is this what global warming looks like locally? (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2009)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; Variable Checkerspots

File.Name: 9b11c_9b10-075.jpg
Notes:
56425536 This is not a great photo, but the fly is so camera shy that I probably won't get a better one. It's a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae in the order Diptera) working new composite flowers of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain by the river. The distinctive fly has whitish "windows" on its abdomen. I've seen the same flies lately on composite flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) and Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrubs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 November 2009)

Checking at BugGuide, this fly might be in the genus Peleteria. See http://bugguide.net/node/view/53639.
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Palmer's Goldenbush; Coyote Bush; Bush Groundsel
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Baccharis pilularis; Senecio douglasii; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Baccharis pilularis; Senecio douglasii; Peleteria

File.Name: 9b12a_9b08-026.jpg
Notes: 56456102 Perfect new flowers of a composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. This shrub is still going strong despite the late season. I guess it got a boost from our unexpected rain a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b12b_9b08-050.jpg
Notes:
56456034 Insects are scarce in the woods, so I was looking for insect galls in the oaks last weekend. This looks and feels like a gall, but it is actually an insect!

I believe this is a Soft Scale Insect (family Coccidae) in the insect order Homoptera. It's living on a twig of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chryolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. Despite the name, it feels hard, like a shell. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b12c_9b08-056.jpg
Notes:
56455844 This "thing" is a total mystery to me - a spider egg sack? a cocoon? a lost toupee? Whatever it is, it's on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2009)

I walk the same trails week after week looking for new photos - and I always find them! That's my first advice for aspiring nature photographers. Find a trail you love, and walk it at least every week. You will find what's new in the woods!

File.Name: 9b13a_9b12-110.jpg
Notes:
56486268 Here is Treebeard himself with my after-school Hike Club kids at the fern-shrouded mouth of the secret cave near the second crossing of Paradise.

I take our after-school kids exploring in the local mountains every Thursday when the busy school schedule allows. I captured this photo using my camera's self-timer mode: push the button and run like hell to get in the photo! My video-buddy Nick Vincent is lying in the ferns shooting video of the photograph. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b13c_9b12-099.jpg
Notes:
56485673 There are real pipe-stem stalactites in this secret cave near the the second crossing of Paradise!

That sounds like a bit of final days/rapture doggerel, but I only mean it's near the second place where the road crosses the Santa Ynez River in the area known as Paradise.

It's a real "live" cave, with water dripping through the massive sandstone rock above and making little pipe-stem stalactites inside. It's not limestone, though there is plenty of calcium in the rock. I used flash for this photo - it's dark in this cave! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b14a_9b14-004.jpg
Notes:
56514313 The same mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) is still watching out for her young on a long-spent plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. She's fading into autumn colors. I guess she'll guard them until she dies. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2009)

She's been tending her babies on the same perch at least since September 12 when I first got a photo of her guarding her nest. That's real dedication and a strong mothering instinct.
