File.Name: 9b15a_9b15-031.jpg
Notes:
56541746 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

The BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) plant family are already starting to flower! Usually we get a mass flowering in February. Some years they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. I have one early record of flowers from December. But what are they doing flowering now in mid-November? (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2009)

A number of significant native plants are flowering very early this year. I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain this year. There are few insects working the flowers except for Honey Bees. Will they flower again when they "should" flower? I wonder - is this what global warming looks like locally?

File.Name: 9b15b_9b15-015.jpg
Notes:
56541110 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

I found these catkin-flowers on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family today. They normally flower in March or April, though I have one record of a tree flowering in December - but never in November. I guess the trees are confused by our great October rain this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b15c_9b08-064.jpg
Notes:
56540308 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

The Bigberry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are already flowering. In past years I've found manzanita flowering in December and January, but never in November. I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain this year - but what will the hummingbirds do in January? (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2009)

This photo is doubly interesting, as the flowers seem to have a swollen gall at the base of the pedicels. My gall book doesn't describe this.

File.Name: 9b16a_9b15-042.jpg
Notes:
56577044 The beautiful late-season flowers of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the composite Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family are getting a second wind this year after our October rain. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2009)

Look close - the anthers joined at the tips into little loops are interesting. Look even closer - see a hoard of tiny orange insect Thrips (order Thysanoptera) trying to hide from my camera. A moment later and no thrips were visible. Talk about camera-shy!

File.Name: 9b17a_9b16-061.jpg
Notes:
56608427 Here is a pretty little nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. You can see its mighty sword-like proboscis. It looks like it's dancing on this leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2009)

I'm not sure why the blackberry leaf looks so white - something to do with shooting into the morning sun.

File.Name: 9b17b_9b16-059.jpg
Notes:
56608071 Here is a rather large Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the insect order Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2009)

It was aware of me. It kept ducking under the leaf, but emerging after a few minutes. It was just a matter of time...

File.Name: 9b17c_9b15-048.jpg
Notes:
56607829 Nano said of my [Previous] photo that "Thrips are notoriously difficult to photograph" - but it's not because they are so tiny, but because they are so darn active and camera shy! A moment before I took this photo, there were 15-20 of the little critters in plain view. I reckon they are all hiding inside the flower!

This is a 500x500 crop of my original 4288x2848 pixel photo of this composite Cliff Aster (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia) flower. You can see two of the tiny orange Thrip insects (order Thysanoptera) below the brown stamen-sheaths of the flower. These are the ones that didn't get away in time. They're kind of cute! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b18a_9b16-052.jpg
Notes:
56638538 I believe this is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus in the Rosaceae plant family) not too far from our compost pile. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2009)

I'll bet these flies are just waiting for the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) to appear. (I'm waiting too!)

Today's photos are all flies - and fly-like flies at that. But there are many different kinds of flies with different habits when you look close. I'll probably do my annual DMS science fair project on families of local flies this year. (I'm not teaching science any more, but I still want to do a project!)

File.Name: 9b19a_9b19-081.jpg
Notes:
56670114 I noticed this Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera trying to warm up in a sunny place in the garden this morning. It's perched on a new leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2009)

Look closer at the nifty way that it's holding its abdomen up in the air with its wings and hind-legs! I guess that helps it warm up?

I got the photo "this morning" - hah, it was about 10:30 AM. That's when we get the first sun at our house as winter approaches on the shady north slopes of the mountains. It's good for mushrooms though, when they finally appear.

Blah - I'm getting sick with a virus. Time for bed.

Notes:
56697377 I captured this Yellow Jacket-type wasp in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera at the hummingbird feeder when I got home from school early today. Because of surface tension, water is not so liquid for these little critters! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2009)

Blah - I'm still down with a virus, but getting better. I'll be ready for my usual walk in the woods tomorrow, and even better for Thanksgiving vacation all next week!

File.Name: 9b20b_9b15-009.jpg
Notes:
56696806 This is an old spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera that I just noticed. The gall looks like a little mushroom growing from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2009)

But that's not the whole story. These wasps have a complicated life-cycle. Parthenogenetic females emerge from the previous season's galls in spring and oviposit on the oak leaves to produce these spring galls. These "monothalamous" (containing one larva) galls hatch in early summer and produce a crop of males and females that mate and produce a totally different kind of "polythalamnous" (containing many larvae) summer galls. The galls develop as "oak apples" and parthenogenic females emerge the following spring to repeat the cycle.

The only photo of the summer galls that I seem to have posted is this one:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/1804154

Galls are complicated, but they are interesting because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal. I'll be doing a public lecture (with my photos) on plant galls for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society sometime next year. Expect lots of gall photos until then!

File.Name: 9b21a_9b21-034.jpg
Notes:
56730865 Just in time for Thanksgiving!

Here is a new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden. The mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do! They get their common name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2009)

We didn't get any rain to speak of from the front that moved through last night, but we did get new mushrooms! At least I noticed the new mushrooms.

It takes a different mindset to photograph mushrooms rather than insects. Insects are fast and live in the light. Mushrooms grow in the shade and require long exposures. I'm remembering how to do it!

File.Name: 9b21b_9b21-023.jpg
Notes:
56730382 I believe this is a Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in the Amanita (Amanitaceae) family - though I don't rule out A. pantherina. Either way it is probably poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria - only more so. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2009)

Update (22 November 2009) - I think I'm wrong. I now think this is really part of the confusing hybrid swarm between A. pantherina and A. gemmata. See my discussion at:

File.Name: 9b21c_9b21-012.jpg
Notes:
56730165 I believe this is a real King Bolete (Boletus edulis mushroom - aka Le Cep, Steinpilz, Porcini, etc. - that I found in the woods today! It has a reticulate stem. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees with Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2009)

If I'm right, this is one of the world's best tasting mushrooms. If I'm wrong, well, I can't be too wrong - it's probably still pretty good! I didn't collect it. I'll go back tomorrow!

File.Name: 9b22a_9b22-046.jpg
Notes:
56758160 These voluptuous mushrooms remind me most of neolithic "venus" figurines!

This is a young "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom that I found in the woods today. What amazing colors! That's the cap sitting on top of the bulbous base. You can see some of the red spore-boring pore surface below the cap. They get quite large - a foot (25 cm) or more across. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2009)

As the species name suggests, this mushroom is not edible. It causes vomiting, diarrhea, and severe cramps. But it sure is impressive (and not dangerous) to look at!

File.Name: 9b22b_9b22-052.jpg
Notes:
56757299 This might be real Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom - at least it's not so influenced by A. gemmata as my [Previous] photo. My only hesitation is that the stalk is a bit scaly above the ring. This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2009)

This mushroom is poisonous! It contains the same toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. Large doses can be fatal!

See my [Previous] photo for more discussion. Mushroom taxonomy drives me crazy - but it keeps things interesting!

File.Name: 9b22c_9b21-022.jpg
Notes:
56756640 This is the same mushroom I called the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in the Amanita (Amanitaceae) family in yesterday's photo - but I'm wrong. I think it's really part of the confusing hybrid swarm between A. pantherina and A. gemmata. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2009)

True A. gemmata is the bright yellow mushroom I sometimes find under the nearby Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family). See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/45698496

I had a twinge of memory when I took my [Next] photo of a similar mushroom that is not so influenced by A. gemmata. David Arora (author of my textbook, Mushrooms Demystified, says that "a confusing series of "hybrids" exist whose cap color and degree of toxicity are intermediate between the two species."

Whatever it is, it's probably poisonous! Assume that it contains the same with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria - only more so.

File.Name: 9b23a_9b23-068.jpg
Notes:
56791033 I found this dead Bobcat (Lynx rufus in the Felidae mammal family) along a little-used trail today. It's a wildcat, not a house cat. It's probably about 30 inches (75 cm) long. It looks so peaceful lying there - I wonder how it died? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2009)

I thought about whether I should post this photo since it looks so much like a tabby cat - but I figure my job is to document what I find in woods, and here it is.

File.Name: 9b23b_9b23-101.jpg
Notes:
56790171 Beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden. You can see the ring or "annulus" on the stems of the mushrooms that happens when the "partial veil" that initially covers the gills tears free. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2009)

Some mushrooms also have a "universal veil" that initially wraps the whole mushroom up like a cocoon and leaves fragments on the cap or the base of the stem - but these mushrooms lack that structural feature.

These mushrooms are edible and tasty - just in time for Thanksgiving dinner!

File.Name: 9b23c_9b23-088.jpg
Notes:
56789495 Like I said in yesterday's comments, these voluptuous mushrooms remind me most of neolithic "venus" figurines!

This is another young "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom that I found in the woods today. That's the cap sitting on top of the bulbous base. You can see some of the red spore-boring pore surface below the cap. The "bulb" of the mushroom looks quite different from yesterday's photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2009)

This mushroom is growing in sandy soil and it's covered with sand.

Notes:
56821410 This little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) seems frozen by the cold on a late-season flower of (I think) native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (It might be S. virgata, but it is less than one foot tall.) I took several photos of this bee and I can see it slowly moving. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

The subject is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) perched high in an oak tree. I got this photo from our upstairs porch from about 20 feet away. I guess these dragonflies are migratory? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b24c_9b21-007.jpg
Notes:
56819333 I realized that I've seen this same Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - in the same place in the same oak tree for a couple of weeks now. It's odd, because the normally white trim on it's thorax is now orange.

I see the problem. This ladybug has been captured, probably by a spider - see the egg case underneath it! I didn't know ladybugs have any predators. This doesn't explain the color change though! (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2009)

The ladybugs are not congregating any more, by the way. They disappeared the day after my last photo, a month ago. I don't know where they've gone.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55640438

File.Name: 9b25a_9b23-074.jpg
Notes:
56851851 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

These beautiful urn-like flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are already almost done flowering. The flowers are two months early this year, and it's been a pretty half-hearted flowering. What will happen to the insects and hummingbirds in January when the plants should flower? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2009)

I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain in this drought year, but these plants are now out of sync with their environment. I'm disturbed because many plants are flowering early this year. Is this what global warming looks like locally?

File.Name: 9b25b_9b24-140.jpg
Notes:
56851346 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

These are new male pollen-bearing staminate catkins of a Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. They must be wind-pollinated? This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. It is a month or two ahead of schedule according to my notes. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b25c_9b25-160.jpg
Notes:
56850682 Something strange and disturbing is happening in our woods. As a naturalist, I figure it's my job to "document the flora" - so here it is.

I found this Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae plant family) tree flowering today down by the river. Most of these grand deciduous trees haven't even dropped their leaves yet. This wasn't just an odd branch - the whole tree was flowering! Usually they don't flower until March, so this is more than 3 months early. Strange and disturbing! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 November 2009)

I guess the trees are confused by our great October rain this in drought year. I searched the catkins for the gall wasps that inhabit the flowers, but nada. This tree is out of sync with it's environment. I'm disturbed because many plants are flowering early this year. Is this what global warming looks like locally?

File.Name: 9b26a_9b24-145.jpg
Notes:
56886172 Here is a small harvest of the dry nuts inside the fruit of the native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family growing in the canyon. Gotta leave some for the squirrels! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so - and the fruit are even more so than the leaves. The secret is to roast the nuts. I'll try it and report back here. It's been 30 years since I prepared the nuts for the table, and it's time to refresh my memory!

I found a video on how to prepare bay nuts on YouTube:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=igb8UD-TtC4

I have a stumper about such forgotten foods. It mentions acorns, but it could just as well be about bay nuts:

"Did you notice that it's the peak of acorn harvest time here in central California? The birds know! It's remarkable that these same acorns that clutter our patios were once the "staff of life" for the Chumash Native Americans in these parts. What other forgotten foods can you find, either local or global? I'm most interested in those unknown foods that are not only edible and delicious, but were once the staple foods that fed whole civilizations, even though the harvest is now ignored and forgotten. Raw acorns taste bitter, so the challenge is to prepare something delicious using acorns for your Thanksgiving feast!"

Read my stumper (and answer) at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/22nov02.html

File.Name: 9b26b_9b24-119.jpg
Notes: 56885761 Despite the drought this year, the California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family are producing a bumper crop of fruit! The fruit resemble little avocado "pups" - and they are indeed related. Hidden inside the fruit is a real nut. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b26c_9b24-120.jpg
Notes:
56885578 There already are new flowers on the California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family growing in the mountains. This plant thus joins the growing list of plants that I've found flowering very early this year. According to my notes, they usually flower in January or February. I have one record from December 20. But here they are in November! It's strange and disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain in this drought year, but these plants are now out of sync with their environment. I'm disturbed because so many plants are flowering early this year. Is this what global warming looks like locally?

File.Name: 9b27a_9b27-176.jpg
Notes:
56910585 New Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) showing the odd cracks they get from the dry weather lately. They are growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2009)

There's a 50% chance of rain tonight and tomorrow! It feels like rain with wind and clouds. We need rain soon to keep the grasses and wildflowers alive that sprouted early after our October rain!

File.Name: 9b27b_9b27-183.jpg
Notes: 56909799 This is a new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden today. The mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do! They get their common name from the color, not the taste. They are catching a rare moment of sunlight in our winter woods. The mushrooms look like hot cross buns! (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b27c_9b22-043.jpg
Notes:
56909273 Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms - though in this case it's true!

This is a young "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom that I found in the woods a few days ago. These mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle then, but they are not that other notorious blue-staining mushroom. It's fun to leave messages for other mushroom collectors to find! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2009)

As the species name suggests, you really don't want to eat this mushroom - it causes vomiting, diarrhea, and severe cramps.

File.Name: 9b28a_9b28-017.jpg
Notes:
56940048 Here is the head and most of the thorax of a cold but beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) trying to warm up in the sun this morning in our canyon. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

I've see them here before at this time of year. See my photos from last year on December 7, 2008 taken in almost the same place: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/44454306

That dragonfly seems much more violet-colored than this one. Maybe it's a white-balance thing?

File.Name: 9b28b_9b28-014.jpg
Notes:
56939809 Usually I can't resolve the individual simple-eyes or "ommatidia" that make up the large compound eyes of dragonflies like a retina, but here they are - many thousands of them!

The subject is a cold Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) trying to warm up in the sun by the creek in our canyon. It worked. A few moments later, and it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b28c_9b28-006.jpg
Notes:
56939421 Look close! Can you see the dragonfly hanging from the native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family trying to warm up on a cold morning? It's perched floor-to-ceiling about 1/3 of the way in from the right, but it's pretty well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

I'm pretty sure this is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata). I think they migrate down from the north and overwinter here.

I've see them before at this time of year. See my photos from last year on December 7, 2008:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/44454306
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata

File.Name: 9b29a_9b28-030.jpg
Notes:
56969291 This is a beautiful young Amanita rubescens mushroom - "The Blusher" - fruiting early under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. My book (David Arora, Mushrooms Demystified, 1986) says that they usually fruit twice - a small flush after the first fall rains, and a larger crop in late winter and spring. That seems right.

Here you can see remnants of the universal veil as warts on the cap, and the partial veil that protects the gills has just about torn free to form a ring on the stem. The cap gets reddish stains with age and handling that are barely visible. I'll post a photo of the whole mushroom tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

I go back to school tomorrow after a week of vacation. Even without academic classes, I feel a bit jittery.

I'll try to re-open my comments today. Please don't send me invitations to be your Fiend/Favorite or cute Ascii art because I'll just delete them. But real comments on my photos will be appreciated!

File.Name: 9b29b_9b28-037.jpg
Notes:
56968153 This is a double-headed King Bolete (Boletus edulis mushroom - aka Le Cep, Steinpilz, Porcini, etc. - that I found in the woods yesterday! The dark yellow stain at the base of the stem is probably a fungal parasite already beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

I've really got to start carrying a mushroom basket on my photo hikes, but I don't like the extra encumbrance - camera and tripod are enough. I had to leave this fine delicious edible mushroom in the woods. No problem - I'd rather photograph them!

File.Name: 9b29c_9b28-016.jpg
Notes:
56967546 "You talkin' to me?"

Suddenly, the Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) in my [Previous] photos turned his head and looked right at me. I guess it was grooming and/or preparing for liftoff. A moment later and it flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

File.Name: 9b30a_9b25-168.jpg
Notes:
57002434 This is a beautiful new flower of a native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. It looks like a studio shot, but I got this photo in the woods "au naturel".

It seems unusual to be flowering now, but my flora gives their flowering time as June to November. Maybe it got a boost from our great October rain? The flowers are doing better than the insects this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 November 2009)

I'll try to re-open my comments. Please don't send me invitations to be your Fiend/Favorite or cute Ascii art because I'll just delete them. But real comments on my photos will be appreciated!

File.Name: 9b30b_9b30-060.jpg
Notes:
57001836 This is a new button-size "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera) growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. There are critters living in there! It looks so surprisingly lush and succulent on the bare oak branch. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2009)

I'm confused about when these "oak apple" galls should start growing. My book says spring, but here it is on the last day of November. I've found new galls in December. I guess they start when they feel like starting!

Galls are the "tumor-like growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in response to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - but why are they so darned beautiful?

Actually, it's been a poor year for insect galls in our mountains. It's not that the galls have failed in this drought summer, but that the wasps that make them have failed. Many insects put all their eggs into one basket, so an unusually dry year like this one hits them hard.
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 9b30c_9b28-028.jpg
Notes: 57000838 Here is the whole mushroom of "The Blusher" - Amanita rubescens - that I showed a detail of yesterday. If you know your amanitas, it might seem strange that it has no volva - the "death cup" at the base - but in this species the friable volva soon wears away. It is said to be edible when cooked (not raw), but why bother confusing it with a deadly poisonous white amanita when there are other tasty boletes in the woods? (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c01a_9b21-008.jpg
Notes:
57034592 Here's an update on the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) in the canyon - she seems to have a new egg sack, so she's ready to do it all again! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

This mama spider has been sitting on the same plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae family since at least September 12 when I first got a photo of her guarding her nest. http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54520745

And now she's doing it all again. Wow - that takes real dedication and a strong mothering instinct. Her colors have faded, and I'm not surprised!
Locality:
Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae family since at least September 12 when I first got a photo of her guarding her nest. http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/54520745

And now she's doing it all again. Wow - that takes real dedication and a strong mothering instinct. Her colors have faded

File.Name: 9c01b_9b29-057.jpg
Notes:
57033936 Beautiful new flower head of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) flowers in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the mountains near home. This paintbrush has woolly-white leaves, unlike other local species. The showy red "petals" are really leafy bracts. The real flowers are the greenish tubes that stick out from the red-tipped bracts and sepals. The flowers really are this brilliant. I did no saturation correction to this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2009)

This is another native plant that is flowering disturbingly early this year. I usually find these flowers in January or February. I have one early record from December 24. But here they are in November!

File.Name: 9c01c_9b25-166.jpg
Notes: 57033308 This is no one's favorite flower - "Russian Thistle", Salsola tragus in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. The flowers really don't look much like flowers at all, but they have their charms. The plants dry out and blow in the wind, piling up as tumbleweeds along fences. They have tumbled across our whole country, spreading seeds all the way! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c02a_9c02-094.jpg
Notes:
57066616 Here is a brave middle-school student with a handful of California Tarantula (Aphonopelma sp.) spider! They've been keeping it as a pet in my old science classroom. It feels like a whisper moving across your hand! (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 December 2009)

We all know that spiders have 8 legs. You can see that these great Hairy Mygalomorph (Theraphosidae) spiders also have a pair of prominent leg-like pedipalps and a pair of furry chelicera that hide their fangs. They can bite, but they rarely do so. It is safe to hold them.

I'm still teaching fun electives like guitar and woodshop and drafting, but I'm officially retired from teaching science at Dunn Middle School. Yahoo! It's still fun to drop by the classrooms in the morning to see what's up and take a few photos.

Some of my school photos are posted most weeks as "Newsnote Photos" at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/electronlinda
Locality: Theraphosidae) spiders also have a pair of prominent leg-like pedipalps and a pair of furry chelicera that hide their fangs. They can bite

File.Name: 9c02b_9b24-130.jpg
Notes:
57065584 This is another plant that shouldn't be flowering this early in the season, but here it is. This is Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae) plant family growing in the mountains. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2009)

I have one record in my notes of flowers on December 24 - but these plants usually flower in February and March. I guess the plants are confused by our great October rain in this drought year, but they are now out of sync with their environment. There are few insects available to jiggle their stamens!

File.Name: 9c02c_9b22-047.jpg
Notes: 57065007 This is another plant that shouldn't be flowering this early in the season, but here it is. This is Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the in the Ericaceae plant family. But I have one record in my notes of early flowers from December 3, 2005, so I guess it's not totally crazy. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c03a_9b25-163.jpg
Notes:
57098172 A new flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family on a dry flat by the river. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. Note the little flies at the throat of the huge flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 November 2009)

The plant is related to the infamous Jimson Weed (D. stramonium) that grows across the country. Current opinion is that it's not really native, but was introduced from Mexico long ago by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you!
Locality: D. stramonium) that grows across the country. Current opinion is that it's not really native

File.Name: 9c03b_9b21-017.jpg
Notes: 57097854 These beautiful red berries of a Madrone tree (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are edible, but kind of mealy. The birds like them more than I do. They have an interesting bumpy texture. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c03c_9b28-050.jpg
Notes: 57097602 I found this "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom along the road in the woods. It has impressive bulk - the cap is about 10 inches (25 cm) across. These bolete mushroom have a spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills. The red color of the pores means this mushroom is NOT edible! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c04a_9c03-177.jpg
Notes:
57126516 This photo shows gulls taking wing (and their reflections) as the sun goes down over the sand flats exposed by the super-low -1.5 low tide at Surf Beach yesterday, out past the town of Lompoc. I'd hate to correct the "white balance" and make the fabulous color go away!

I can't quite say this is the color that I saw, but this is what my camera saw - shooting into the sun and 3.7 stops over-exposed. I did no color correction. (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c04b_9c03-146.jpg
Notes: 57126147 One of my after-school Hike Club boys chasing seagulls at Surf Beach - what could be better? (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c04c_9c03-130.jpg
Notes:
57125992 This is the view underneath the rusty train trestle over the river estuary at Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc. I took my after-school Hike Club kids to the beach for the super-low -1.5 ft. low tide yesterday. See those boards invitingly laid out to walk on? Of course we went for it! (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

I have a photo of some of the bridge at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56265359

File.Name: 9c05a_9c03-185.jpg
Notes:
57154033 So that's why they call them sea dogs!

Here is the skull (and attached body) of a Seal or Sea Lion that we found on remote Surf Beach out beyond Lompoc. I wonder how it ended up here? (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

Sure, I got new photos today. But there's a storm coming! I'll save my new photos.

BTW that's the trestle we were underneath in yesterday's photo!

File.Name: 9c05b_9c03-155.jpg
Notes: 57153745 Sand dunes advancing on the estuary at Surf Beach out beyond Lompoc. Beautiful view, but the bad news is the introduced Ice Plant (Carpobrotus edulis in the Aizoaceae plant family) that dominates the dunes. It was introduced here deliberately to stabilize the dunes and protect the railway. What a mistake. (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c05c_9c03-182.jpg
Notes:
57153245 Strange footprint in the sand at Surf Beach! It's 4 or 5 inches (10-13 cm) across. It looks like a butterfly, but it isn't. (Surf Beach, 3 December 2009)

I'll make this a stumper and give the answer tomorrow!

File.Name: 9c06a_9c06-027.jpg
Notes:
57177906 Exquisite mandala of a late-season native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the woods today. They are having a good year. Our great October rain was a boost for them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2009)

I think that yellow is the hardest color to photograph, especially when the white balance is funky. I took this photo in the shade on an overcast day in the breeze.

It feels like a real storm moving in! There's a wind from the south and a cloud cap on the mountains. The temperature is falling. We might get snow tomorrow!

File.Name: 9c06b_9c05-013.jpg
Notes:
57177077 A perfect King Bolete (Boletus edulis mushroom - aka Le Cep, Steinpilz, Porcini, etc. - pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods. (That's the mound of leaves the mushrooms push up from down below.)

Well, it's not quite perfect, because another fungus has already laid claim - look at the yellow stains climbing up the stem. You can see that this mushroom still has white pores, though they turn yellow with age. They are delicious - if you get them before the invading fungi! We once had Boletes and Chanterelles on the same plate, and the Boletes easily won. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c06c_9c06-031.jpg
Notes: 57176424 I found clusters of these "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) in the woods today growing from dead oak wood. I guess they are Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila) in the Coprinaceae mushroom family. They are probably not edible, but who cares because they are so small and insubstantial. The gills are gray, only becoming black as the mushroom turns to mush. The caps are "hygrophanous" - which means they change color as they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c07a_9c05-023.jpg
Notes:
57215117 The Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) just keep on coming! Here is a large cluster of new mushrooms growing from an old stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden. The mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do! They get their common name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

The very young mushrooms are darker colored. You can see a few of them in the lower-left of my photo.

We got about 2.5 inches of much-needed rain today. This is our first rain since October. The mushrooms will be happy - they've been fruiting in crunchy-dry woods!

File.Name: 9c07b_9c05-016.jpg
Notes:
57214565 Here are new Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from buried wood in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with delicious chanterelle mushrooms, but I suppose anything is possible. DON'T confuse them with chanterelles! Both mushrooms are orange and sort of trumpet-shaped, but these are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge, and they always grow from wood, sometimes (like this) buried. But I've been fooled before - just for a moment!

Go on a mushroom walk with someone who knows. You won't confuse them after you've seen them both. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen it here.
Locality:
like this) buried. But I've been fooled before - just for a moment!

Go on a mushroom walk with someone who knows. You won't confuse them after you've seen them both. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark

File.Name: 9c07c_9c05-017.jpg
Notes:
57213942 Here's why wild mushrooms are so expensive to buy in the store!

Do you see the perfect King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom - aka Le Cep, Steinpilz, Porcini, etc. - in this photo? I almost missed it too, but look again in the center of the photo! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

Mushrooms come from the earth, pushing up dirt and leaves from below as they emerge. They are hard to find!

File.Name: 9c08a_9c08-040.jpg
Notes:
57246282 It was cold last night! This glass filled with rain water from yesterday's rain storm was outside, and somehow grew this strange finger of ice when it froze solid overnight. I call it an "upsicle"! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2009)

I checked at about 6 AM, and it was 20 degrees Fahrenheit (-7 Celsius). That's cold for us here in sunny Santa Barbara. The frost stayed on the ground all day where the sun doesn't shine - and that's most of the garden at this time of year.

It was actually cold enough to freeze our propane. We had to pour hot water over the regulator this morning to restore the gas flow. I guess it was water condensation in the regulator?

File.Name: 9c08b_9c05-009.jpg
Notes:
57245499 Here are a pair of Russula mushrooms in the woods - maybe Russula cremoricolor? They resemble R. emetica except that they have pale yellow-white rather than bright red caps. They share the acrid (hot peppery) taste. Sure, I nibbled a small piece - but they are probably poisonous if you eat more than a little. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

These mushrooms are "chalk caps". I guess I tripped over the one 'shroom, and it snapped clean, like a piece of chalk. It's a good field test.

File.Name: 9c08c_9c05-021.jpg
Notes:
57244874 These are new caps of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing near yesterday's cluster. The new caps look quite different from the mature caps - they are rounder and darker gray rather than honey-brown colored. They're not particularly elegant, but they are common right now. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

These mushrooms are growing from an old stump of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden. They are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do! They get their common name from the color, not the taste.
Locality: Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of our garden. They are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do! They get their common name from the color

File.Name: 9c09a_9b22-054.jpg
Notes:
57307054 Strange galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. They are growing on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like cotton candy gone bad! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

Galls are the "tumor-like growths of plant tissue produced by a host plant in response to chemical and/or mechanical stimuli of invading organisms" - but why are they so darned beautiful?

I was wiped out after a long Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board of directors meeting last night, so I didn't post. I'll make up for it today by posting some gall photos from the archive. I'll be doing a presentation on plant galls for the society in June. (Sorry Nano!)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 9c09b_9b08-049.jpg
Notes:
57306421 I think this is the gall of a Beaked Spindle Gall Wasp (Heteroecus pacificus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing from a Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chryolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family - which usually don't form many galls, at least in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

Remember that oaks have acorns. Any fruit-like body growing from an oak that's not an acorn must be a gall!
CommonNames: Beaked Spindle Gall Wasp; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Heteroecus pacificus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Fagaceae chryolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroecus pacificus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Fagaceae chryolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: 9c09c_9b24-135.jpg
Notes: 57305969 These are unknown furry insect galls on the leaves of a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They were on both sides of the leaves. I guess these galls are caused by Cynipid wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, though they are not listed in my fairly comprehensive book on plant galls. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

File.Name: 9c10a_9c06-034.jpg
Notes:
57310908 What a surprise to find such a luscious, succulent growth inside a scratchy old Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family)!

These are new "polythalamous" (containing many larvae) galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2009)

This species of wasp is only known from parthenogenic females. Many other gall wasps alternate unisexual and bisexual generations. (See my [Previous] photos.) As a male, I hate to think these wasps have found the final solution!

About that "upsicle" of ice - Sorry Gary, there was no twig or straw in the glass, and no drip from above. It's a complete mystery to me!

File.Name: 9c10b_9c06-037.jpg
Notes:
57310095 Here is the gall of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family). The wasp once inside has made a perfectly centered exit hole for its escape. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2009)

This is another little wasp with a complicated life-cycle. This is the unisexual summer gall of these wasps. It is "monothalamous" (containing one larva). I guess the bisexual generation has already emerged to produce a crop of less conspicuous bud galls. See my [Previous} photo for more discussion.
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: 9c10c_8b08-053.jpg
Notes:
57309284 This is the summer gall that goes with the spring gall that I posted a couple of weeks ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56696806

It's the gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They're not as common as the "oak apples" on Scrub or Valley Oaks. They look more gnarly, and they're more complicated. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2009)

These wasps have a complicated life-cycle. Male and female wasps mate and form these "polythalamous" (containing many larvae) summer galls that develop as "oak apples". A crop of parthenogenic females will emerge next spring. The females (with no help from males) will oviposit on the oak leaves to produce "monothalamous" (containing one larva) spring galls that look completely different. Male and female wasps will hatch from these galls in early summer next year to mate and produce a another crop of summer galls on the oak branches. And the cycle repeats.

Galls are interesting like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: 9c11a_9c09-041.jpg
Notes:
57342979 I went to the Santa Ynez River on Wednesday morning to look for fall color photos. I found them!

These are brilliant Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) trees in the Salicaceae plant family, with a few Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees tucked in. My beloved San Marcos Pass is off to the right in this photo, on top of the mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 December 2009)

This is a wet place in the river (at the first crossing), but the river is still mostly dry. The rains that we've been having this week will help. We had rain last weekend, more rain today, and a big storm is expected tomorrow night.

It's good weather for mushrooms!

File.Name: 9c11b_9c09-069.jpg
Notes: 57342592 These are Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) trees in the Salicaceae plant family growing along the river near the end of the road. The leaves are mostly gone, but you can see clusters of Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) growing from the bare branches. That's Camuesa Mountain in the background. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c11c_9c09-068.jpg
Notes:
57341960 I went to the Santa Ynez River on Wednesday morning to look for fall color photos. I found them!

These are brilliant Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family growing along the river near the end of the road. The still-green trees are deciduous White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia). They'll be next. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c12a_9c11-164.jpg
Notes:
57369304 Here is the "Carpe Diem" tree at my school. "Seize the day!" This grand tree has seized many days! It's a deciduous Valley Oak, Quercus lobata tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaves are showing as much fall color as they ever get - not much. (Los Olivos, Santa Ynez Valley, 11 December 2009)

Note the clouds hanging over the mountains in the background. I live up there, about 20 miles to the east, but the clouds are the same at home. One winter storm has passed and another is upon us!

It rained (or drizzled) all day today. We have about 2-1/2 inches in my coffee-can rain gauge so far. I never got outside with my camera today, but this is great weather for mushrooms. Maybe tomorrow...
Locality: or drizzled) all day today.  We have about 2-1/2 inches in my coffee-can rain gauge so far.  I never got outside with my camera today

File.Name: 9c12b_9c09-053.jpg
Notes: 57368542 Perfect reflections of the rocks at our favorite swimming hole along the Santa Ynez River. It's way too cold for swimming now! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c12c_9c09-060.jpg
Notes: 57368371 Small Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees making a reflection in a pool at the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c13a_9c13-020.jpg
Notes:
57397421 Here is the only arthropod (insects, arachnids, etc.) that I captured on my walk in the almost-winter woods today. It's a furry little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I tried to get closer and it jumped away. I wonder what its prey is? (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

We had another 3 inches of rain this weekend and more earlier in the week. It's good weather for mushrooms!

----

About my "upsickle" photo last Tuesday - it's an "ice spike". There's info about the phenomena here:
   http://www.its.caltech.edu/~atomic/snowcrystals/icespikes/icespikes.htm and
   http://www.physics.utoronto.ca/~smorris/edl/icespikes/icespikes.html.

File.Name: 9c13b_9c13-051.jpg
Notes:
57396473 Maybe this is Russula cyanoxantha which is said to be edible and sweet? And maybe it's some other Russula that is spicy hot and poisonous if you eat more than a nibble! I don't really care. I'd rather collect the wild mushrooms from our woods with my camera than put them on my plate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

I'm very conservative with the mushrooms I collect for the table. Chanterelles of course, honey mushrooms, an occasional agaricus, and a few special boletes are about it. But I sure do enjoy finding and photographing them!

File.Name: 9c13c_9c13-064.jpg
Notes:
57395542 Strange Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" emerging from the ground with it's spore sack intact. It will soon lose the spore case and leave its strange starfish-like rind in the woods for me to find and wonder about months later. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

It's really a kind of double-skinned puffball. The outer tough rind opens in the rain (and closes when it's dry!) exposing the inner sack of spores. These odd mushrooms seem to come from nowhere. I find them scattered on the ground with no sign that they came up from the earth.

File.Name: 9c14a_9c13-034.jpg
Notes:
57431785 This is certainly an Amanita mushroom in the woods, but what kind? It has a yellowish cap, striate gills, a membranous partial veil forming a ring on the stem, and a long slender stalk with a fraible, indistinct, white volva at the base. (See my [Next] photo tomorrow.)

I guess it's another of the "hybrid swarm" between Amanita pantherina and Amanita gemmata. Both species grow nearby under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Yikes - as if mushroom IDs weren't hard enough, we also have to consider hybridizing??

This fine 'shroom is growing with the Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum in a pristine part of our canyon that I call the Mushroom Woods. It's probably not edible, and may be quite poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

I collected photos of 19 different kinds of interesting mushrooms in the woods yesterday. And mushroom season is just beginning!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Gallium californicum
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Gallium californicum

File.Name: 9c14b_9c13-037.jpg
Notes: 57430732 Beautiful Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. I hear they are edible, but only the caps are tender enough to eat - and they're pretty thin. I consider it starvation-food. They are pretty though, and they will become very common in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c14c_9c13-030.jpg
Notes: 57430225 It's a "JAR" - "Just Another Russula" - as my mushroom book puts it. I guess this is a White Russula (Russula albidula or kin). It's quite slimy when fresh after the rain. These are certainly our most common mushrooms in the woods right now. It has an "acrid" taste - hot like wasabi! I like to nibble, but they are probably not edible in large portions. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c15a_9c13-019.jpg
Notes: 57463845 I believe these are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms in the woods. The cap is usually zoned concentrically with yellow and orange bands, and the stalk is usually "scrobiculate" - with darker or lighter spots. These mushrooms contain white latex that appears when you break the gills and then stains them yellowish. The taste is very acrid - peppery hot, like wasabi. I like to nibble on them, but they are definitely not edible in quantity. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c15b_9c13-025.jpg
Notes: 57463291 This is another "JAR" - "Just Another Russula" - as my mushroom book puts it. I guess from the rosy colors of its cap that this mushroom is in the "Russula maculata" group. Sounds good, but that's really not saying much ID-wise. It has a mild taste, unlike some of its cousins that taste peppery-hot. Here it is coming up in the woods with a "mushrump" cap of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c15c_9c13-035.jpg
Notes:
57462397 This is another view of the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I guess it is an intermediate between Amanita gemmata and Amanita pantherina? Both species grow nearby under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

Here you can see the friable volva that breaks into scales and almost disappears. The cap is (I'm guessing) about 3 inches across, which makes the long stem about 8 inches long. It's growing with Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum). This mushroom is definitely not edible!

Trying to identify mushrooms by color and form often seems like trying to identify clouds by their shapes!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Gallium californicum
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Gallium californicum

File.Name: 9c16a_9c16-110.jpg
Notes:
57496252 I'm pretty sure this is a rusty-brown Cortinarius fulmineus or kin mushroom in the woods by the creek today. It's coming up through a mass of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) leaves. You can see the yellow/orange-stained hairs on the stem that brand this species as a Cortinarius. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2009)

This is my generic name for all these yellow/orange Cortinarii mushrooms. I don't collect them for the table because I can't tell the many species apart. That's a good reason right there not to consider them for the table!
ScientificName: Cortinarius fulmineus; Platanus racemosa; Cortinarius; Cortinarii
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius fulmineus; Platanus racemosa; Cortinarius; Cortinarii

File.Name: 9c16b_9c13-011.jpg
Notes:
57495093 I can tell at a glance that this is a Lepiota mushroom coming up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. And isn't that a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the lower-right? The Lepiota mushroom caps usually have a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands. With some practice, they are easy to recognize. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

I have no idea what species this is - a good reason to avoid eating it. Some relatives are poisonous!

File.Name: 9c16c_9b28-047.jpg
Notes: 57494253 Here's another photo of the Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms that are fruiting in the woods right now. (See my [Previous] photo.) If you look close, you can see the white latex that appears when you break the gills and the "scrobiculate" stem with darker spots. The taste is very acrid - peppery hot, like wasabi. I like to nibble on them, but they are definitely not edible in quantity. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c17a_9c13-039.jpg
Notes:
57526980 I believe this handsome mushroom is part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. The gills are white, but they develop reddish-brown spots as they mature. (I'll post another photo of the gills tomorrow.) It has a strong "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil.

According to my books, these mushrooms are probably poisonous - not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. I'm not tempted to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c17b_9c13-038.jpg
Notes:
57526634 When good mushrooms go bad...

Here is the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus devouring an edible King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom, like mold on a piece of bread. The infection starts as a moldy-looking growth like this, then turns bright yellow and powdery. Needless to say, it's not edible even though the host mushroom is. It's associated with decay. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

It's hard to find bolete mushrooms in our woods that aren't already attacked by this fungus. Look closer at my "perfect bolete" photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57177077

File.Name: 9c17c_9c13-014.jpg
Notes:
57526197 Here is a different Cortinarius with violet colors instead of orange like my [Previous] photo. It is definitely in the subgenus Bulbopodium with that rimmed basal bulb, and the cap is viscid or slimy but the stem isn't. I guess it is part of the Cortinarius glaucopus group. It's not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

These mushrooms have beautiful violet colors when fresher than this specimen. All our Cortinarii seem to look the same dingy-brown when they get old.

File.Name: 9c18a_9c18-253.jpg
Notes:
57555452 Here is a little Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera perched on the edge of a White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushroom. It seems darker than our usual Drosophilid flies, but maybe that's just the lack of light so close to the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2009)

Yahoo - I'm now on winter vacation from school for two weeks! I reckon the woods are full of life after our rain last week.
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; White Russula

File.Name: 9c18b_9c18-254.jpg
Notes:
57554924 Here are perfect little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves on the forest floor today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2009)

The blue colors are part of a continuing issue I have with my camera about white balance in the deep winter shade.

File.Name: 9c18c_9c13-040.jpg
Notes:
57554569 This is looking under the skirts of the same mushroom in my [Previous] photo. The mushroom is part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. This photo shows the notched gills and sordid reddish-brown spots that develop as the gills mature. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2009)

This mushroom has a strong "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. According to my books, these mushrooms are probably poisonous - not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. I'm not tempted to nibble this one.

File.Name: 9c19a_9c19-066.jpg
Notes:
57583623 This mushroom looks harmless enough in this photo, but it's actually a deadly poisonous Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)! See my [Previous] photo for the details. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

Learn this species if you plan to eat any gilled mushroom! It's particularly dangerous because the symptoms are delayed for 6 to 24 hours - too late to pump your stomach. It basically goes into a loop that destroys your liver.

It's interesting that this mushroom may have been introduced from Europe. I have no idea how it got up here in the mountains. (Or how anyone decided it was introduced.)

I happened to find 3 of our most deadly mushrooms in the woods today. Don't eat any of these - or any mushroom if you can't tell them apart!

File.Name: 9c19b_9c19-071.jpg
Notes: 57583085 This mushroom I found in the woods today is the deadly poisonous Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom. It has a yellowish (or greenish) cap, a "partial veil" ring on the stem (here it's just pulling away from the cap), and a "universal veil" saclike volva that sheaths the base of the stalk. Learn this species if you plan to eat any gilled mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c19c_9c19-058.jpg
Notes:
57582685 Here are a pair of deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushrooms that I found in the woods today. It's remarkable to hold these mushrooms knowing that they are deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold or photograph them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

This mushroom lives up to its name! The mushroom has a mostly white cap and free gills. The cap is not "striate" with lines from the gills underneath. It has the remnants of a ring on the stem that are the remains of the "partial veil" membrane that once covered the gills and tore apart as the cap expanded. You can't see the sacklike "volva" or "death cup" that sheaths the base of the stalk like a broken eggshell in this photo - and that's been the bane of many mushroom collectors. (See my [Previous] photo.)

This mushroom looks ordinary, but it is deadly poisonous with one of the most lethal natural toxins known. It is elegant and beautiful. I love the name!
Locality:
See my [Previous] photo.)

This mushroom looks ordinary

File.Name: 9c19d_9c19-061.jpg
Notes: 57582283 Here are a pair of deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushrooms that I found in the woods today. It's remarkable to hold these mushrooms knowing that they are deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold or photograph them! You can see the "death cup" - really the saclike volva - at the base of the stems. They are one of our most beautiful mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c19e_9c19-033.jpg
Notes:
57581988 Beautiful, but deadly!

This is the real Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina), not much influenced by A. gemmata. It's growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. The white warts on the cap are fragments of the universal veil that once covered the whole mushroom. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

It's poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. This mushroom should not be used for recreational purposes! My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria

File.Name: 9c19f_9c19-035.jpg
Notes:
57581675 I believe this mushroom in the woods today is the real Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina), not much influenced by A. gemmata. It's growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It has a ring on the stem and a small collar-like volva at the base of the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

It's poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. This mushroom should not be used for recreational purposes! My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria

File.Name: 9c20a_9c19-012.jpg
Notes:
57609661 Here is a perfect Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. It's perched on the edge of an unpicked Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea) growing from the stump of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree near the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

Will the fly lay even more eggs? The Honey Mushrooms are already becoming a river of slime, but I guess there are still new mushroom caps to colonize. I'll post a "river of slime" photo tomorrow!
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; Honey Mushroom; California Bay
ScientificName: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 9c20b_9c20-084.jpg
Notes:
57608970 Mushrooms sometimes dry out, but sometimes they become mush! These critters are often the cause.

These are the larvae (maggots) of Pomace Flies aka Small Fruit Flies in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera. They are turning my unpicked Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) into a river of slime. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2009)

It's interesting that the maggots on dead animals do the same thing. Both of these critters are essentially aquatic organisms, and they create their own liquid environment.
CommonNames: Pomace Flies; Small Fruit Flies; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: 9c20c_9c19-068.jpg
Notes:
57608346 Here's how some mushrooms get a "ring" on the stem. It starts with a "partial veil" - the membranous layer of skin stretching from the stalk to the cap margin to protect and cover the immature gills. Then the veil tears free from the cap and hangs from the stem as the ring or annulus. Many mushrooms have this feature.

This is a deadly poisonous Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. This mushroom has both a ring on the stem and a cup or "volva" (the "death cup") at the base of the stem. You must learn to recognize both these features if you plan to eat any wild mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c21a_9c21-133.jpg
Notes:
57637594 Here is our first new wildflower of this rain year!

This is native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. It has an edible root (the "tooth"), but I don't really care to dig it up. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2009)

It's appropriate that this flower appeared today on the Winter Solstice. The days will start getting longer tonight in our mountains north of the Equator. It's the perfect time for new life and new hope in the woods!

File.Name: 9c21b_9c21-136.jpg
Notes:
57636834 Here is a huge mass of Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila) mushrooms growing from the wood chips on a hillside that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - about two years ago. I guess the area of the photo is about 3 feet across, and there are thousands of mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2009)

It's not surprising that I think mushrooms are so interesting in our woods!

File.Name: 9c21c_9c20-080.jpg
Notes:
57635929 The Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera maggots are starting to turn this once-impressive (and human-edible!) cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) into a mass of black slime. (It's not quite a "river of slime" yet, but it will be.) It smells like a diaper pail - ammonia. Look close - you can see the orange-colored maggots at work! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2009)

I have a "before" photo of the same cluster of mushrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57215117

It's interesting that the maggots on dead animals do the same thing. Both of these critters are essentially aquatic organisms, and they create their own liquid environment.
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: 9c22a_9c22-154.jpg
Notes:
57668630 It was very windy today, but I managed to capture this little fly (order Diptera) grounded on a sporophyte of Moss (Bryophyta) growing in the canyon. It was not very windy just 1/2 inch from the ground! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2009)

I can make out the the wing venation on this fly, but I give up trying to ID it. Life is too short! (Maybe Rhagionidae?)

We had a bit of rain last night - just a bit, but it was enough to take the crunch out of the oak leaves in the woods.

File.Name: 9c22b_9c20-122.jpg
Notes:
57667164 This is an attractive Yellow-Staining Milky Cap (Lactarius chrysorheus) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Or maybe it's L. vinaceorufescens?

When you cut across the gills with a twig, they exude a white juice that turns bright yellow in a few seconds. It has a bitter taste, not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2009)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak
