File.Name: 9c22c_9c20-125.jpg
Notes:
57666821 Here is an odd mushroom-like plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This early-flowering native has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in this place. It looks mushroom-like coming fully-formed from the earth, and it probably has a fungal connection with its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2009)

I usually find the tubular red flowers in February, but it looks to be early this year - like many other natives.

File.Name: 9c23a_9c23-178.jpg
Notes:
57702441 I believe this is an Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom - but it's growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree instead of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Like all boletes, they have sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2009)

The gills of this species quickly turn blue when you bruise them, and it's fun to write messages with a twig for other fungophiles (mushroom-lovers) to find!

In this case the message is true. The mushrooms are edible and tasty IF you find fresh specimens like these. They are quickly parasitized by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes, and turns them into bright yellow mush.

You can see a tree root sticking out from the the mass of mycelium at the base of this mushroom. This mushroom - and most of the mushrooms I've been showing - is mycorrhizal, which means that it gets its nutrition in a symbiotic association with the roots of their host trees. Mushrooms and trees are partners in the woods. The trees get soil nutrients and the fungi get carbohydrates. Both partners benefit. Ain't nature grand!
Locality: Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Bolete; California Bay; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Umbellularia californica; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum; mycorrhizal
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Umbellularia californica; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum; mycorrhizal

File.Name: 9c23b_9c20-115.jpg
Notes:
57701166 This is a perfect - but tiny! - Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods. It's like a miniature - only about 1 inch (2.5 cm) across. I read that the name is a corruption of "blue hat". (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2009)

These mushrooms are edible and delicious. I'm still waiting for the larger specimens along our driveway where they always (so far) appear.

There's something about purple/blue foods - blueberries and... what else?

File.Name: 9c23c_9c23-166.jpg
Notes:
57700621 I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle - Coccinella septempunctata in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera - that I found out in the cold this morning. I didn't realize when I took the photo, but it seems to be munching a larval Hemiptera. Darn, I would've focused differently! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2009)

I've just read that this beetle was introduced to North America from Europe for the biological control of aphids. It seems like the perfect pollinator for Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. See my photo at:


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/49653100
CommonNames: Seven-spotted Lady Beetle; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae

File.Name: 9c24a_9c24-215.jpg
Notes:
57733893 Do you know those tiny Flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after the rains? Here they are - at least here are the reflected light trails they leave behind. Look close - you can see every wing-beat with the sun just right in this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2009)

To get such a photo you have to be shooting almost into the sun with a long exposure. I reduced my ISO to 100 and got the photo at 0.77 sec at F/22. If I know how long the exposure is and how long the fly's trace is, I should be able to calculate how fast each wing-beat is!

I wish all my dear friends at fotolog a Merry Christmas!

File.Name: 9c24b_9c24-222.jpg
Notes:
57733486 Here's my Christmas present this year - this winter California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly flew by and perched on the oak tree off of our upstairs deck just when I happened to have my long lens on my camera! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2009)

This is a "tele-macro" photo with my "big glass" - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens, opened all the way.

File.Name: 9c24c_9c24-187.jpg
Notes:
57733233 This beautiful Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom is in the process of shedding its partial veil to expose its gills to the world. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2009)

It's ok to admire and photograph and even handle this mushroom - just don't eat it because it contains one of the most deadly toxins known!

My son Ryan found this mushroom yesterday near home. He captured a California Slender Salamander (Batrachoseps attenuatus) curled up in the volva. I followed his directions and found the mushroom, but the salamander had moved on. See Ry's photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/rkummel/4209402213/

A poisonous mushroom for a Christmas Eve photo? Well, why not? I captured it today, it's beautiful, and it comes from nature, in the woods, with God's blessing. For Christmas this year, I'm only giving what the Earth brings forth.
Locality: Batrachoseps attenuatus) curled up in the volva. I followed his directions and found the mushroom

File.Name: 9c25a_9c25-229.jpg
Notes:
57754891 Here are swarms of the remarkable little Flies (order Diptera) that I've been featuring in my [Previous] few photos. They dance in the sunbeams after the rains. Here they are rising above the oak trees in plumes of thousands that constantly change shape like schools of fish or flocks of starlings. You can see the dim shapes of beautiful Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii) in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2009)

I got this photo this morning from our upstairs porch. The flies are fascinating to watch, but you have to be standing in just right place to see them, almost looking into the sun. When the sun isn't just right, they are nearly invisible. There's some unavoidable lens flare in my photo.

I managed to catch one of the flies in my hand. I smushed it in the capture, but I guess from the remains that it was a Midge in the family Chironomidae - very tiny and very fragile, with large antennae. I reckon this is a mating flight. What do they do for the rest of the year?

Merry Christmas,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: 9c25b_9c24-217.jpg
Notes:
57753687 An undiscovered Jackson Pollack painting?
No such luck!

Here is another photo of the tiny Flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after the rains. Here they are - at least here are the reflected light trails they leave behind. They move constantly, and sometimes make sudden turns. You can see every move they make. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2009)

I got this photo by shooting almost into the sun with oak trees behind, using a slow shutter speed. The mass of dancing flies is almost invisible when you look away from the sun.

File.Name: 9c25c_9c24-215.jpg
Notes:
57753265 Jellyfish tendrils? Tapeworms?

Nah, here's another photo of the tiny Flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after the rains. Here they are - at least here are the reflected light trails they leave behind. You can see every wing-beat when the sun is pointed just right in this photo. See my [Previous] and [Next] photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c26a_9c25-244.jpg
Notes:
57795654 Toyon berries aren't the only red "Christmas berries" in the woods. These are the ripening fruit of my favorite native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae) plant family. The berries are usually high up in the trees and hard to photograph, but this tree was low. The berries are tasty and less astringent than the toyon berries. They've been disappearing fast as the birds eat them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2009)

Julie and I went for a Christmas hike at Kinevan Ranch near the old tumble-down cabin where we lived 30 years ago. The woods are reclaiming the old apple orchard with these young madrones and many more native trees and shrubs.

File.Name: 9c26b_9c25-241.jpg
Notes: 57795279 Christmas Berries for Christmas! They are also known as Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. The berries look fairly plentiful - good news for the birds! The raw fruit are astringent - they dry your mouth out - but they are tasty when cooked and were a native American Chumash staple. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2009)
CommonNames: Christmas Berries; Toyon; California Holly; Rose

File.Name: 9c26c_9c25-237.jpg
Notes: 57794810 This pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) - maybe in the family Lygaeidae? - was perched on a sunny leaf in the woods on Christmas Day. I guess they overwinter as adults. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c27a_9c27-048.jpg
Notes:
57814328 Slugs have teeth?

Well, sort of. This giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch these new leaves of Sheep Sorrel (Rumex acteosella) in the Polygonaceae plant family growing in the woods. No wonder, I like to nibble them too! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2009)

This is the first Banana Slug I've see this year. I watched it for some time, and it put away quite a few of the leaves. This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains. (There are much bigger mollusks in the ocean!)

File.Name: 9c27b_9c27-060.jpg
Notes:
57813097 I'm afraid this is a real Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. My son Ryan found it today resting on his camera(!) in our house. He moved it to a house plant, and I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2009)

I hear they live in Dusky-footed Woodrat (Neotoma fuscipes) nests, but they can move also into houses. I'm not surprised - the woodrats have moved into our house too in the past!

It's a problem because the insect is a potential host of Chagas disease, caused by the parasite Trypanosoma cruzi. My book (Charles Hogue, The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin, 1974) says that "The Western Cone-Nose Bug often harbors a trypanosome parasite apparently identical to the one found in the disease. It may be a non-virulent strain."

But I've read that Chagas Disease has grown in Southern California because of increased immigration from Mexico and Central America, so the chances of infection must be greater. The CDC says that "Although there are triatomine bugs in the U.S., only rare vectorborne cases of Chagas disease have been documented."

Sounds good, but we'll keep them out of our bed!
CommonNames: Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug; Dusky-footed Woodrat; Chagas disease; The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin
ScientificName: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi
associatedTaxa: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi

File.Name: 9c27c_9c26-002.jpg
Notes:
57811062 We went down to visit my Dad yesterday. In his garden, I found these Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees, just like they do at home. (Santa Monica Mountains, 26 December 2009)

This photo is looking at a small mushroom growing under a larger one like a puppy. Look at the larger mushroom - you can see the veils torn between the universal veil that covers the whole cap and points out and the partial veil that covers the gills and points in.

These mushrooms are deadly poisonous. Enjoy them, but don't eat them!

File.Name: 9c28a_9c19-014.jpg
Notes:
57838976 These are elegant Pleated Inky Cap (Coprinus plicatilis or kin) mushrooms fruiting along our driveway soon after the last real rain. The grooves or "pleats" in the cap look silver! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2009)

Today's photos are all dark-spored mushrooms from the last week or so. It's too dry for new mushrooms and too cold for insects, so I'm catching up on my photos over winter vacation from school. I have lots to catch up on!

File.Name: 9c28b_9c21-137.jpg
Notes:
57838881 Here are a mass of dark-spored Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila) mushrooms growing from the wood chips on a hillside that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - about two years ago.

The mushrooms are "hygrophanous" which means they change color from dark to light as they dry out. These mushrooms are already pretty dry. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c28c_9c21-145.jpg
Notes: 57838813 This is a beautiful member of the dark-spored Psathyrella longipes group of mushrooms coming up with new woodland plants. These mushrooms are very fragile - they break apart when I so much as touch them. You can see fragments of a white veil spaced evenly around the edge of the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c29a_9c29-077.jpg
Notes:
57875437 With so many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) in the trees, the real challenge is to get just one! Here is a Monarch resting on the ground with new leaves of Poison Hemlock (Conium maculatum). It's one of the invasive weeds that my wife Julie is trying to control at this place. See my [Previous] two photos. (Refugio Creek, 29 December 2009)

You can read more about Julie's restoration project here: http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/refugiodetails.htm

File.Name: 9c29b_9c29-095.jpg
Notes:
57875013 Here are lots of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from a Black Cottonwood (Populus balsamifera ssp. trichocarpa) tree today. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Refugio Creek, 29 December 2009)

I got today's photos with my 105 mm macro lens - it's all I had, so I had to crop. This photo is 2 stops over-exposed to bring out some detail in the butterflies. The butterflies look very dark when you see them - almost like bunches of dead leaves. It's good camouflage.
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa

File.Name: 9c29c_9c29-090.jpg
Notes:
57874395 "Strange fruit hanging from the poplar trees..."

Look close! These strange fruit are many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from a Black Cottonwood (Populus balsamifera ssp. trichocarpa) tree - which is one of our two native poplars. (Refugio Creek, 29 December 2009)

These aren't the horrible "strange fruit" that Billie Holiday sang of - here is something wonderful! The monarch butterflies from all the western states on our side of the Rocky Mountains congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. (East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

I went with my wife Julie to visit a restoration project she's working on along Refugio Creek on the south slope of our mountains. We noticed lots of butterflies, and I found the source. This is not the well-known Coronado Butterfly Preserve - just a tree in a local canyon.

You can read more about Julie's project here:
   http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/refugiodetails.htm
Locality:
East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

I went with my wife Julie to visit a restoration project she's working on along Refugio Creek on the south slope of our mountains. We noticed lots of butterflies
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa

File.Name: 9c30a_9c30-106.jpg
Notes:
57903308 Look close! Here is another large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that I found under the eaves of our roof. I found it a couple of weeks ago, and I don't think it's moved since then. Today seemed a good day to try for a photo, since it's been gloomy and drizzling all day so I had nothing else to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2009)

I had to balance my tripod on three chairs to get this photo looking almost straight up. The exposure is 8 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200 while I held my breath!

I took a couple more photos with flash, but I like this one better. The spider seems to have a mournful expression painted on its abdomen! You can see the neat pattern of "stars" it uses to fasten itself upside down.

File.Name: 9c30b_9c26-007.jpg
Notes: 57902633 This is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that I found on a leaf at my Dad's house a few days ago when we went to visit. These large winter orb-weaver spiders are pretty cool - unless you happen to walk into one of their webs at night. Yikes - where's the spider?? (Santa Monica Mountains, 26 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c30c_9c29-078.jpg
Notes: 57902252 Here are the white-spotted head and thorax of a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) that I found on the ground yesterday where Julie is doing restoration work. See my [Previous] few photos. (Refugio Creek, 29 December 2009)

File.Name: 9c31a_9c31-110.jpg
Notes:
57940253 Dancing in the New Year...

This is the first new flower I've seen of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the canyon. It looks like a ballet dancer "en pointe"! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

Feliz 2010!
Happy New Year!

File.Name: 9c31b_9c31-156.jpg
Notes:
57940057 Here is a giant "millipede" (class Diplopoda - maybe Hiltonius pulchrus?) that my son Ryan found in the garden today. We posed it on this acorn. It's at least 3 inches long. We're not sure if it's alive or playing dead. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

It has remarkable eyes - they look riveted on!

Millipedes are "thousand leggers" differing from the "hundred legger" centipedes - they have two pair of legs on most body segments. Our large millipedes are often parasitized.

Ry's been hunting for Gastropods - land snails. He's finding lots of other critters. You can see Ry's photos at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/rkummel/

File.Name: 9c31c_9c31-152.jpg
Notes:
57939586 Here is a "Woolly Bear" caterpillar - the larva of a Tiger Moth (order Lepitoptera, family Arctiidae). This one didn't live to see the new year. It has a serious "fungus infection" and there is also a pupal case right next to it that is maybe our large Spiny Tachina Fly (order Diptera, family Tachinidae, species Paradejeania rutiloides or kin). (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

My son Ryan found this "caterpillar cadaver" with the pupal case right next to it and brought them home to photograph. You can see Ry's photos at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/rkummel/

File.Name: 8101a_8101-098.jpg
Notes:
Even in a drought year, I'm sure there is always a native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family with new flowers somewhere in the mountains! Here's a new flower with the bones of last summer's seed pods. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2008)

I don't care if this is an old flower or a new one. Life goes on, and that's the right message for the New Year. It's an election year here at home... I hope we will all have a grand new year!

File.Name: 8101b_8101-101.jpg
Notes:
New Morning-glory flowers in the mountains - but what kind? I believe this is Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) which usually grows near the beach, but here it is along West Camino Cielo Road on top of the mountains. Our usual mountain species is Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) which has a pair of bracts below the calyx of the flower rather than overlapping the calyx like this. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2008)

It's not right, but it's also a stumper. Should I remove these native plants that are flowering when flowers are scarce, or let them be?
ScientificName: Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata
associatedTaxa: Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata

File.Name: 8101c_8101-092.jpg
Notes:
This is my favorite photo today - here are new leaves of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) flower in the Lily (Liliaceae) family coming up through bare ground on a fire trail that was cut through the chaparral last summer. I just found this new trail today. I understand that fire-follower plants come up after wildfires, but I don't understand why this fire-follower has come up so mature along the new trail. I guess I have seen the plants when crawling under thick chaparral, just waiting for their time in the sun... (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2008)

I guess life goes on, and that's the right message for the New Year. It's an election year here at home. I hope we all have a grand new year!

File.Name: 8101d_7c31-075.jpg
Notes:
I posted another photo of this same Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods a week ago. Take a look at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/28800998

These mushrooms live up to their species name - they are perfect humidity measurers, legs down after a rain in the last photo, and legs up now when it's dry. This is the same mushroom as the previous photo now curled up after a week of dry wind. The good news is there might be a real storm coming later this week. Fingers crossed... (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

File.Name: 8102a_7c29-005.jpg
Notes:
Hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) exploring the woods after just a drizzle of rain last week. These large terrestrial mollusks (Gastropoda) are only active after rains (even drizzles) when there is some fresh moisture in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2007)

Today, we're been under a "Red Flag Warning" for single-digit humidity and fire weather in winter - the big slugs are hiding. But now the weather forecast is for several days of real rain, starting tomorrow. I'm hoping for more banana slug weather!

File.Name: 8102b_7c31-085.jpg
Notes: Me and my shadow... Here's a fly-sized Scavenger Fly (family Sarcophagidae in the order Diptera) with bright red eyes resting on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) with its shadow in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

File.Name: 8102c_7c30-052.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from leaves of a non-native Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) butterfly tree. This is a remarkable location just a mile from the local CostCo big-box shopping center, where tens of thousands of these butterflies migrate every winter from across the western states. The butterflies come to the same trees every year. Now the trees are preserved as part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

There's more info here:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

File.Name: 8102d_7c27-152.jpg
Notes:
It's been one of our driest years on record here in the mountains behind Santa Barbara. The good news is that it's been a hard year for most of the non-native weeds as well as the native plants. The bad news is that this invasive Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum, family Poaceae) - introduced from the Mediterranean region - has been doing really well this summer, and it's taking over along the mountain roads. This "bad grass" is attractive and has a native look, but it does not belong here. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

More good news is that our weather forcast is for several days of real rain, starting tomorrow!

File.Name: 8103a_7c21-258.jpg
Notes:
Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms in the woods - or in the newspapers or TV or the Web!

I scratched this message with a twig on the blue-staining cap of this Boletus satanas mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees last week in the canyon. This is Satan's Bolete mushroom that is NOT EDIBLE. There are other interesting mushrooms that turn blue in the woods, but this one is definately poisonous.

It's fun to write messages on these mushrooms. Two weeks later, I can still read it under the same mushroom! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2007)

File.Name: 8103b_7c21-259.jpg
Notes: Hefty Boletus satanas mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees last week. This is Satan's Bolete - poisonous - it has *DON'T EAT ME* colors and attitude despite what you might read on the gills! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2007)

File.Name: 8104a_8104-120.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on a succulent plant in a hanging basket under the eaves of the porch in the rain today. It's been raining all day - almost 5 inches so far, and more coming tonight. This is just what we need to bring the woods back to life after a drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2008)

File.Name: 8104b_8104-119.jpg
Notes: D'oh - I must have bumped my tripod during this long exposure of a succulent plant hanging from a basket on the porch in the rain today. It looks electric! (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2008)

File.Name: 8104c_8103-111.jpg
Notes:
This is like one of those puzzle pictures - how many Lichens can you count in this photo?

This is a twig of a native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) shrub in the Rose (Rosaceae) family growing near Lake Cachuma. I think of Lichens as simple epiphytes that grow where they can find space - so why do they prefer one shrub over another? There must be more to the story! (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2008)

These lichens have been waiting for rain - we're getting it today!

File.Name: 8105a_8105-030.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Lepiota mushroom coming up through fallen leaves of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family in the woods. This little mushroom is distinctive with free white gills and a cap with a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2008)

We had 9+ inches of rain yesterday, and more drizzle today -and more expected tomorrow! That's a good remedy for a drought year. I was hoping for mushrooms everywhere today, but they do take time. The creek is running. Life is good!

File.Name: 8105b_8105-028.jpg
Notes:
Interesting Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. The old rinds of these fungi are common in the woods for months, but I don't think I've ever found one just starting to open like this. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2008)

These odd "mushrooms" are natural humidity sensors that open when damp and close when dry. Compare my recent photos of these 'shrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/29191472 and
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/28800998

File.Name: 8106a_8105-025.jpg
Notes:
We had 9+ inches of rain on Friday night after a drought summer, and here is the first tasty Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom already pushing up through the fallen oak leaves in the woods! The new rains are bringing the woods back to life. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2008)

It's still raining tonight. We're up to 10.5 inches of rain since Friday, and it's raining hard now.

I had no time for photos today getting ready to start school tomorrow, but I look forward to finding more new mushrooms next weekend.

File.Name: 8107a_8105-012.jpg
Notes:
What the rain brings... This plant looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. Dinosaurs stepped on primative plants similar to this! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2008)

The plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places, like lichens. They are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. Compare my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 8107b_7c26-100.jpg
Notes:
Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a dry rock outcrop on south-facing Grass Mountain on the other side of the Santa Ynez Valley. These fern-relatives curl up when dry, but they look very different after a rain. See my [Next] photo. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 December 2007)

The plants are collected for model train layouts because they look like little conifers.

File.Name: 8108a_8105-004.jpg
Notes: New leaves of deciduous Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family) still wet with rain from the storms last weekend. I've been studying the rainfall charts. We received 10.94 inches of drought-buster rain last weekend. By comparison, we only had 10.90 inches of rain all last year - our driest year ever in the 40+ year rain record for San Marcos Pass. It's good to see the woods coming back to life after a hard year. I hope the woods recover with new life to photograph. I'm afraid some local species may be lost after the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2008)

File.Name: 8109a_8109-072.jpg
Notes: Winter-bare deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family) tree at school this evening with beautiful clouds streaking behind. We call this the "Carpe Diem" tree at my school - "Seize the day!" - this grand tree has seized many days. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 January 2008)

File.Name: 8110a_8110-108.jpg
Notes:
Looking towards the misty mountains past a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) not far from home. That's Loma Alta in front, with Ranger Peak and Figueroa Mountain in the blue distance. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2008)

"So many roads, so many trains to ride,
so many canyons to explore!

File.Name: 8111a_8111-193.jpg
Notes: The morning drizzle drops were still hanging on this new seedling leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae family when I got home from school late this afternoon. We live on the dark north-facing side of the mountains where the sun never shines so close to the solstice. I got this photo with a 3 second exposure at f/32 and ISO 200. You can see the woods reflected again and again in the drops. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2008)

File.Name: 8111b_8111-151.jpg
Notes: Looking up into the "Carpe Diem" tree at my school - "Seize the day!" It's a winter-bare deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family), the same tree I showed two photos back. This is the tree of life for many critters. Look close and you might spot a pair of Acorn Woodpecker (Melanerpes formicivorus) nest cavities. The woodpeckers make the cavities, but they have to drive off the non-native Starling (Sturnus vulgaris) birds that try to take over. Living in nature is never easy. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 January 2008)

File.Name: 8112a_8112-022.jpg
Notes: Hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) contemplating a fallen leaf of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) in its path in the woods. It did get over the leaf, but it took many minutes to do so. I reckon the upper pair of tentacles are eyestalks with sensitive dark "pupils" and the lower pair are feelers and tasters. They are very sensitive. They always withdraw when I get close with my camera, but after a few minutes, the slugs open up and let me get close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2008)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; Tanbark Oak; Tanoak

File.Name: 8112b_8112-019.jpg
Notes: New flower buds of uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae family. This plant is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it only grows here in the shady places where the sun never shines. I see flowers every year, but I've never seen berries. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's trying to hold on against climate change. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2008)

File.Name: 8112c_8111-128.jpg
Notes: Perfect little dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushroom - maybe a Mica Cap (Coprinus micaceus) with glistening whitish particles of the universal veil on the cap, but probably some other Coprinus species with a similar trait and no common name. Touch it, and it falls apart. Being edible is not an issue with such friable little mushrooms, but let me know if you try it. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 January 2008)

File.Name: 8113a_8113-027.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant new flowers of Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The red "petals" are really leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the narrow beak-like green tubes coming from the calyces hidden among the bracts. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2008)

We have two different Paintbrush species in the mountains - actually more - and they both started to flower this weekend. This one has green leaves and bracts without woolly hairs It's a treat to find new wildflowers after a drought year!

I can't resist photographing these paintbrush flowers with the sun behind!

Compare my [Previous] photo of a different Castilleja paintbrush.

File.Name: 8113b_8111-182.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant new flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The red "petals" are really leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the narrow beak-like green or yellow tubes coming from the calyces hidden among the bracts. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2008)

We have two different Paintbrush species in the mountains - actually more - and they both started to flower this weekend. This one has white-woolly leaves. You can see some fuzz on the green bracts of the flower. It's a treat to find new wildflowers after a drought year!

I can't resist photographing these paintbrush flowers with the sun behind! Compare my [Next] photo of a different Castilleja paintbrush without the woolly leaves.

File.Name: 8113c_8112-008.jpg
Notes: Into the woods... this hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) is s_l_o_w_l_y and deliberately looking for whatever it looks for among the new seedlings in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2008)

File.Name: 8114a_8113-036.jpg
Notes:
There are troops of these little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably white-spored Mycena mushrooms - coming up through fallen oak leaves in the woods after the holiday rains. These little 'shrooms are the fruiting bodies of the fungi that turn fallen leaves into compost to keep the woods alive. I only find then in shady places where the sun never shines. It's great fun to lay on my belly with my low tripod to capture them in dim light with long exposures. This photo is 4 seconds @ F/22 @ ISO 200. I hold my breath for every photo! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2008)

I don't know if they're edible. They are too small to matter. Despite the rain, the air is dry. The tasty pan-size mushrooms are scarce.

File.Name: 8114b_8111-083.jpg
Notes:
The bright red bracts are the showy part of this Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) flower in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family - it's a good name since the flower heads look like they've been dipped in red paint. But here is the real flower - that fused green and yellow tube that sticks out from the red-tipped bracts and sepals. The pollen-receptive female pistil sticks out from the end, and you can get a glimpse of the male pollen-bearing stamens hidden inside the translucent flower tube with the sun behind. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2008)

We have two different perennial Paintbrushes in our mountains. This is the woolly one I show two photos back. My [Previous] photo is the other species.

File.Name: 8115a_7c15-028.jpg
Notes:
This is the waning half-moon above the native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Heath (Ericaceae) family on the ridge at home as seen from our porch. There are still a few berries in the trees. I got this photo with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens opened all the way. The moon is another world! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

I took this photo exactly one month ago on December 15, but I never got around to posting it. Here we are on January 15 a month later. I went out and looked this afternoon, and the half-moon is much higher above the trees, way too high for a photo. Every day - and every night - are different.

File.Name: 8116a_8113-032.jpg
Notes: There are many little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the woods after our holiday rains, but I only found a few of these LBMs that are white with a distinctive funnel-shape. They are growing from fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves, which gives a sense of scale. I guess these are genus Clitocybe with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk. They are too small to think about eating. Pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2008)

File.Name: 8116b_8112-002.jpg
Notes: I guess this dark-spored little LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - is a Psathyrella, maybe Psathyrella gracilis or kin. It's growing with new seedlings from a pile of garden trimmings and wood chips. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2008)

File.Name: 8117a_8117-077.jpg
Notes:
You know those little flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after the rains - here's a galaxy of them dancing by a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree hanging over flowing Birabent Creek at the base of Grass Mountain today. (Figueroa Mountain, 17 January 2008)

The paths of these little flies are beautiful. I didn't have my tripod today for a long exposure, but I have a few more photos in the archive:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9287342
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9094273
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8813003
   http://www.fotolog.com/bugsncritters/9023802

File.Name: 8117b_8117-120.jpg
Notes: Kids know what to do with big trees - climb them! I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek at the base of Grass Mountain today. This is looking up into a grand Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae family. Trees to climb, mushrooms to find, the creek is running, the sun is shining - it was a perfect afternoon with friends! (Figueroa Mountain, 17 January 2008)

File.Name: 8117c_8113-031.jpg
Notes: This is one of the same little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - as my [Previous] photo nested in the fallen leaves in the woods. Maybe it is genus Clitocybe with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - it's too small to eat, so I really don't care. I love the smooth form of these little mushrooms against the curves and points of the fallen leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2008)

File.Name: 8118a_8118-186.jpg
Notes:
Some mushrooms stand out with brilliant colors, but these woodland mushrooms have the chiaroscuro colors of the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves they are growing from. The exposure is 1/5 second @ f/22 @ ISO 200 - with no flash.

I believe these are Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola or kin) mushrooms. The caps have brown spores and bruise yellow and they have a slightly enlarged base that does not turn bright yellow when cut. They have a pleasant spicy smell and taste. It's said to be edible, but I find the local Agaricus group really confusing, so I usually stick with a nibble or two. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2008)

File.Name: 8118b_8117-113.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Cortinarius mushrooms in the Bulbopodium subgenus - they have a sticky cap that collects woodland debris and a bulbous base. The young gills of this mushroom are yellowish, so maybe it's Cortinarius fulmineus or kin. All Cortinarius mushrooms have a "cortina" - the beautiful veil of hairs that covers the immature gills of the mushroom on the left. As the mushrooms mature, the veil collapses leaving hairs on the stem that collect the rusty-brown spores, like the mushroom on the right. (Figueroa Mountain, 17 January 2008)

Cortinarius is the largest genus of gilled mushrooms, and they are very hard to tell apart. There are poisonous species, so it's good advice not to eat any of them. The pioneering Swedish mycologist Elias Magnus Fries said in 1838: "No genus [of mushrooms] is more natural nor more sharply distinguished from others... but the species are so intimately related amoung themselves that to distinguish the separate ones is almost to be despaired of." I don't collect any of them for the table, but I always seek them out for photos.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus; Cortinarius; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus; Cortinarius; Cortinarius

File.Name: 8119a_8119-010.jpg
Notes:
Deep purple - that's the word for this beautiful little Cortinarius mushroom in the woods. The cap broke off when I picked it today, but here it is with a bit of its stem. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2008)

We had good rain two weeks ago, but the air has been dry since then, and mushrooms are scarce even though the soil is still damp. Somehow the mushrooms know! I took a long walk today and came up empty. Then I noticed a bump in the oak leaves near home and started digging. There are new mushrooms hidding under the oak leaves in the woods hardly raising a "mushrump" of leaves to help me find them.

Some mushrooms make a habit of this and become "cleistogamous" so they never open in the air. Truffles are one example, but there are many more.

Today's photos are all violet Cortinarius mushrooms that are not edible except for the violet Blewit that is edible and tasty. Be careful!

Cortinarius is the largest genus of gilled mushrooms, and they are very hard to tell apart. There are poisonous species, so it's good advice not to eat any of them. The pioneering Swedish mycologist Elias Magnus Fries said in 1838: "No genus [of mushrooms] is more natural nor more sharply distinguished from others... but the species are so intimately related amoung themselves that to distinguish the separate ones is almost to be despaired of." I don't collect any of them for the table, but I always seek them out for photos.

File.Name: 8119b_8119-014.jpg
Notes: Here's a different Cortinarius mushroom in the woods, though it also has a violet cap like the 'shrooms in my [Previous] and [Next] photos. Look close and you can see rusty-brown spore-stained hairs on the stem that are the remnants of the cortina. The stem of this mushroom is not enlarged, and the cap does not seem to be sticky or viscid. I won't try to guess a species or even a subgenus. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2008)

File.Name: 8119c_8119-019.jpg
Notes:
This Cortinarius mushroom is spooky because it looks so much like the tasty and collectable Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms that grow nearby. Compare my [Previous] photo. Look close and you can see the rusty-brown spore-stained hairs on the stem which declare it as genus Cortinarius. I guess this 'shroom is subgenus Phlegmacium - viscid cap and swollen base, but the basal bulb is not rimmed. Or is it really subgenus Bulbopodium?

It's easy to recognize a Cortinarius mushroom, but impossible to figure species. It's hard to recognize the same mushroom a week later! There are poisonous species, so it's best to avoid them all - except for photos.
Locality: Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms that grow nearby. Compare my [Previous] photo. Look close and you can see the rusty-brown spore-stained hairs on the stem which declare it as genus Cortinarius. I guess this 'shroom is subgenus Phlegmacium - viscid cap and swollen base
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Cortinarius; Phlegmacium; Bulbopodium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Cortinarius; Phlegmacium; Bulbopodium

File.Name: 8119d_8112-007.jpg
Notes:
Edible and tasty Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom in the woods. These "blue hat" mushrooms are common along the road in our canyon, and we collect them regularly when there are no chanterelles to be found. But be careful! We have a very similar violet Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that is not edible - at least its edibility is unknown. Compare my [Next] photo and look at the gills and stem. Cortinarius mushrooms have rusty-brown spore-stained hairs on the stem that Blewits never have. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2008)

The best way to learn mushrooms is to go for a walk with someone who knows them. Caveat emptor!
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Cortinarius; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Cortinarius; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor

File.Name: 8119e_8117-114.jpg
Notes: Looking under the cap of a different Cortinarius mushroom that was growing with the one I posted two photos [Previous]. All Cortinarius mushrooms have a "cortina" - the beautiful veil of hairs that covers the immature gills of the mushroom. In this photo, the gills have violet colors, and the hairs are pulling free and picking up a dusting of rusty-orange spores. As the mushroom matures, all that's left of the cortina are the spore-stained hairs on the stem - the most important clue for identifying these mushrooms. See my [Next] photos. (Figueroa Mountain, 17 January 2008)

File.Name: 8120a_8119-007.jpg
Notes:
First new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon. The red "petals" are really sepals, and the real petals are white - compare my [Previous] photo. You can see the red-tipped glands at the base of this flower, and the leaves and even the flower petals have pin-like glandular hairs. This flower seems to be resting its stigma on the twig. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

This is the most common Ribes in our canyon, though there are several other species in the mountains. Today's photos are all different early-flowering Ribes species. We're expecting rain - maybe snow - this week. That will help bring out more flowers and all that the rain brings.
ScientificName: Ribes amarum; hoffmannii; Grossulariaceae; Ribes; Ribes
associatedTaxa: Ribes amarum; hoffmannii; Grossulariaceae; Ribes; Ribes

File.Name: 8120b_8120-030.jpg
Notes: New flower bud of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae family. This shrub lacks the small spines between the big ones like the [Previous] species, and it lacks the glandular hairs of the [Next] species. It's scattered in our mountains, but not common. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

File.Name: 8120c_8120-040.jpg
Notes: Fresh new foliage of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family coming back to life for the new year. This plant has little spines between the big spines! I haven't seen any flowers yet. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

File.Name: 8120d_7c31-074.jpg
Notes:
There are still new flowers on the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae family growing on the dry edge of our garden. I noticed the first flowers this year at the end of September, and here they are still going strong when the other new flowers in the woods are just starting to appear. This humble shrub that flowers early is an important food source for the resident hummingbirds during the most difficult time of the year. Now I've been noticing the hummers working the new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2008)

This currant is a Ribes without thorns. The rest of todays photos are new gooseberries - Ribes with thorns.
ScientificName: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes ; Ribes
associatedTaxa: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes ; Ribes

File.Name: 8121a_8118-202.jpg
Notes:
New urn-like flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. The pretty flowers remind me of those candy "Easter eggs" with dioramas inside - I want to look inside! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2008)

This photo also shows the trails of a Leaf Miner insect inside the leaf. Imagine being so tiny that a single leaf is your continent to explore. I reckon we all leave these trails through space-time that maybe others will notice and wonder about!

The Manzanita flowers are important in our mountains to feed the hummingbirds between the Ribes currants and the spring wildflowers that are still in seedling stage. See my [Previous] photo. The woods have dried out since our last good rain, but there's a forecast for new rain this week - maybe even snow!

File.Name: 8121b_8118-195.jpg
Notes:
I know this photo isn't sharp, but I captured the moment! This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working the new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. We think of typical hummingbird flowers as being red and long and tubular, but these short urn-like Manzanita flowers are good enough in midwinter. I keep a humming feeder in our garden, but the hummers haven't been usung it much lately. Now I know why, though they still come home at night. I guess they prefer the real thing. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2008)

I was trying to photograph the flowers with my 105 mm macro lens with manual focus and "A" aperture-priority exposure. No time to adjust when I heard the hummer, I grabbed this photo at 1/80 second @ f/22 @ ISO 200. Good enough!

File.Name: 8121c_8119-001.jpg
Notes:
We had lizard weather two days ago - warm enough to bring out this little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on the rocks on the sunny-side of the creek in the canyon. I guess these new lizards are light sleepers. There are a few flies in the air - they are also light sleepers who wake up on sunny winter days. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2008)

The weather is different today - it's cold and gloomy, and we've had some welcome sprinkles. We've been in the 30s all day, and there's a chance of snow tonight.

File.Name: 8122a_8118-196.jpg
Notes:
Strange red leaves of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. This plant comes up fast like a mushroom and then disappears early after it flowers. There is a fungus (among us) connection. This plant is a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It has some green leaves, but the foliage is mostly bright red like this. I reckon it's a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub in which the invisible fungus partner makes the intimate connection between herb and shrub. I wonder if each partner benefits? Sometimes I wonder if the whole forest is connected by mycelial strands of fungi into one interconnected living thing.

Today's photos are all native plants that remind me of mushrooms because they appear fully-formed so fast on bare ground in the woods or under the chaparral. All it takes is a bit of rain after the summer drought. The good news is that it's raining as I write this. We've had an inch of rain today, and more - maybe much more - is expected in the next few days. Bring it on!
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It has some green leaves

File.Name: 8122b_8118-153.jpg
Notes: New growth of native perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family appearing from the roots in the woods near Lake Cachuma. There will be flowers in a few weeks. The perennial roots used to be collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. Maybe they still are, but I'd rather find the strange flowers in the woods\\, no matter how useful they are as an herb. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 January 2008)

File.Name: 8122c_8120-023.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family growing from a bulb in a place that was cleared and "masticated" for a fire break last summer. This place was thick north-slope chaparral that's impossible to walk or crawl through a year ago. This native plant is not a seedling. It must have been waiting for the enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine, aka "The Masticator" - or a wildfire - to arrive. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

I see many of these mature plants ready to flower in places in the chaparral that have been recently cleared. They must grow and wait under the chaparral for years until it's their time.

File.Name: 8122d_8118-152.jpg
Notes:
New lily-relative monocot plants coming from bulbs in the Earth. I guess they are new Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. I won't know for sure until they flower. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 January 2008)

I've been looking for new mushrooms, but I also find these native perennial plants that seem to emerge fully-formed after the rains finally come after a very dry drought summer. It's like meeting an old friend - good to see you!

File.Name: 8123a_8123-052.jpg
Notes: Looking up the road when I got home from school today and stopped to get the soggy mail from our mailbox. We had an inch of snow today - along with almost 5 inches of rain! The snow was turning to rain and slush by the time I got home from school to get this photo. We get snow in our mountains every year or two, and it's usually a treat rather than a nuisance. I get most of my photos in the canyon to the left. Last weekend I got a photo of a lizard basking in the sun. I wonder what it is doing now - along with all the other critters in my photos?
Locality: Looking up the road when I got home from school today and stopped to get the soggy mail from our mailbox.  We had an inch of snow today - along with almost 5 inches of rain!
