File.Name: 8123b_8123-071.jpg
Notes: We had an inch of snow today - after almost 5 inches of rain! The snow was turning to rain and slush by the time I got home from school. Here's a pot of succulents on the porch. They can take it. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2008)

File.Name: 8124a_8120-025.jpg
Notes:
First new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I guess some plant has to be first, but this salvia doesn't look right because the flower stalk and sepals are very dark rather than the usual green. This plant is growing in a place that was cleared (and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break, so maybe it's confused. If you look very close at its two pollen-bearing stamens and the red female Y-shaped pollen-receptive stigmas, you can see that it is self-pollinating by touching itself. Just as well. Insects are scarce in the winter woods, though there are hummingbirds working the Manzanita flowers nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

The good news is that there are native plants - including fire-followers - appearing on the fire-break. I've been worried the whole hillside might come back with noxious invasive weeds like Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis).

I got this photo last weekend before the heavy rain and snow this week. So far, we've had about 6.5 inches (16 cm) of rain. Not bad! The forecast is for that much again - maybe twice that - this weekend. No chance for photos today - it was pouring rain when I got home from school today. This is a real treat after a record drought year - this will be a beautiful spring!
Locality:
Centaurea solstitialis).

I got this photo last weekend before the heavy rain and snow this week. So far
CommonNames: Hummingbird Sage; Mint; Yellow Star Thistle

File.Name: 8124b_8120-043.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family near our garden. This tree is cousin to Cinnamon, Camphor, Avocado, and Sassafras. It is pungent with ethereal oils, and a piece of a leaf tastes good in a stew if you don't use too much.

I took this photo on a sunny day last weekend before the storm. My [Previous] photo shows the same tree bent down with snow! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2008)

File.Name: 8124c_8123-059.jpg
Notes:
California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family bent down with snow on the edge of the garden yesterday. I got this photo from the upstairs deck at home. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2008)

The snow is mostly gone now after heavy rain this afternoon. So far, we've had about 6.5 inches (16 cm) of rain. Not bad! The forecast is for that much again - maybe much more - this weekend. This is a real treat after a record drought year!

File.Name: 8125a_8125-185.jpg
Notes:
Lone Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree holding on in the Santa Ynez River, with a background of willows and cottonwood and oak trees. It looks like it's trying to keep its balance in the moving water! (Santa Ynez River, 25 January 2008)

The river looks just like it should after 7+ inches of rain this week, and 18 inches this month. That's better than normal - and much better than our record drought last year when we only received 10.9 inches of rain all year. We're expecting another big storm on Saturday night that could really make the river flow!

I'm old enough to remember "the great rain" of January, 1969 when we got 46.8 inches of rain on the pass just in January, and 81 inches for the year! Every year is different, and that's what makes our mountains what they are. This mix of drought and deluge is tough on the plants and critters, but it's good for diversity.

File.Name: 8125b_8125-176.jpg
Notes: We have a river again! Here's the Santa Ynez River running like it should be. That's a clump of composite Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family holding on in the fast water, and winter-bare Willows and Cottenwood trees (Salicaceae) across the river. I got this photo in the rain after school today near White Rock in the Paradise area along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 25 January 2008)

File.Name: 8125c_8125-200.jpg
Notes: All the little mountain streams are running after all the good rain this week. The water is tea-color with soil and leaves. We've had 7 inches of rain this week, and 18 inches this month. That's better than normal - and much better than last year when we only received 10.9 inches of rain all year. But I'm old enough to remember "the great rain" of January, 1969 when we got 46.8 inches of rain just in January! Every year is different, and that's what makes our mountains what they are. This mix of drought and deluge is good for diversity. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2008)

File.Name: 8126a_8126-026.jpg
Notes:
The Slug Ouroboros! Well, the ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. This "worm" is a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) terrestial mollusk in the woods. I think it's doing something unmentionable that rabbits also do. I used on-camera flash for this photo. In the shade with new storm clouds approaching, even these slugs move too fast for a natural light long-exposure. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

These Pacific Banana Slugs are the largest terrestrial slugs in North America. I worry about these strange critters as the world becomes simpler. Fans root for banana slugs at UCSC, and I root for them in the woods. I think of them as an "indicator species" of where I want to live. So far I'm happy here in the Santa Barbara mountains!

I passed a small milestone at Fotolog today by posting photo number 5,000 to this Treebeard's Photos f'log - not counting all the other photos to groups. I'm proud that most of these are natural history photos from our canyon close to home, like a nature diary. *Sigh* Check out the [Recent Photos] archives to see what I've been up to all these years.
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; Pacific Banana Slugs

File.Name: 8126b_8126-003.jpg
Notes:
No surprise - after heavy rains this week, there are new mushrooms in the woods!

I believe this is a young white-spored Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushroom coming up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon. The grass blades are native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

Mushrooms are actually scarce today after a week of rain with more on the way tonight. Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of fungi, but they can't spread their spores in the rain. Somehow they know.
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens

File.Name: 8126c_8125-188.jpg
Notes: Road closed by the river at the first crossing in Paradise - that's the local name for this section of the Santa Ynez River above Lake Cachuma. The river (and lake) is muddy with runoff after heavy rains this week. I don't see trees floating down, so all is well so far. Another major storm is just starting to kick in as I write this. I'm really curious what's happening in the backcountry after the massive Zaca Fire burn last summer. Because it is the BACK-country, there's no easy way to investigate other that watching the debris in the river, but there is the potential of major sediment flows into the reservoir which is Santa Barbara's water supply. So far, so good. (Santa Ynez River, 25 January 2008)

File.Name: 8127a_8126-019.jpg
Notes:
This photo is like a CSI crime scene investigation. This is what I found in the woods yesterday in the lull between the rains - a very dead (and soggy) California Mole aka Broad-footed Mole (Scapanus latimanus) resting next to a scat that is mostly native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) berries. What critter killed the mole and left its dung to mark the spot? Why did the perpetrator break the rule of "you kill it, you eat it"? I guess there are some stories we will never resolve. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

It's another rainy day - 6.5 inches so far and still coming. No chance for photos today. I've been caching up with school stuff.
CommonNames: California Mole; Broad-footed Mole; Bigberry Manzanita

File.Name: 8128a_8126-011.jpg
Notes:
This new mushroom in the woods is some kind of Russula 'shroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The rosy colors are distinctive, and some species have brilliant red caps. Some Russulas are poisonous, but I like to nibble on them like the mice and wood rats have in this photo. They have a hot spicy taste that's more like wasabi than jalapeno. But just a nibble! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

I love the hunt for mushrooms, but I also love the chance to get down on my belly in the humus of fallen leaves to photograph them wqith the angular forms of the leaves. We've had lots of rain this month, and I look forward to a wonderful spring!

File.Name: 8128b_8126-002.jpg
Notes:
Pretty new Cortinarius mushroom pushing up a perfect halo of fallen oak leaves in the woods. (Maybe it's Cortinarius glaucopus or kin.) That's the hint for finding mushrooms to photograph or gather for the table. Don't look for the mushroom caps - they're too old by the time you see them. Look for the mushrumps, the little bumps in the oak leaves made by mushrooms pushing up. There are often new mushrooms underneath! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

I didn't collect this mushroom for the table. There are many different species that look the same, and some are poisonous. But they bring great beauty to the woods after the first heavy woods. I collect them with my camera instead!

File.Name: 8128c_8126-028.jpg
Notes: This is the same uncoiled "Slug Ouroboros" - a giant Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) terrestial mollusk in the woods - that I show two photos [Previous]. I took this photo a minute after the previous photo. If you compare their hindquarters before and after, you can see what it's been doing. I used on-camera flash for this photo. In the shade with new storm clouds approaching, even the slugs move too fast for the long-exposure natural light shots I prefer. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

File.Name: 8129a_8126-018.jpg
Notes:
This strange Giant Earthstar (Astraeus pteridis) mushroom - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - looks like it's walking through the woods on starfish-like legs! (Maybe they are more like triffids - or the tripods from The War of the Worlds!) It's really an odd puffball mushroom. It starts with a tough outer rind that splits radially into star-like points with a membranous spore sack inside that soon ruptures. The legs are hydroscopic - moisture sensitive - so they close up when dry and open down when wet to raise the spore case above the forest leaves. In the photo it looks like there might be another membrane in-between that sticks to the rays to make the checkered pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

These large earthstars are scarce in our canyon, but their smaller cousins (Astreus hygrometricus) that lack the checkered rays are common:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/28800998
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/29365363
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18922347

File.Name: 8129b_8126-037.jpg
Notes: This is looking under the cap of the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I believe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin). Like all boletes, it has sponge-like spore-bearing pores underneath instead of gills. This one has bright red and yellow colors on the stem. The yellow pores stain blue when bruised, though it's not related to those other notorious blue-staining mushrooms that do have gills. Note the bright yellow rhizomorphs at the base of the stem. These are root-like structures that are thicker than the usual mycelium that come from all mushrooms. It is said to be edible, but not wonderful. I've nibbled, but I haven't tried eating it. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

File.Name: 8129c_8126-034.jpg
Notes:
This fist-sized mushroom looks like a potato when you first notice it in the woods. I believe it is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin). Like all boletes, it has sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills, and this one has bright red colors on the stem. See my [Next] photo for the details. This particular bolete is said to be edible, but not wonderful. I haven't tried it. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

This mushroom also seems to pass the "nibble test" - like the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. Little critters in the woods have nibbled on the cap. But that's a really bad test. I have also found deadly Amanita mushrooms with nibble marks in the woods!

File.Name: 8130a_8126-007.jpg
Notes: Elegant brown mushroom coming up through the fallen oak leaves and stringy long leaves of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the woods. The debris on the cap is a sign that the cap is sticky or viscid. I believe this is the brown-spored Poison Pie (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushroom. It is not edible, but it's perfect for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

File.Name: 8130b_8126-016.jpg
Notes:
A pair of new Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods - one in the ground, and one that I pulled up nearby and laid down. These mushrooms are in the Bulbopodium subgenus - they have a sticky cap that collects woodland debris and a bulbous base with remnants of a universal veil. The mushroom on the right shows the spore-stained rusty-brown hairs on its stem that are the only signs of the beautiful "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered the immature gills. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

Cortinarius is the largest genus of gilled mushrooms, and they are very hard to tell apart. There are poisonous species, so it's good advice not to eat any of them - but I always seek them out for photos!

File.Name: 8131a_8126-005.jpg
Notes: I believe these are Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms, though they are soggy and dull after all the rain last weekend. The caps are wine-red when fresh, and the stems are smooth like wire, even in the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

File.Name: 8131b_8126-004.jpg
Notes: Small white mushroom in the woods after the rain last weekend. I'm not sure what it is, but my first thought was Hygrophorus. It really doesn't matter. It's fun to crawl in the woods looking for mushrooms, and the fallen leaves and twigs and lichens are so beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2008)

File.Name: 8201a_8201-150.jpg
Notes:
These first new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family look so exotic, but they are one of the earliest plants to flower in the woods after the recent rain and snow. The calyx at the base of the flower looks like it has tender claws holding the petals! The upper petals are united into a tube and the tiny lower petals have little yellow tips like clasping hands. The female stigma sticks out from the tube, and the male pollen-bearing stamens are coiled up inside. See my [Previous] photo for a photo of the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2008)

It sure looks like a hummingbird flower, and I have seen hummers work them even though this flower is only an inch or two (a few centimeters) from the ground.

File.Name: 8201b_8201-148.jpg
Notes:
Strange red leaves of the inflorescence of a native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the chaparral. This plant comes up fast like a mushroom and then disappears. It's a "scroph" in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's also a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place, though I also find it under manzanita. It has some green in the leaves at the base, but the foliage is mostly copper or red like this. (San Marcos Pass, 1 Fenruary 2008)

There are new flowers at the base of the stalk on the left - look for a bit of yellow. See my [Next] photo for a close shot.

File.Name: 8202a_8202-016.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushroom in the woods among the fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It looks like an arrangement for a still life, but that's just what I found and framed. Nature does that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

These little mushrooms with wiry stems have a connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain.

File.Name: 8202b_8202-005.jpg
Notes: Elegant white-spored Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushroom coming up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon today. They are edible, though the stems are tough. I haven't found enough to collect this year - and I'm still waiting for the choice mushrooms like Chanterelles. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8202c_8202-012.jpg
Notes: This handsome Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) has the color of the fallen leaves of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees it grows from. It's growing in a moist place in the woodland that supports native Juncus in the Rush (Juncaceae) family. Like all boletes, it has sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. This bolete is said to be edible, but not wonderful. I haven't tried it. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Rush
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Juncus; Juncaceae
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Juncus; Juncaceae

File.Name: 8202d_8201-105.jpg
Notes: New leaves of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae family in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This is another early perennial that comes up mushroom-like after the rains ready to flower. See my [Previous] two photos for a very different native with a similar habit. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 February 2008)

File.Name: 8203a_8202-002.jpg
Notes:
This Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera woke up grumpy this morning! The temperature was just a few degrees above freezing, but here it is on the side of our house trying to catch some warnth from the low winter sun. I guess this wap fell out of its nest. We have another nest of these wasps over the sliding door in the bedroom that we have to remember not to open in winter because dozens of these wasps fall out every time! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

We had another inch of rain today to keep the mushrooms going. This will be an interesting soggy spring after the drought last year!

File.Name: 8203b_8202-003.jpg
Notes: Tiny little gray Fly (order Diptera) perched on new growth of the Moss (Bryophyta) on a rocky bank in the canyon. This might be one of those flies than dance in the sunbeams. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8204a_8202-010.jpg
Notes: This brown mushroom in the woods is probably another rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom, though I didn't pick it to make sure. When mushrooms push up through the fallen woodland leaves, they seem to make a portal - or is it a portkey? - to another world in the earth that is mysterious and inviting - the stuff of fairy tales! This mushroom portal is especially inviting as the native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) leaves seem to define a gate, like the entrance to Moria in the Misty Mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8204b_8201-153.jpg
Notes: This brown mushroom in the woods is probably a rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom, though I didn't pick it to make sure. When mushrooms push up through the leaves and forest debris, they seem to make a portal to another world in the earth that is mysterious and inviting - the stuff of fairy tales! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2008)

File.Name: 8205a_8119-003.jpg
Notes: The rains bring out more than mushrooms in the woods, though I love hunting mushrooms for thrill of the hunt and their amazing diversity and the occasional grand meal. Here's something different - tiny spore-bearing Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes growing on a rock in the canyon. Mosses are commonplace, but they have a strange lifecycle with alternating haploid and diploid forms that look very different. I guess we do too, but we don't usually think of it that way! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8205b_8113-041.jpg
Notes: The rains bring out more than mushrooms. Here are new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns on a mossy rock in the woods where the sun never shines. Look close and you can see the outlines of new spore-bearing sporangia structures forming under the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8205c_8202-018.jpg
Notes:
Another mushroom pushing up through the fallen woodland leaves in the canyon. When mushrooms emerge from the earth like this, they seem to make a portal to another world in the earth that is mysterious and inviting.

I'm sure this is another rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom. You can see the spore-stained rusty-brown hairs on the pretty lavender stem that are the only signs of the beautiful "cortina" hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. This mushroom does not have a bulbous base, so I guess it is subgenus Phlegmacium. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8206a_8202-023.jpg
Notes: Looking under the cap of another rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom growing near the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. This mushroom is younger and has more intense colors. You can see the spore-stained rusty-brown hairs on the pretty lavender stem that are the only signs of the beautiful "cortina" hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. The debris stuck to the cap shows that the cap is viscid or sticky. It also has a bulbous rimmed base, so I'm sure it's in the Bulbopodium subgenus, probably in the Cortinarius glaucopus group. It's not drop-down-dead poisonous, but it's probably not edible. I've nibbled, and I'm not tempted to nibble again. But it sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8206b_8202-024.jpg
Notes: Another rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" of leaves in the woods. I expect to see tiny elves hiding in the space underneath, but centipedes and springtail insects are more likely. These Cortinarius mushrooms lose their color as they mature, but the bright spore-stained hairs on the stem and cap identify them. This 'shroom still has some purplish colors. It has a bulbous base, so I reckon it's in the Bulbopodium subgenus, probably in the Cortinarius glaucopus group. These mushrooms are not well-tested for edibility. I've nibbled, and I'm not tempted to collect them except with my camera. See my [Next] photo for a younger specimen growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2008)

File.Name: 8207a_8207-075.jpg
Notes:
Hah - one of my students found this mushroom growing in her locker at school today! Over Christmas vacation, we had to remove an iconic Willow (genus Salix in the Salicaceae family) tree at school that split at the base and was posing a threat. *Sigh* I remember when we planted that tree many years ago.

My student collected a piece of the tree and put it in her locker for sentimental reasons. Now she has mushrooms! I reckon this is a delicious Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotis ostreatus or kin). The tiny button caps growing from the fungus "spawn" at the base of the stalk are remarkable. This natural "mushroom log" will make a nice living bouquet in my science classroom - and maybe a perfect lunch! (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2008)

I'll post more close photos of the tiny button-caps when I can. Tomorrow is a late night with the fun school Talent Show, but I'll post soon.

File.Name: 8208a_8208-153.jpg
Notes: Shiny new foliage of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family in the woods near Lake Cachuma today. It's not really "poison" - but most people are sensitive to the oil in the leaves. Enjoy, but don't touch! I often hike through Poison Oak looking for photos. It doesn't bother me unless I really ask for it. Maybe that's why? (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2008)

File.Name: 8208b_8207-067.jpg
Notes: Tiny button caps growing from the fungus "spawn" at the base of an Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotis ostreatus or kin) growing from a piece of a Willow (genus Salix in the Salicaceae family) tree at school. It's a mushroom mosh-pit! See my [Previous] photo for more info about this natural "mushroom log". (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2008)

File.Name: 8209a_8209-062.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) surfing a new leaf in the garden today. Are you looking and looking and looking and looking at me?

Suddenly it feels like spring. The creek is still running, and we had T-shirt weather with temperatures in the 70s! I saw two different butterflies on the wing today - a little Sulfur (Colias) and California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica). I couldn't get the photos to prove it. Maybe tomorrow. There is new life in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8209b_8209-015.jpg
Notes: We had lizard weather today, with the creek still running and temperatures in the 70s! Here's a little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) I found resting on the rocks on the sunny-side of the canyon. I saw a few new insects as well - mostly flies - so I guess this lizard is ready to break-fast after winter. (I hope we have more winter and rain coming!) (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8209c_8209-007.jpg
Notes:
Native Land Snail (Gastropoda) shell in the woods. This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

I rarely notice the live native snails in the woods. See my photo at: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9497119

File.Name: 8210a_8209-036.jpg
Notes:
Learning mushrooms is like solving crimes - you have to get good at reading the evidence!

This mushroom in the woods is Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees - the first I've seen this year. You can see the last threads of the beautiful "cortina" hairs that once covered and protected all the immature gills - only three strands are still connected to the cap. The hairs turn rusty-brown from the falling spores. You can still see a rusty-brown rim around the edge of the cap where the rest of the cortina has pulled free. You can also see woodland debris stuck to the slimy cap of the mushroom, evidence of a "slime veil" - a thick layer of slime over the cap and cortina that quickly dries up, but leaves some residue on the stem of this species. More photos of this mushroom tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8210b_8209-056.jpg
Notes: This Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera seems to be sucking a leaf in our garden. This leaf happens to be directly under our hummingbird feeder, so I guess it's gathering the drops of sugar water that leak when the wind blows. There are many of these wasps waking up in the garden, though I'm not sure what they're looking for. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8210c_8210-073.jpg
Notes: Pretty little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a leaf in the garden. These flies are among the last I see before winter, and one of the first insects I see in spring. I guess they over-winter as adults and reproduce over summer. This seems like a good strategy for these flies whose larvae consume other insects' larvae - they are ready in the fall when eggs are laid, and again in spring as they emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2008)

File.Name: 8211a_8909-051.jpg
Notes:
These rusty-brown mushrooms in the woods are the same Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushrooms growing under the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon as my two [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

That's the problem with Cortinarius mushrooms - there are too many species that look very different when young, but they all end up looking the same with age. This species has a distinctive "slime veil" when young, and I can still find remnants of the slime on the stems of these mature caps. They are said to be edible, but I don't collect them for the table because I can't tell the many species apart. The ones I've nibbled don't taste very good either. I'd rather collect them with photos!

File.Name: 8211b_8209-022.jpg
Notes: This is the same kind of Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroon under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees as my [Previous] photo. This is an earlier stage, with the gills still covered by the "cortina" veil and a "slime veil" that collects debris on the cap and leaves deposits on the stem. It's maybe edible in spite nof the slime, but I'd rather wait for the Chanterelles. All the Cortinarius mushrooms are poorly known and hard to tell apart. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8212a_8208-149.jpg
Notes:
Pretty brown-spored mushroom growing with new grasses and herbs in the woods near Lake Cachuma. The spores are brown, but not rusty-brown like the more common Cortinarius clan. I guess this is Spring Agrocybes (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it - or collect them for dinner! (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2008)

I only collect a few mushrooms for dinner for dinner - those that are distinctive and delicious, like Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius). But it's great fun hunting all the other interesting and beautiful mushrooms with my camera!
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: 8212b_8909-049.jpg
Notes: Pretty Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. Like all boletes, they have sponge-like pores underneath instead of gills. The mushrooms are edible if you find fresh specimens, but they are quickly parasitized by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that seems to prefer this species among all boletes, and turns them into bright yellow mush. See my [Previous] photo. The first sign of infection is a patch of white mycelial hairs - visible in the lower right corner of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Hypomyces chrysospermum

File.Name: 8212c_8909-038.jpg
Notes:
Even mushrooms get mushrooms - fungi infections at least!

I believe this is (or was) an Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom that has been parasitized by the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that consumes boletes and turns them into an unrecognizable bright yellow gooey mush of spores. The bolete mushrooms are edible, if you can find them before the Bolete Eater does! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8213a_8209-045.jpg
Notes: I believe these large handsome mushrooms in the oak woods are part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. At first I thought they were more Cortinarius mushrooms because of the reddish-brown caps and stains on the stems, but they definately have white spores despite the distinctive reddish-brown stains on the gills. Another clue that they are Tricholomas is the strong "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. According to my books, these mushrooms are probably poisonous - not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. I'm not tempted to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8213b_8201-098.jpg
Notes:
Here's another photo of the same brown-spored mushroom in my previous photos, maybe the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 February 2008)

There's an important mushroom leason here. These are all the same kind of mushroom growing together, but look how the shape and colors change dramatically as the mushrooms mature. I don't see a partial veil "ring" on the stem, but I think I do see fragments around the edge of the young caps. Did you notice the little "buttom" 'shroom growing in the lower-left corner? Said to be edible, but mediocre at best. I didn't collect them.

File.Name: 8214a_8214-165.jpg
Notes:
Leaves of three, let it be.
Leaves of four, eat some more! - Homer Simpson

Shiney new bronze trifoliate leaves of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family in the woods near Lake Cachuma today. I guess the new leaves of this deciduous shrib are shiny with the surface oil urushiol, which causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits! Many people are sensitive to the oil on the leaves, but it doesn't usually bother me, and the deer and pigs and woodrats like to browse on this notorious shrub. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 February 2008)

File.Name: 8214b_8201-142.jpg
Notes: I guess these dark-spored mushrooms in the woods are Psathyrella longipes or kin. The mushrooms are fragile - they break apart when I touch them. Look very close, and you can bits of a white veil on the edge of the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

File.Name: 8214c_8209-047.jpg
Notes:
This is the same mushroom as my [Previous] photo - I guess it's part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. See my [Previous] photo for the details. Growing in the woods like this, it sure looks like an oak leaf pizza! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2008)

The leaves stuck to the cap are a sign that the cap is viscid or sticky when fresh. That's one of the clues you learn to notice as you get to know your local mushrooms. This Tricholoma is not edible, though others are.

File.Name: 8215a_8215-209.jpg
Notes: First new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the woods. The pretty urn-like flowers remind me of those candy "Easter eggs" with dioramas inside - but there's a different surprise here. These interesting native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) found the flowers before I did. I count three ants here - one on the prowl, one going in, and one in the hole! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8215b_8215-184.jpg
Notes: I use the acronym "LBM" for "Little Brown Mushrooms" - but it also works for Little Brown Moths like this! This little diurnal (daytime) LBM moth (order Lepidoptera) has brilliant red color on its hind-wings, but it's hidden at rest with wings folded. This moth is tiny - it could easily perch on a pencil erasor, but the colors catch my eye when it flies. I'll keep trying to get a photo with wings open that shows the color. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8215c_8215-222.jpg
Notes:
The first new flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family - at least the first flowers I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

Look at the main flowers up close - I'll number then 1 to 4 from left to right. Flower #3 is just opening, with only the female pollen-receptive pistil showing. In flower #1, you can see the male pollen-bearing stamens starting to emerge, and they are longer in flower #3. Now look at flower #2 and see how the male stamens are clasping the female stigma!

Many flowers have this strategy to give natural pollination a chance, but then self-pollinate at the end just to make sure pollen gets to ovule to produce a new generation of seeds. This is a backup in case the right insects and hummingbirds (etc) don't appear on time.

File.Name: 8215d_8215-195.jpg
Notes:
One side-benefit of prowling the woods for mushrooms is that I notice other sprouts that I would otherwise miss. I believe this is an uncommon sprout of our native summer-flowering Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii?) in the Orchidaceae family. I don't remember finding a mature orchid at this place, but orchid seeds are like fairy dust, and they must combine with a fungus to get started. I found a dozen orchid "seedlings" at this place. Maybe it has the right fungi? (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)
ScientificName:
Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii; Orchidaceae
associatedTaxa:
Piperia elongata

- or maybe P. michaelii; Orchidaceae

File.Name: 8216a_8216-044.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new flower of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. I got this photo in the woods today on Midland School property at the base of Figueroa Mountain. That's on the south-facing north side of the Santa Ynez Valley. I live on the north-facing south side of the valley. We're a little behind with the winter sun still low, but it won't be long before we have these wildflowers at home! (Figueroa Mountain, 16 February 2008)

File.Name: 8216b_8216-030.jpg
Notes:
Golden new California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) flower on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain today. We have other native poppies, but this species has the distinctive round disk under the flower. They are often seeded along the highways, but I reckon this is the real thing in the mountains. The spring wildflower season is just beginning after good winter rains this year! (Figueroa Mountain, 16 February 2008)

My digital camera seems to have trouble with yellows and oranges. I can't capture the real colors of these brilliant wildflowers. Going by my camera histogram and saved shots on the LCD, I always over-expose. It's a stumper. Why yellow?

File.Name: 8216c_8216-048.jpg
Notes:
A perfect bouquet of native Milk Maids (Cardamine californica) flowers in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. These early flowers have a pleasant radish-like taste and are good in a salad. It's also known as Toothwort (formerly in the genus Dentaria), but you have to dig it up to find the "tooth" - a tasty little tuber hidden underground. I don't do that any more to protect the plants, but I do like to nibble on the flowers! (Figueroa Mountain, 16 February 2008)

I got this photo in the woods on Midland School property at the base of Figueroa Mountain. I didn't use flash. This is a beam of sunlight with shadow behind - my favorite natural lighting.

File.Name: 8216d_8215-210.jpg
Notes: Interesting native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) "talking" by touch on new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the woods. There's another ant "butts-up" in the flower on the right. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8217a_8216-009.jpg
Notes:
We pulled the bark from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, and found these tiny Mycena acicula mushrooms growing from the decomposing wood underneath. This photo is very close with my macro lens, and a 1.3 second exposure @ f/32 @ ISO 200. The little 'shrooms are pinhead sizeYou can see the fungus mycelium growing from the mushroom stems into the substrate wood. I wonder why anything so small would bother to be so beautiful! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 February 2008)

I got this photo on a mushroom foray with Dr. Robert Cummings, local mushroom expert and longtime botany teacher at Santa Barbara City College. This Saturday workshop was sponsered by the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (I'm involved) at Midland School at the base of Figueroa Mountain.

Books and experience help, but the only real way to learn mushrooms is to foray with someone else who knows more. Mushroom knowledge is really passed on generation-to-generation, person-to-person, mano a mano. It doesn't matter how much you know, find someone who knows more and go for a walk!
Locality:
I'm involved) at Midland School at the base of Figueroa Mountain.

Books and experience help

File.Name: 8217b_8216-003.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little mushrooms growing from the rotting wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. My first thought was the Deer Mushroom Pluteus cervinus, but I was wrong. *Sigh* - don't trust *any* of my mushroom ID's!

I got this photo on a mushroom foray with Dr. Robert Cummings, local mushroom expert and longtime botany teacher at Santa Barbara City College. This Saturday workshop was sponsered by the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (I'm involved) at Midland School at the base of Figueroa Mountain. With Bob's help, I believe this mushroom is Psathyrella uliginicola - note "lignin" in the species name, part of what trees are made of. As David Arora says in his book "Mushrooms Demystified", this mushroom is unusually robust for a Psathyrella. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 February 2008)

File.Name: 8217c_8215-192.jpg
Notes:
I've been finding lots of these large dark-capped Entoloma mushrooms in the woods lately. I believe they are Entoloma ferruginans. They have "pink" spores and a surprising smell of bleach, like a swiming pool. I can't imagine eating it, though there are reports that it is edible. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

Why would a mushroom smell like bleach? Does it actually produce Sodium hypochlorite?

File.Name: 8217d_8217-086.jpg
Notes:
White cap, white gills, and a partial-veil "skirt" on the stem - I thought I'd found my first beautiful Amanita mushroom of the year! As soon as I carefully dug it up, I realized that it does not have the "death cup" volva-sac at the base - and it doens't have those "amanita vibes" that you only learn with years of experience!

I guess this is a beautiful - and edible - Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola or kin) mushroom. I'm sure it has chocolate-brown spores that will color the gills as it matures, though these gills are still white. Don't trust gill color to determine spore color! The cap stained yellow as I handled it, but the base of the stem did not. That partial-veil "skirt" on the stem originally was connected to the cap to protect the young gills. You can find the same veil on store-bought mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

File.Name: 8217e_8215-212.jpg
Notes: The new sweet-smelling white flowers of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) are starting to cover the south-facing slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains like snow. Sometimes you can see them from the beach, like snow in the mountains! The mass flowering is made up of gazillions of these tiny individual white flowers that go unnoticed as we drive past at 60 mph. Up close, the combination of white petals and hoods, yellow stamens, violet pistils, and the magenta fleshy gland around the base of the ovary is remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8217f_8217-054.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) catching sunlight in the woods - like lady dancers lifting their skirts in the air to show their petticoats! The red sepals catch our eye, but the white "petticoats" are the real petals. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

It's interesting how the heart-shaped male stamens clasp the female styles. They remind me of other native flowers in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family. They are "shakers" with male pollen-bearing stamens that clasp the female style tightly so that pollen falls out when an insect pollinator jiggles the flower, like salt from a saltcellar. I wonder if these gooseberries do the same thing?
Locality: Solanaceae) family. They are "shakers" with male pollen-bearing stamens that clasp the female style tightly so that pollen falls out when an insect pollinator jiggles the flower

File.Name: 8218a_8218-101.jpg
Notes:
I guess this dark-spored little LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - is a Psathyrella, maybe Psathyrella gracilis or kin. It's growing with new seedlings near a pile of garden trimmings and wood chips at home. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2008)

I took a deep breath today and went to Samy's Camera in Santa Barbara to buy a new tripod. I ended up with Gitzo GT2930EX legs, and a Gitzo G1275M off-center ball head for a very good price. This purchase was complicated because I have stripped out the tripod threads of my camera, but we managed to make it work. By the time I got home, the clouds had moved in - not good for photos, but I wanted to try my new tripod close to the ground on a little mushroom. It works!

I've been carrying my 30+ year old Leitz Tiltall and Slik 101 tripods into the woods for years - a heavy load, and I have to swap between them - but I think this new Gitzo will replace them both. I figure a crappy camera on a good tripod will make better photos than a good camera on a crappy tripod. Having a good tripod is that important!

File.Name: 8218b_8218-098.jpg
Notes:
New flowers and seed pods of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) - in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it's growing like a weed near the pile of garden trimmings at home. I first noticed this plant a few years ago, and it's spreading like a weed in our canyon, even though it is native to California. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2008)

I bought a new Gitzo tripod today. By the time I got home, the clouds had moved in - noy good photo weather, but I wanted to try my new tripod close to the ground. It works! See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 8218c_8217-077.jpg
Notes: It's hard to see - that's camouflage! - but here is a new Dragonfly (order Odonata, suborder Anisoptera) resting on the ground in the canyon yesterday. I'd been photographing mushrooms (that don't move) with long exposures and I only had one chance for this photo without checking camera settings, so the exposure is something crazy like f/32. It's still the first dragonfly I've seen since before Christmas in the winter season. They must over-winter as adults, ready to prowl on a warm winter day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

File.Name: 8218d_8217-059.jpg
Notes: Here's another mystery mushroom I can't identify from just the photo. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. It has pretty viotet colors on stem and cap. I guess it's another Cortinarius mushroom, though it doesn't have the usual rusty-brown hairs on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008) To really identify these mushrooms, I need to make a spore print and examine spores and gills under my microscope, and then consult the technical literature. I'm good with saying "I don't know!" I don't collect these mystery mushrooms for the table, but I love to stalk them in the woods for photos. It's interesting to compare mushroom photos from years' past in the canyon o see what I really have found. (That will be my school science fair project this year.)

File.Name: 8218e_8217-067.jpg
Notes: This is the same mushroom as my previous photo, growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. I'm don't know what it is. It seems to have brown spores on the gills, but there is a rusty-brown ring on the stem. I guess it's another Cortinarius mushroom, though it doesn't have the usual rusty-brown hairs on the stem. My first thought was Inocybe. It smells like a mushroom, nothing interesting like the exotic smells of many Inocybes. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

File.Name: 8218f_8217-070.jpg
Notes: Learning mushrooms is humbling - even after years of practice! I don't know what this pretty mushroom is that's growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. Fibrillose cap, partial-veil ring stained rusty-brown like a Cortinarius mushroom, but no cortina hairs on the stem. I guess it's some kind of Cortinarius or kin, probably not edible. But it sure is pretty for a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

File.Name: 8219a_8219-111.jpg
Notes:
Aren't these the real lyrics for the song "My Favorite Things"?
(Add John Coltrane soundtrack:
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I_n-gRS_wdI&)

Raindrops on lupines and whiskers on kittens;
Bright copper kettles and warm woolen mittens;
Brown paper packages tied up with strings;
These are a few of my favorite things...

Here are raindrops caught on seedling leaves of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae family on the dry side of our garden. Raindrops on lupines are some of my favorite things. Real rain knocks the drops off the leaves, but a light drizzle like we've had today is just perfect. For some reason - something to do with the hairs - the lupines catch the drops better than any other plant in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

I'm on school vacation this week - mid-winter vacation, aka Ski Week! The forecast is for rain all the rest of the week. Just as well, as I have lots of work to do, but I will make time for a few more photos!

I hope the rain clears for the total lunar eclipse tomorrow night (Wednesday, Feb. 20). It should be visible over all the Western Hemisphere, a real photo-op if your sky is clear of clouds and city lighta!

File.Name: 8219b_8219-106.jpg
Notes: Raindrops caught on a seedling leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae family on the dry side of our garden, like it's frosted with sugar crystals! Real rain knocks the drops off the leaves, but a light mist like we've had today is just perfect. For some reason - something to do with the hairs - the lupines catch the drops better than any other plant in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8219c_8219-117.jpg
Notes: New flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) posing with raindrops in the woods today. It was a gloomy day with some drizzle and little wind, perfect to captue the colors of these native flowers with long exposure and low contrast. They look like lady dancers holding their skirts in the air to show their petticoats! The red sepals catch our eye, but the white "petticoats" are the real petals. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8219d_8219-124.jpg
Notes: New flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) with raindrops in the woods today. It was a gloomy day with some drizzle, perfect to show the colors of these pretty dancer-like flowers with long exposure and low contrast. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8219e_8219-109.jpg
Notes: We have native orchids in the garden! Here is a new sprout of our uncommon native summer-flowering Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family. This is the wild side of the garden that we mostly leave alone, though this is only about 10 feet from where I park my car. I counted three other native orchids nearby. Sure I'm bragging! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8219f_8219-127.jpg
Notes: First new male staminate catkin flowers of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek in the canyon. I didn't see any insects paying attention, though it's been a cold drizzly day with rain on the way. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8220a_8220-141.jpg
Notes:
Pretty LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing from the green Moss (Bryophyta) on a rocky bank in the woods. I know there are some mushrooms that only grow on moss - and some of these LBMs are deadly poisonous. Enjoy, get photos, don't eat! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

I have a photo of maybe the same mushrooms growing from moss exactly one year ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18876770

File.Name: 8220b_8220-148.jpg
Notes:
Looking under the cap of a beautiful Agaricus mushroom in the woods today. I believe this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is poisonous to many people. It looks a lot like the edible Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola) mushrooms that I've been finding lately.

Compare my recent photo of A. silicola at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31198480

Both mushrooms have a large partial-veil "skirt" on the stem, and no "death cup" at the base of the stem. This skirt is the characteristic membrane that originally covers and protects the young spore-bearing gills. As the mushroom cap opens, the menbrane tears free from the edge of the cap leaving the "ring" on the stem and (usually) little fragments on the cap. The ring is this photo is thick and stands away from the stem, which is a sign that it is a different species that my previous photo where the thin ring has collapsed against the stem.

These small differences in taxonomy can make all the difference between a fine dinner and a night puking your guts out. It's not worth it to me. I like to nibble everything, but I only collect a few distinctive and safe mushrooms for the table. And darn it - I'm finding many mushrooms this year, but very few that I want to eat. Where are the chanterelles?
Locality: usually) little fragments on the cap.  The ring is this photo is thick and stands away from the stem
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; Agaricus silicola; A. silicola
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; Agaricus silicola; A. silicola

File.Name: 8220c_8220-146.jpg
Notes:
I'm really confused by our local Agaricus mushrooms. It's easy to recognize the group - brown spores, a ring on the stem, and no "death cup" at the base. But there are many species in the woods that range from delicious to poisonous.

This mushroom growing in the woods looks tasty and the cap smells good, but I believe this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is poisonous to many people. It looks a lot like the edible Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola) mushrooms that I've been finding lately, but the pinkish-brown fibrillose scales on the cap are distinctive. Edible or not, this handsome mushroom is another beautiful living creature in the woods to be thankful for. Give thanks! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

I generally avoid all Agaricus mushrooms except the tasty little Meadow Mushrooms (A. campestris) that are so sweet eaten raw in the woods... I know I'm missing some perfect meals, but I'm not puking all night either!
Locality: A. campestris) that are so sweet eaten raw in the woods... I know I'm missing some perfect meals

File.Name: 8220d_8919-113.jpg
Notes:
I can't resist one more photo of my favorite raindrops on lupines...

Really these are more like mist-drops, caught on a seedling leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family on the native side of our garden, like the leaf is frosted with sugar crystals or diamonds! Real rain knocks the drops off the leaves, but a light mist is perfect. For some reason - something to do with the hairs - the lupines catch the drops better than any other plant in the woods. I have more photos [Previous] to here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8221a_8220-137.jpg
Notes:
These remarkable new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family look so exotic, but here they are in the woods on a cold rainy day in mid-winter. The flower heads are mostly made up of bright red leafy bracts, but you can see a few open red tubular flowers near the base. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

This low winter plant has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. It is a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in this place, though I usually find it under Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae family. I guess the plants are common under the thick chaparral, though I only notice the flowers along trails that have been cleared to let some light in.

D'oh! I had my camera and long lens ready on my tripod last night, but clouds moved in just as the moon was rising to obscure the lunar eclipse. Today it's raining. My next chance for an eclipse photo at home won't be until December 21, 2010.
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae family. I guess the plants are common under the thick chaparral
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Figwort; Eastwood Manzanita; Chamise
