File.Name: 8221b_8219-122.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) dancing in the rain in the woods near home. The flowers look like lady dancers holding their skirts in the air to show their petticoats! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8221c_8220-133.jpg
Notes: These new buds of uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae family are getting ready to flower near home. This plant is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it only grows here in a few shady places where the sun never shines. The flower buds take a long time to develop - I first spotted these in mid-December. I find a few flowers every year, but I've never seen berries. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's trying to hold on against climate change. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

File.Name: 8221d_8220-130.jpg
Notes: I usally reserve the name "LBM" for "Little Brown Mushrooms", but there are many "Little Brown Moths" as well! I call these little brown moths "rain moths" because they collect on our windows at night by the hundreds if the lights are on during or after rain storms. Pretty good camouflage! I never would have noticed this one on fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves if I didn't see it land. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

File.Name: 8221e_8220-157.jpg
Notes:
I guess these are White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushrooms in the woods. They have a burning acrid taste that's more bitter than the other russulas I like to nibble on. They pass the "Russula test" - the stems are brittle, and you can snap them like a piece of chalk. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

The timing of our mushrooms depends on the rain, so it does no good to organize them by month when the rains are so variable. I think of the mushrooms as waves... The first wave are the little decomposers like Mycena and Psathyrella. Then comes the second wave of brown-spored Cortinarius mushrooms. They're still appearing, but now the third wave of Lactarius and Russula mushrooms are showing up, and I guess they will become common in this pretty good rain year. Now I'm also looking out for the fourth wave of the most beautiful Amanita mushrooms.

I'm still looking for the tasty Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms. I've only found one soggy specimen this year. They were more common last year during our record drought. Go figure!
ScientificName: Russula albidula; Mycena; Psathyrella; Cortinarius; Lactarius; Russula; Amanita; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Russula albidula; Mycena; Psathyrella; Cortinarius; Lactarius; Russula; Amanita; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: 8221f_8220-162.jpg
Notes: Hand-sized Cortinarius mushroom pushing up a large "mushrump" - the mound of raised leaves and moss - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. There are many species that are hard to tell apart. Some are poisonous, so I don't collect any of them for the table, or care much about scientific names I can't verify. I figure it's ok to make up my own non-scientific names, and it helps me recognize them again. I dub this one the "Earth-mover Cortinarious"! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

File.Name: 8222a_8216-007.jpg
Notes:
Orders of magnitude - powers of ten!

Up front is a pinky-sized little wood-loving mushroom of Psathyrella uliginicola growing from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. A mature mushroom cap is behind it, about an order of magnitude larger. Now look at those tiny red caps of tiny Mycena acicula mushrooms growing from the same rotting wood! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 February 2008)

Now if we could go 10 times smaller than this, and do it again, and again... The woods are full of life at all orders of magnititude, and I have to think it's all important, no matter how tiny - since here it is!
ScientificName: Psathyrella uliginicola; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena acicula
associatedTaxa: Psathyrella uliginicola; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena acicula

File.Name: 8222b_8217-091.jpg
Notes: Here's another "Earth-mover Cortinarious" mushroom - genus Cortinarius, I don't know the species - pushing up a large "mushrump" - the mound of raised leaves - under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. I believe the green plants are native Wood Mints (Stachys bullata) in the Mint (Labiatae) family. This mushroom is probably not edible, but I'm not sure. Let me know if you try it and survive! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Wood Mints; Mint
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Stachys bullata; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Stachys bullata; Labiatae

File.Name: 8222c_8215-219.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family - at least the first flowers I've found this year. We have at least four different species of Ribes in the mountains near home that all flower in mid-winter while the wildflowers are just sprouting. They are important flowers that keep the hummingbirds alive during the hard time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8222d_8220-154.jpg
Notes:
Here's the whole plant with the same kind of flower head I show in my [Previous] photo - native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The flower heads are mostly red leafy bracts, but you can see the real red tubular flowers sticking out near the base. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2008)

The flowers look like classic hummingbird flowers, and I have seen the winter hummers work these flowers just inches from the ground!

File.Name: 8223a_8223-001.jpg
Notes:
This native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae family is the mother-shrub that feeds our winter insects and humingbirds during winter when nothing else is flowering! There are new flowers in the woods, but this shrub keeps on flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2008)

I've been posting photos of the flowers and buds of this same shrub since September 30, 2007 - and it's still going!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/25487362

My photos today are all flowers and berries of this same shrub in the garden.

File.Name: 8223b_8223-004.jpg
Notes:
Immature red berry on the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae family growing in our garden with the sun behind this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2008)

That was the only sun we had today. An impressive winter storm is moving in tonight.

File.Name: 8223c_8215-172.jpg
Notes: Ripening berries of the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae family growing in our garden. The berries start red and then turn deep blue as they ripen. The birds pick off the blue berries as soon as they ripen, but they ignore the immature red-colored berries. The berries are glandular. You can see the little pin-head glands stuck in the berries like cloves in a ham.
Locality: Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae family growing in our garden. The berries start red and then turn deep blue as they ripen. The birds pick off the blue berries as soon as they ripen

File.Name: 8223d_8215-171.jpg
Notes: Ripe blue berries of the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae family growing in our garden. Everything about this plant is glandular. You can see the little pin-head glands stuck in the berries like cloves in a ham, or T2 viruses on an Escherichia coli bacterium! The immature red berries are bitter, but the birds know to wait until they turn sweet and blue. The ripe blue berries don't last long! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8224a_8223-009.jpg
Notes:
I can almost hear this giant terrestial Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) going "D'oh!" in its tiny mollusk Homer Simpson voice as it runs into a rock in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2008)

We've been having Banana Slug weather lately - several days of drizzle, and 2.5 inches of rain last night and today. Just as well, I've been busy with school stuff and grades. Classes start tomorrow after a wonderful vacation week.

File.Name: 8224b_8223-008.jpg
Notes: Mushrooms are hard enough to learn, but rain makes it even harder! I guess this is a soggy Entoloma ferruginans in the E. nidorosum clan. Even though it looks very different, I guess this is the same species as my [Previous] photo. They both have "pink" spores and a surprising smell of bleach, like a swiming pool. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2008)

File.Name: 8224c_8215-178.jpg
Notes: There are many of these large dark-capped Entoloma mushrooms in the woods lately. I believe they are Entoloma ferruginans. They have "pink" spores and a surprising smell of bleach, like a swiming pool. I can't imagine eating it, though there are reports that it is edible. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8224d_8119-006.jpg
Notes: Foliage of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the woods, catching a beam of sunlight with shadow behind. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2008)

File.Name: 8225a_8217-081.jpg
Notes: It's easy to tell if a mushroom is a Russula. Just give it the "Russula test" - the stems are brittle, and you can snap them like a piece of chalk. There are many different kinds. This one has beautiful violet colors along the edge of the depressed cap. I got the photo, but I didn't nibble. Maybe it's Russula cyanoxantha which is said to be edible and sweet, and maybe it's some other Russula that is spicy hot and poisonous if you eat more than a nibble. I don't really care. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2008)

File.Name: 8226a_8224-010.jpg
Notes:
New Crocus (Iridaceae) flower in the garden with rain drops from the storm that ended on Sunday. There's nothing better that the first light after a storm. Look close on the middle-right of the flower, and there is one drop that is a mirror of the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2008)

We got about 2.5 inches of rain over the weekend. We've had about 35 inches of rain since September, better than average. Not bad for what was projected to be another dry "La Nina" drought year!

I've been busy with school stuff - grades, comments, and all that - but I'm finally done. Today it feels like spring!

File.Name: 8226b_8219-116.jpg
Notes: Here's an unusual pair of new native summer-flowering Green Rein Orchids (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family in the garden! This is the wild side of the garden under the apple trees that we mostly leave alone, though this is only about 10 feet from where I park my car. I look forward to getting summer photos of the tiny flowers. Sure I'm bragging! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2008)

File.Name: 8227a_8227-104.jpg
Notes: Exquisite little sweet-smelling white flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the mountains. I had to look for a little flower cluster for this photo. The little flowers make clusters that cover the shrubs that cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains. You can see them from the beach, like snow. Up close like this, the flowers are intricate and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2008)

File.Name: 8228a_8227-107.jpg
Notes:
New purple flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family that is just starting to flower in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2008)

These flowers are "shakers" - the yellow male pollen-bearing stamens are curled into tubes with loose pollen inside. They clasp the extended female style tightly so that pollen falls out when an insect pollinator jiggles the flower, like salt from a saltcellar. Many familiar garden plants in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family have the same design and trick - tomatoes, chiles, eggplant, potatoes, and many more.
Locality: Solanaceae) family have the same design and trick - tomatoes

File.Name: 8228b_8227-110.jpg
Notes:
New pollen-bearing male flowers of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) growing in the mountains. It's in the same Cucurbitaceae family as cucumbers, melons, squash, and gourds, but you would never think of eating the spiny fruit. The flowers have a frosted but immature look. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2008)

This photo looks like flash, but it isn't. I caught the flowers in a beam of afternoon sunlight with shadow behind - my favorite lighting.

File.Name: 8229a_8229-180.jpg
Notes:
The flower is open!

At least I think this flower is open... It's a new walnut-sized flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The petals and sepals are wrapped up tight like a little cabbage. They always face the earth and start with a small opening at the bottom for pollinator insects to enter, like a fig. These plants usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a few years ago to give them light. The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the interesting flowers in the woods. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 February 2008)

File.Name: 8229b_8228-137.jpg
Notes:
Tiny white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 February 2008)

The exquisite little sweet-smelling flowers are nearly the same as the BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) flowers I posted two days ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31668647

The BigPod Ceanothus have alternate leaves and grow on the south-facing slopes and summits of the mountains. The Buck Brush Ceanothus has opposite leaves and grows over the mountains in the valley. The seed capsules are different. It gets more confusing because the BigPod Ceanothus have opposite leaves on the Channel Islands, and there are intermediates in our mountains!

I just get photos of what I find in the woods. Naming species is like giving names to clouds - there's always something in between!
Locality:
Ceanothus megacarpus) flowers I posted two days ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31668647

The BigPod Ceanothus have alternate leaves and grow on the south-facing slopes and summits of the mountains. The Buck Brush Ceanothus has opposite leaves and grows over the mountains in the valley. The seed capsules are different. It gets more confusing because the BigPod Ceanothus have opposite leaves on the Channel Islands

File.Name: 8229c_8228-151.jpg
Notes:
Large winter-bare deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family) and it's reflection in the pond as the sun goes down behind me. Yes the hills really are that green after the good rain this year! (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 February 2008)

This is the pond at the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve at the base of Figueroa Mountain on the south-facing side the the Santa Ynez Valley across from my north-facing home in the Santa Ynez Mountains. We had a Natural History Society board meeting with the Sedgwick Reserve director sitting under that oak tree yesterday, and watched the Red-winged Blackbirds circle around and disappear into the rushes that border the pond as the sun went down. Life is good!

File.Name: 8301a_8301-025.jpg
Notes:
Looking under the cap of a beautiful fist-sized Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom in the woods today. It looks delicious, but it is poisonous to many people. You can see the partial-veil "skirt" pulling free from the cap - it really does look like ripping a piece of felt. See my [Previous] photo, and compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31342277

As the mushroom cap opens, the membrane will tear free from the edge of the cap all around leaving a "ring" of tissue on the stem and little fragments on the edge of the cap. These are the clues we learn to identify mushrooms as they mature. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

After a sunny week, clouds moved in and we had drizzle and wind up in a cloud all morning. If you like mushrooms, look at all my photos today.

File.Name: 8301b_8301-023.jpg
Notes: Beautiful fist-sized Agaricus mushroom in the woods today. I was hoping this was "The Prince" - Agaricus augustus - but I believe it is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is poisonous to many people. See my [Next] photo to look under the cap of this 'shroom. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8301c_8301-031.jpg
Notes:
New Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) in the woods today - the first Amanita mushroom I've found in the woods this year. Amanita's are the most poisonous, or the best-tasting, and always the most beautiful of all our mushrooms - a genus of superlatives!

This mushroom is poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria - the iconic mushroom with red cap and white "stars". It's mind-altering for sure, if that description includes death! It has white spores, a partial-veil ring on the stalk, and the remains of the universal-veil as warts on the cap and the cup at base of the stalk. See my [Previous] photo for a look "under the skirts" of this mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8301d_8301-035.jpg
Notes: Looking under the skirts of a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) in the woods today. It has white spores, a partial-veil ring on the stalk, and the remains of an universal-veil cup at the base of the stalk. This mushroom is poisonous with the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria. It's mind-altering for sure, if that description includes death! This is the first Amanita I've found in the woods this year. The drops under the cap are interesting. We had cloud drizzle all morning, but these drops under the cap must have come from the mushroom itself. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8301e_8301-016.jpg
Notes:
These violet-capped Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods are old enough to show their true colors that separate them from the tasty Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. The top mushroom has made a natural "spore print" on the lower cap. Blewits have off-color white or dull-pink spores. The rusty-red spore color here is a sure sign of Cortinarius, which is not safe to eat - there are too many species that are too hard to tell apart. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

But there's no danger in photographing these beautiful 'shrooms!

File.Name: 8301f_8301-009.jpg
Notes: Edible and tasty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods today. This is listed as one of the "safe mushrooms" in the books, but be careful. There is a violet Cortinarius mushroom in our woods that looks very similar when young. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8302a_8301-052.jpg
Notes: A new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) and another flower bud getting ready to open. I got this photo in the cloud-drip drizzle yesterday. Look close and you can see the woods reflected in the drops! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8302b_8302-059.jpg
Notes: Many new flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family near home. This one shrub had too many flowers to capture in 3,000 or 500 pixels! It's a hummingbird shrub, and the hummers were working the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2008)

File.Name: 8303a_8301-006.jpg
Notes: New pollen-bearing male staminate catkin flowers of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek in the canyon. Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated - and maybe they are - but they also have sweet nectar glands under the dark scales at the base of the stamens. They do attract insect pollinators. See my [Next] photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8303b_8302-063.jpg
Notes: This is the same native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family near home as my [Previous] photo. There aren't enough pixels in this photo - or my camera - to show this many flowers! We've had good rain this year despite La Nina conditions. The early flowers are extravagant, and the hummingbirds are digging it. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2008)

File.Name: 8303c_8302-060.jpg
Notes: A line of new flowers dangling from native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family near home. These are hummingbird flowers, with long tubular pendant flowers that point down so that only hovering hummingbirds can get to the sweet nectar. At least that's the theory. Hummers were working the flowers, but so were some bees and flies who got into the flowers anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2008)

File.Name: 8304a_8228-131.jpg
Notes: Pretty "Little Brown Mushrooms" - with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe Clitocybe? It's a fairy tale scene with magic. There are many of these little 'shrooms growing along a little-used trail among new wildflower seedlings near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 February 2008)

File.Name: 8304b_8229-187.jpg
Notes:
This pretty brown-spored mushroom is growing with new grasses and herbs in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I guess it is the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family, but only because I found the same mushrooms in the same place near Lake Cachuma a few weeks ago. Compare my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/30979745
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31028530

They are said to be edible, but I only collect a few kinds of more distinctive mushrooms for the table. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 February 2008)

I'm more interested in the seedlings! There's native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) on the left, and whorled-leaf Bedstraw (Galium aparine) on the right, and many more in between. This is a photo of new life in the woods, with cool shadows!
ScientificName: Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Galium aparine
associatedTaxa: Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Galium aparine

File.Name: 8304c_8229-176.jpg
Notes: Perfect "Little Brown Mushrooms" ("LBMs") in the woods near Lake Cachuma with new wildflower seedlings. My guess is that it's a pink-spored Leptonia or Nolanea in the Entolomataceae. I didn't pick it or even look under the gills, so I'm not sure what it is, and I don't really care. They sure are pretty! (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 February 2008)

File.Name: 8305a_8301-050.jpg
Notes: For a moment, I thought this was a tasty Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom. Nah, I guess I was tired and hopeful. Chanterelles don't have thin gills like this. I swiped the gills with a stick and saw the white milky juice - a sure sign of genus Lactarius that is probably not edible. I guess this is the Golden Milk Cap (Lactarius insulsus) or kin, though I didn't collect it to be sure. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8305b_8301-048.jpg
Notes:
This sure looks like a tasty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods, but look closer! This is a non-edible violet Cortinarius mushroom in our woods that looks very similar when young. If you look very close under the cap, you can see that the gills have a rusty-red color that is not good for dinner. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

The mushroom cap is pushing up a pretty "mushrump" in the woods, with pretty native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) leaves in the photo. You don't have to eat mushrooms to enjoy them!
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Agrostis pallens; Galium californicum
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Agrostis pallens; Galium californicum

File.Name: 8306a_8301-045.jpg
Notes:
This pretty mushroom in the canyon is some kind of Russula 'shroom growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees after a drizzly morning. The shape and rosy colors are distinctive, though other species have violet or brilliant red caps. Most Russulas are listed as poisonous, but I like to nibble on them like the mice and wood rats have in this photo (in the back). They usually have a hot spicy taste that's more like wasabi than jalapeno. But just a nibble, and you should probably spit it out after you get the taste! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

Spring flowers are just starting to appear in our mountains, but this is the peak of our usually short mushroom season. We've had a good rain year, and I've been finding great beauty and diversity in the mushrooms growing in our canyon. That's good for photos, but for some strange reason, I haven't been finding many tasty edible mushrooms. So it goes.

File.Name: 8306b_8301-040.jpg
Notes:
I believe these crust-colored tan mushrooms in the woods are the brown-spored Poison Pie (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms. They smell a bit like radishes, but they are not edible. I often find them speckled with tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects stuck in the viscid caps when they dry. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

I pulled the smaller mushroom on the left away from the larger one to show the natural brown spore deposit on the cap. The color of mushroom spores is the first key characteristic in identifying mushrooms. The spores are too small to see, but you can see the color in aggregate by cutting off the cap and leaving it on a piece of white paper overnight to make a "spore print". I can usually guess the spore color by looking for natural spore deposits like this.

File.Name: 8306c_8301-013.jpg
Notes: I didn't think much of this rusty-spored mushroom when I found it in the woods last weekend - just another Cortinarius mushroom, at least it's in the Cortinariaceae family. Looking at the photo tonight, it really is a beautiful mushroom with elegant form and a golden winter-woodland color. I don't know what it is, but I hope I find more. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2008)

File.Name: 8307a_8229-175.jpg
Notes: Pretty new flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family starting to flower in the woods near Lake Cachuma. Pretty as they are, it's a non-native weed that does spread into the woods where it shouldn't be, though it usually sticks to disturbed places like trails. I've been seeing brilliant patches of these flowers in the open ranch land near school. They really do grow like wildflowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 February 2008)

File.Name: 8308a_8308-015.jpg
Notes:
I guess this little mosquito-size native fly (order Diptera) is Dance Fly in the family Empididae working the male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. It needs that long proboscis to get to the nectar glands hidden under the scales between the stamens. My books say that most species in the family are predacious, but some also visit flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

I find these pretty pollen-dusted flies every year working the willow flowers on the same tree by the creek:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19125313
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12640730
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12650062
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9481534

File.Name: 8308b_8308-007.jpg
Notes: Handsome fingernail-size Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) perched on a winter-brown leaf of Canna Lily (family Cannaceae) in the garden this morning. I believe the larval stages of these flies are predators or parasitoids of other insect eggs and larvae, so it makes sense that the adults are on the wing early in spring to find new hosts for their babies. One critter's gain is another critter's loss. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

File.Name: 8308c_8308-013.jpg
Notes:
I'm pretty sure this is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on the foliage of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

I've been finding these caterpillars since Christmas on different plants in the Scrophulariaceae family. Some butterflies migrate, or over-winter as eggs or pupae. These Variable Checkerspots are interesting because they overwinter as caterpillars. The adults are on the wing in June and July. I notice the late-summer caterpillars on native plants in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family, but they disappear before they mature.

My book says that they "overwinter or spend extended dry periods as partially grown caterpillars in a special 'thicker' skin with a more dense covering of spines." I guess they go dormant until about now when they emerge again. There may also be a late generation of eggs that hatch in spring.

I did see butterflies on the wing today, but I couldn't get a photo. I saw California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) and Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterflies. I wonder how they get through winter to be on the wing so early in spring?
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; Variable Checkerspots; Figwort; California Tortoiseshell; Brown Elfin
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Nymphalis californica; Callophrys augustinus
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Nymphalis californica; Callophrys augustinus

File.Name: 8308d_8308-029.jpg
Notes:
I guess this pretty mushroom in the canyon is the same kind of Russula 'shroom growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees as my photo two times [Previous] - I guess these mushrooms are both the Rosy Russula (Russula rosacea). It's not quite edible, but it has a spicy hot taste that I like to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

Last weekend may have been the peak of our mushroom season. I got many photos that I've been posting all week. Since then, the weather has been warm and dry. This is the only fresh-looking mushroom I found today.
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Rosy Russula (Russula rosacea
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Rosy Russula (Russula rosacea

File.Name: 8309a_8309-059.jpg
Notes:
I was looking at the new flowers of this native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family, so I almost missed this little Cicada (Cicadidae) hugging the branch below the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

Our cicadas seem to click rather than sing. I haven't heard them yet this year, so I guess this is one of the first to emerge.

File.Name: 8309b_8309-038.jpg
Notes: Handsome little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) perched in the spring weeds in the garden - mostly non-native Chickweed (Stellaria media) and annual grasses. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8309c_8309-022.jpg
Notes: It's a dirty job working the male staminate catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek - see my [Previous] photo. This native Dance Fly in the family Empididae (Diptera) is taking a break to wipe the pollen from its long proboscis with its forelegs. This fly is only interested in the nectar glands hidden under the dark scales between the stamens. Other critters will collect the hard-to-digest pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

File.Name: 8310a_8309-048.jpg
Notes: Looking close into a cluster of new flowers of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. The flowers are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same raceme - or sometimes bisexual with both stamens and pistils in the same flower, look close on the left. The female (or bi-) flowers have pretty red structures that will become the characteristic wings of the samara seeds that flutter down like little helicopters when you shake them from the branches in autumn. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8310b_8309-071.jpg
Notes: Another photo of the same new flowers of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. This is the only native Maple in our mountains, and it's not common. I've only found one tree in our canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8310c_8309-057.jpg
Notes: Burgeoning new flowers of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. Look close at the base of the flower stalk in the lower right, and you can spot the little Cicada (Cicadidae) insect hugging the branch that I show in my [Previous] photo. It's hard to notice the insects - even large critters like this - when there are new flowers emerging after winter! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8311a_8309-096.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma and maybe A. lanei - in the woods under native Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family). (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

This large Amanita mushroom has a cap about the size of the largest orange you can hold in your hand. It is said to be edible, though I make it a rule not to collect any Amanitas for the table, since some cousins are deadly poison.

In this photo, you can see some of the sac-like white cup or volva at the base and the soft white tissue on the cap that are part of the universal veil that once covered this mushroom like a soft egg shell. In the upper-right, you can see the partial veil membrane tearing free from the cap to expose the white gills and leave a skirt or ring on the stem.

I'm nervous about eating these beautiful Amanita mushrooms, but they are my favorite to collect with my camera!
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: 8311b_8309-090.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma and maybe A. lanei - in the woods under native Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family). This is the same mushroom as my [Previous] photo that I carefully pulled from the ground (and replanted) for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

This mushroom shows all the classic mushroom anatomy that you need to know before you start eating wild mushrooms. It has a sac-like white cup or volva at the base, and white tissue on the cap that are part of the universal veil that once enclosed this mushroom like a soft egg shell. You can also see the partial veil membrane that once covered the gills pulling free from the cap to leave a skirt or ring on the stem.

Some mushrooms with these same characteristics of universal and partial veil are deadly poisonous, so I make it a point not to collect any Amanita mushrooms for the table. Despite it's toxic relatives, this native mushroom is distinctive with that thick soft felt-like veil that gives it a cocoon look. It is said to be edible and delicious. Maybe I'll try it sometime.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; A. lanei; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

It is one of our most beautiful mushrooms in the woods, and it has Amanita power-vibes! See my [Next] photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

Notes: New flowers of native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family with the afternoon sun behind. There are many new flowers on the south-facing lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain. I hope to get back tomorrow after school. (Figueroa Mountain, 12 March 2008)

Notes:
Maybe this looks more like a moldy potato than a beautiful mushroom, nut this the early stage of the same kind of Coccora mushroom (Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma and maybe A. lanei) that I show in my [Previous] three photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

Here the soft white universal veil is starting to tear free as the mushroom cap expands, but the mushroom gills are still conceiled and protected. See my [Previous] photos for more info.

File.Name: 8313a_8313-176.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable new flowers of native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain after school today. It was windy, but these plants have stout stems and don't shimmy in the wind as much as most new flowers. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

The lily flowers have dark chocolate/burgandy colors - like that expensive 70% cocoa dark chocolate. It's easy to walk right past a group of these new flowers and mistake them for the dead remains of last years' flowers. This seems to be a good year for the Chocolate Lilies. Some of these little plants have 6+ flowers on the same stem like this!

File.Name: 8313b_8313-169.jpg
Notes: Here's a different Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. The lupine flowers in my [Previous] two photos are arranged in whorls - many flowers radiating from the same place on the stem. But these flowers grow one-by-one from the stem at different angles. I see a face in the flower - and nice whiskers! (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

File.Name: 8313c_8313-160.jpg
Notes:
New flowering plant of native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family with a single perfect whorl of new flowers. My [Previous] photo shows the same kind of lupine flowers. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

I went back to the same south-facing lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain after school today, but the wind was even worse than yesterday. I bumped my ISO to 400 and looked for protected places and waited for those moments when the wind drops to null.

File.Name: 8314a_8313-165.jpg
Notes:
I've seen a few butterflies this winter, but this is the first I've been able to capture with my camera!

I reckon this is a female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on leaves of a yellow-flowered Lotus - probably Bishop's Lotus (Lotus strigosus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I usually find these butterflies later in summer, but I believe this is the female cold-season form "cottlei" which has more blue on her upper wings.

As the seasons change near the vernal equinox next week, I have to rearrange my piles of books from desk to floor... and try to re-learn all the species names I forget every year!
Locality: Fabaceae) family. I usually find these butterflies later in summer
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus strigosus; Fabaceae; cottlei
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus strigosus; Fabaceae; cottlei

File.Name: 8314b_8314-212.jpg
Notes:
Not a great photo, but it was a great moment, and I got this photo to record it!

We were all gathered outside at school today after lunch, when I heard a cry and noticed this American Bald Eagle (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) flying overhead. I was lucky to get a photo, though this is a deep crop. There are resident and wintering Bald Eagles at Lake Cachuma about ten miles away, but I've never seen one flying over school in the valley before! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2008)

File.Name: 8314c_8313-189.jpg
Notes:
I thought these were new flowers of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing in grassland on serpentine soil on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain. But they look all wrong up close. The plants at home (Z. fremontii var. inezianus) come up after chaparral burns, and they are larger with more congested flowers that have green glands rather than these yellow glands at the base of the stamens.

I believe these flowers are the real Death Camas (Zigadenus venenosus). As both common and scientific names suggest, they are good for photos, but not good to eat! (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)
CommonNames: Chaparral Death Camas; Lily; Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Z. fremontii; inezianus; Zigadenus venenosus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Z. fremontii; inezianus; Zigadenus venenosus

File.Name: 8314d_8313-163.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new flower of native Blue Dicks (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish. It's another spring flower in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family flowering on the lower slopes of south-facing Figueroa Mountain across the Santa Ynez Valley from my canyon on the north-facing side of the Santa Ynez Mountains to the south. We have these flowers at home too, but they're still getting ready to flower. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

File.Name: 8314e_8312-146.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain. See my [Previouis] photo. Thanks for asking, Ependimo - the flowers look thick and waxy like yucca, but this plant is an herb that rarely grows a foot (30 cm) high. (Figueroa Mountain, 12 March 2008)

File.Name: 8315a_8314-201.jpg
Notes:
Red walnut-sized flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. The strange petals and sepals are wrapped up tight like a little cabbage. These plants usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a few years ago to give them light. The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2008)

Today's photos are a rainbow of color - like the traditional mnemonic "Roy G. Biv" - red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet - but without the indigo. The rumor is that Isaac Newton only included indigo because he wanted the number of colors in his spectrum to come out to seven to match the number of days in the week, the number of notes in the major scale, and the number of known planets. I can only post 6 photos a day, so I'll stick with ROYGBV!

File.Name: 8315b_8309-099.jpg
Notes:
Orange streaming plasmodium of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) on a decaying branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It (or they) are a remarkable critter that's part fungus and part giant amoeba. They (or it) live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. Sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" like this that can move to the surface. It will finally dry and form little mushroom-like structures that release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying branch to another. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

Today's photos are a rainbow of color - like the traditional mnemonic "Roy G. Biv" - red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet - but without the indigo. At least that was my plan. File.Name: 8315c_8309-108.jpg
Notes: Yellow flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family growing near home. The common California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica and kin) are herbs, but this head-high (or more) native shrub has flowers most of the year. I reckon it's a fire-follower, but it also feels at home along disturbed roadsides, so it's easy to find. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8315d_8308-011.jpg
Notes:
Green succulent leaves and new flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Purslane (Portulacaceae) family growing in the canyon. The upper umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf is really two separate leaves grown together and surrounding the flower stalk. Leaves and flowers are edible and tasty in a salad. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2008)

I've heard that this California native has become a weed around the world. Let me know if you've seen it at home.

File.Name: 8315e_8314-191.jpg
Notes:
Blue (close enough) flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2008)

These plants are listed as a different variety because they are quite glabrous - without hairs. Compare my photo of the species plants near home:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31712221

The closer you look at native wildflowers, the more you notice these little differences. This natural variation is evolution in action!

File.Name: 8315f_8313-173.jpg
Notes: Violet flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family. I got this photo across the valley on Figueroa Mountain. I haven't seen them flowering at home yet. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

File.Name: 8316a_8313-159.jpg
Notes:
Looking into the heart of a new flower of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. The flower has yellow on its pistil and stamens, but if you look close you will see a pretty tiny yellow Springtail (order Collembia) insect with dark eye-spots and long antennae perched on the column of the flower. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 March 2008)

This is a 500x500 pixel crop from my original 3008x2000 pixel photo taken on a windy day. I have another photo of the same plant at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32385228

We're heading off to Santa Cruz Island on a school camping trip tomorrow - if the winds don't interfere with our plans. I'll be back with interesting new photos on Friday!

File.Name: 8316b_8314-208.jpg
Notes:
Pretty white flowers of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) family. I found this colony of flowers in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2008)

These flowers have opposite leaves at the base of their flower stalks. At home we have the similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with alternate leaves at the base. These are the little differences that make our local plants interesting! See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/13391184

File.Name: 8316c_8309-104.jpg
Notes: Tiny mushroom button in the woods with secret veils still intact. It's the size of a pencil stub with eraser. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8316d_8309-050.jpg
Notes: Elegant new flowers of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Maple (Aceraceae) family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2008)

File.Name: 8316e_8314-195.jpg
Notes: This flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma is as open as it gets before it starts falling apart. The flowers point to the earth - I propped this up with a stck to look under its petals. See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2008)

File.Name: 8321a_8321-722.jpg
Notes:
Treebeard and entwife Julie ready to hike out from our camp on Santa Cruz Island in the Channel Islands National Park at the end of our wonderful school camping trip last week. We kayaked, hiked across the island and to the 1800+ foot summit of El Montanon and cleared invasive fennel as a public service project. (Santa Cruz Island, 21 March 2008)

We've been doing a spring backback trip with my school for years, and this photo is a tradition. Check out:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19946920

File.Name: 8322a_8320-654.jpg
Notes: Looking past pretty yellow flowers of Giant Coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family on the cliffs above Potato Harbor on Santa Cruz Island. The cliffs are hundreds of feet straight down and unstable, and the most interesting plants are always near the crumbly edge. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 March 2008)

File.Name: 8322b_8319-415.jpg
Notes: Looking down from the summit of El Montanon, the 1800+ foot high point on the east end of Santa Cruz Island. There are so many canyons to explore! (Santa Cruz Island, 19 March 2008)

File.Name: 8322c_8319-446.jpg
Notes: Show one - show another. Here I am on the 1800+ foot summit of El Montanom the high point on the east end of Santa Cruz Island with the ocean beyond. The next land to the south is Antarctica! (Santa Cruz Island, 19 March 2008)

File.Name: 8323a_8318-210.jpg
Notes: Kayaking towards a guano-rock near the Scorpian Anchorage on Santa Cruz Island. Yes, I'm kayaking too. I got this photo with our Olympus Stylus 500 digital camera that can survive getting wet. (Santa Cruz Island, 18 March 2008)

File.Name: 8323b_8318-230.jpg
Notes:
This is like one of those puzzles in a childrens' book - how many Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) birds can you see on this guano-rock near the Scorpian Anchorage on Santa Cruz Island? (Santa Cruz Island, 18 March 2008)

Interesting how the pelicans are so well camouflaged on the rock covered with guano! I got this photo from my kayak. I think I can see 7 pelicans here!

File.Name: 8323c_8320-577.jpg
Notes: Pretty female Western Tailed-Blue (Everes amyntula) butterfly resting on a plant of what I think is San Miguel Island Locoweed (Astragalus miguelensis) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. This plant is named for a different island, but my book says it does grow here on the bluffs near Potato Harbor. I've seen this butterfly near home, but the plant is a species of special enviromental concern found only on the Channel Islands. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 March 2008)
CommonNames: Western Tailed-Blue; San Miguel Island Locoweed; Pea

File.Name: 8323d_8320-603.jpg
Notes: We worked on Santa Cruz island last week to destroy introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family that grows as a weed and takes over native plant habitat. But I also noticed this new flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) growing in Scorpian Canyon that was introduced by Chumash native Americans from Mexico in the old days. This was a ritual plant for Native Americans used to induce visions. I could feel the ancient vibes. (Santa Cruz Island, 20 March 2008)
ScientificName: Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Datura wrightii; Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Datura wrightii; Solanaceae

File.Name: 8324a_8323-039.jpg
Notes:
Little native Ant (family Formicidae, order Hymenoptera) - maybe a California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin) - curled up around a new flower of this odd flowering Euphorbia in our garden. I guess these ants are also pollinators, with those yellow pollen grains stuck on the hairs on its head and abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

I have many more photos from Santa Cruz Island - I'll save them for a busy day!

File.Name: 8324b_8324-067.jpg
Notes:
Interesting tiny native Ants (family Formicidae, order Hymenoptera) working on a new leaf bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. Interesting shape with those spade-like abdomens and large heads. I wonder if they are tending the new eggs of the March Flies (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) that are oviposting on the buds of the same tree today? See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2008)

Sometimes I think that ants are the workers that hold our woods together.
CommonNames: Ants; Coast Live Oak; March Flies
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bibionidae; Diptera

File.Name: 8324c_8324-072.jpg
Notes: There were swarms of these busy little flies (order Diptera) in the woods today. I finally found out what they are up to. I believe this is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) oviposting on a new leaf bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon today. If you look close, you can see a yellow egg at the base of its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2008)
CommonNames: flies; March Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 8324d_8323-057.jpg
Notes: Brilliant blue flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. It's the most beautiful Ceanothus in our mountains, though it doesn't cover the mountains like some other Ceanothus species. The leaves are three-veined beneath, and rimmed with tiny glandular drops. The flowers have a juicy green glandular "biscuit" at the base of the flower to attract pollinators. I don't see many large insects visiting these flowers, but when I hold my hand under a flower cluster and shake the flowers, I get a handful of tiny beetles every time! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

File.Name: 8324e_8323-042.jpg
Notes: Thick flower head of uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae family. This plant is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it's uncommon in our mountains. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never seen a berry. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's trying to hold on against climate change. I've never seen such a dense flower head on this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

File.Name: 8324f_8322-017.jpg
Notes: New flowers of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) tree in the canyon. They are related to the Manzanitas (Arctostaphylos ). The urn-like flowers remind me of candle lanterns when they catch the light like this - or those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene hidden inside! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8325a_8323-089.jpg
Notes:
A pair of mating "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) on a leaf of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. Look at all the other bugs hiding under the leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

No time for new photos today. I'm heading down to L.A. shortly to help my Dad.
CommonNames: True Bugs; California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort

File.Name: 8325b_8324-062.jpg
Notes:
Mature caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) n the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. In the back, you can see the shed skin of another caterpillar. They molt several times times as they grow, and sometimes look quite different between molts. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

These Variable Checkerspots are interesting because they overwinter as caterpillars. I saw the first adult butterfly on the wing yesterday. Judging by all the caterpillars I've been seeing, this will be a good year for these pretty butterflies.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Figwort; Variable Checkerspots

File.Name: 8325c_8322-030.jpg
Notes: Little spider (Arachnida) in the woods. It's web was pretty chaotic with no particular organization that I could see. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8325d_8322-028.jpg
Notes: Little brown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) perched on an oak leaf. The bug has 5-segmented antennae, so I guess it's a true "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2008)

File.Name: 8326a_8326-122.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing - laying her eggs - on the bristly leaf of a volunteer Borage (Borago officinalis in the Boraginaceae family) herb growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

It's another one of those sex and food moments - note how she has her proboscis out along with her ovipositor, even though there is no food to gather on this leaf!

File.Name: 8326b_8326-123.jpg
Notes: Little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in the air approaching the bristly leaf of a volunteer Borage (Borago officinalis in the Boraginaceae family) growing in the garden. This native fly was intent on laying her eggs on this non-native plant this morning. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

File.Name: 8326c_8326-141.jpg
Notes: Looking over the ocean past new flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) flowers in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the mountains near home. This paintbrush has wooly-white leaves, unlike other local species. The dark band across the top of the photo is Santa Cruz Island where we camped last week. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

File.Name: 8326d_8326-157.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family near home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

File.Name: 8326e_8325-113.jpg
Notes: Interesting yellow flowers and inflated balloon-like seedpods of Bladderpod (Isomeris arborea) in the Caper (Capparacea) family growing near Point Mugu. This native shrub has an interesting distribution. It grows in the desert washes in the Joshua Tree desert, and also near the beach at Point Mugu, and on the Channel Islands off the coast - I got photos of this plant on our school trip at Santa Cruz Island last week. Where ever it grows, it has a strong unpleasant smell. (Point Mugu, 25 March 2008)

File.Name: 8326f_8325-098.jpg
Notes:
I went south to visit my Dad yesterday and drove past an amazing display of yellow flowers of native Giant Coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea) shrubs in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family on the bluffs at Point Mugu. There were so many flowers that the strange plants were hard to see. I found this plant growing by itself - with elephant-leg trunks and a canopy of yellow daisy flowers. (Point Mugu, 25 March 2008)

I posted a photo of this same native shrub on Santa Cruz island last week:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32734191

These plants also grow along the Gaviota Coast beaches and near Point Conception. They are like a floral bridge to the Channel Islands.

File.Name: 8327a_8327-198.jpg
Notes:
The remarkable flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family are still going strong in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I've been finding them since the beginning of February. The two stamens are usually bundled inside the flowers - you can see the outlines in silhouette - but the stamens of the second long flower on the left have spilled out. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

This early native plant has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae family in this place.
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: 8327b_8327-190.jpg
Notes:
Perfect blue flowers of Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This native flower is part of the dificult Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family, but nothing else has a color like this! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

Can you see the tiny orange mite and the little black beetle? I wonder what other little critters I've missed!

File.Name: 8327c_8327-163.jpg
Notes: Interesting new flowers of Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Olive (Oleaceae) family. Look close and you can see that the shrub has pinnate leaves, and the little flowers each have two white petals. This uncommon native shrub usually grows near the river, but it's not a riparian plant like willow or cottonwood. There are even a few plants along East Camino Cielo in the mountains near home. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

File.Name: 8327d_8327-174.jpg
Notes: The strange native flowers of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma are starting to fall apart. The petals and pollen-bearing stamens have done their work, and the thick-walled pistils are swelling with new seeds. You can see a bit of the bright-yellow nectary-disk glands under the stamens. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

File.Name: 8327e_8327-189.jpg
Notes:
It's spring wildflower time, so I really wasn't expecting to find any mushrooms today. I first thought this was a white styrofoam plate tossed along the trail - it's big enough! I'm pretty sure this is a large white Pine Cone Amanita (Amanita strobiliformis aka A. magniverrucata) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods near Lake Cachuma. Look close to see the remarkable volcano-shaped warts in the center of the cap! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

This large distinctive white Amanita mushroom doesn't have a distint ring on the stem or volva cup at the base. It smells a bit like dirty socks. My books say that it's edibility is unknown. Do you feel lucky?
CommonNames: Pine Cone Amanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Amanita strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 8327f_8326-121.jpg
Notes:
One more photo of this pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing - laying her eggs - on the bristly leaf of a volunteer Borage (Borago officinalis in the Boraginaceae family) herb growing in the garden. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

This mama fly really is bulging with eggs, but she didn't deposit them all at once like many insects do. The larvae of these flies are preditors of other insect larvae, so it makes sense to spread them around. The mama fly was hoveriung around the same plant this morning a day later.

File.Name: 8328a_8323-048.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new flower of a native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflower in the Iris (Iridaceae) family growing in the woods near home. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

File.Name: 8328b_8323-056.jpg
Notes: Looking close at the two pollen-bearing stamens and the two-pronged style of a native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

File.Name: 8328c_8328-207.jpg
Notes:
Handsome mating pair of "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera - maybe family Pentatomidae?) trying for a moment of privacy among the leaves of a native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) shrub in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2008)

It was very windy today. I managed to get a few photos between the gusts, but I gave up early to work on school stuff. My "spring break" vacation week is almost over!

File.Name: 8328d_8328-203.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the woods today doing some personal hygene. I don't know if these flies actually lick their feet like cats, but this fly is definately paying some attention to its feet. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2008)

File.Name: 8328e_8322-003.jpg
Notes: Unrolling new frond of native Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the canyon. I have to remember not to over-expose the shiny new leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8328f_8322-020.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new flowers of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) tree in the canyon. They are related to the Manzanitas (Arctostaphylos ). The urn-like flowers remind me of candle lanterns when they catch the light like this. They usually grow high in the trees. I have to hunt to find flowers low enough to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8329a_8329-008.jpg
Notes:
It was cold and cloudy today, but at least the wind died down. I found this beautiful creature perched inside a "Mood Indigo"-colored Ranunculus flower in the garden. I guess it is a Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae in the order Hymenoptera) - a wasp without the narrow waist. That yellow medallion on it's thorax between the wings is pretty cool! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2008)

I'll take clouds over wind any day for getting photos - there's less contrast and deeper colors, and the cold slows the insects down.

File.Name: 8329b_8329-001.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the garden this morning. It has golden vestiture fit for royalty. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2008)

It's interesting to ponder that these flies have a hard exoskeleton, so every golden hair must have an opening made just for it!

File.Name: 8329c_8329-005.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a winged male Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) perched on a new flower cluster of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family that grows next to our house. The same ants have been falling into our bathtub, and I have to sweep them out before my morning shower! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2008)

I don't see large black ants in the woods (or the bathroom). My guess is that it is the mating form of the ant in this photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32810151

File.Name: 8329d_8328-200.jpg
Notes: Unknown furry fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the woods - maybe a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae)? Catching photos of insects is like fishing in the ocean. Sometimes we get a hit at a particular place and time, but we know almost nothing about the life of the critter we capture. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2008)

File.Name: 8329e_8322-027.jpg
Notes: Looking head-on a different "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8329f_8324-088.jpg
Notes: A dogpile of mating "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) on a leaf of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. Maybe they are in the family Pentatomidae with 5-segmented antennae. Gotta chuckle! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2008)
CommonNames: True Bugs; California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort

File.Name: 8330a_8326-147.jpg
Notes:
Native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot with flowers (Marah fabaceus in the Cucurbitaceae family) growing in the mountains - throwing its tendrils ahead looking for new plants to cover. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

It was too windy for photos again today. Just as well. Spring break vacation is over. I go back to school tomorrow (as a teacher), and I spent the day getting ready for classes. I'll be busy for the next two weeks with my school science fair on April 11. Sure, I do a project too. I'm making a database of all my mushroom photos from the last five years of collecting for fotolog. I'll do by best to keep posting new photos here!

File.Name: 8330b_8326-154.jpg
Notes: This is the other white-flowered Ceanothus flowering in our mountains near home - Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. It has bicolored leaves with strange brown "corky stipules" at the base of the leaves. It grows higher in the mountains than our other white-flowered Ceanothus species. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

File.Name: 8330c_8322-011.jpg
Notes: I guess this is the flower of a grass-like Sedge (Cyperaceae) in the canyon, but I don't know these plants and don't have time to learn tonight. This native plant grows in the woods away from the creek. It seems to have pollen-bearing stamens at the end, and a separate bundle of pollen-receptive female pistils lower on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2008)

File.Name: 8330d_8327-199.jpg
Notes: Beautiful mushroom in the woods near Lake Cachuma. When mushrooms push up through the leaves and forest debris like this, they seem to make a portal to another world in the earth that is mysterious and inviting - the stuff of fairy tales! I didn't collect it, so I'm not sure what it is. It's built like a Russula but the gills are brown. øQuiÈn sabe? (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

File.Name: 8330e_8327-179.jpg
Notes: Large white Pine Cone Amanita (Amanita strobiliformis aka A. magniverrucata) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods near Lake Cachuma. The volcano-shaped warts in the center of the cap are remarkable. It's the size of a restaurant pancake - I first thought it was a discarded styrofoam plate along the trail! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)
CommonNames: Pine Cone Amanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Amanita strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 8330f_8327-196.jpg
Notes: Pretty spring flowers of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

File.Name: 8331a_8326-128.jpg
Notes: Beauitiful new flower heads of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) flowers in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the mountains near home. This paintbrush has wooly-white leaves, unlike other local species. The showy red "petals" are really leafy bracts. The real flowers are the yellow tubes that stick out from the red-tipped bracts and sepals. It's a good year for these native flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2008)

File.Name: 8331b_8323-045.jpg
Notes: Perfect new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family in the woods. It has two pollen-bearing stamens and a two-pronged style to receive the pollen. I guess it's a hummingbird flower, but it also offers the broad lower lip of the flower as a landing platform for large bees. Maybe it works like computers - always have a backup! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2008)

File.Name: 8331c_8327-192.jpg
Notes: A flock of new flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family near Lake Cachuma. The pretty flowers remind me of birds, so of course they are a flock! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2008)

File.Name: 8401a_8401-023.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable adult Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) perched on the window screen behind the kitchen sink when I got home from school today. I couldn't remove the dusty window screen without disturbing the moth, and there's no way to get outside here, so I did the best I could to get a photo.

There's a figure-ground reversal thing happening in this photo. The giant moth is outside and I'm inside with the window screen keeping us apart. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2008)

These large moths are 10-12 cm. across - swallowtail butterfly size - with wings spread! I rarely see these adult moths, though I sometimes find their egg-sized silk coccons in the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrubs in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family that grow in the canyon. See my photo of the coccoon at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/27697020
CommonNames: Ceanothus Silk Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn
ScientificName:
