File.Name: 8401b_8401-025.jpg
Notes: Remarkable adult Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) perched on the window screen behind the kitchen sink when I got home from school today. I couldn't remove the dusty window screen without disturbing the moth, and there's no way to get outside, so I did the best I could to get a photo. These large moths are 10-12 cm. across - swallowtail butterfly size - with wings spread! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2008)

File.Name: 8402a_8403-074.jpg
Notes:
Sky-blue Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family, with a golden field of California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) flower in the background. This is near the south-facing summit of Figueroa Mountain across the Santa Ynez Valley from my north facing home. (Figueroa Mountain, 3 April 2008)

My [Next] photo is taken further up this hillside near the poppies.

File.Name: 8403a_8403-055.jpg
Notes:
I took my after-school Hike Club kids to the summit of Figueroa Mountain after school looking for wildflowers. We found them!

Here are sky-blue Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family up front. Then there is a golden patch of California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. In the back are the round shrubs of Silver Bush Lupine (Lupinus albifrons) in the Fabaceae family. It's interesting how the different flowers separate themselves into different patches. (Figueroa Mountain, 3 April 2008)

There aren't enough pixels in this photo to show this many flowers!
CommonNames: Sky Lupines; Pea; California Poppies; Poppy; Silver Bush Lupine
ScientificName: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae; Lupinus albifrons; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae; Lupinus albifrons; Fabaceae

File.Name: 8404a_8404-121.jpg
Notes: Beautiful open flower of a native Larkspur (I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi) widlfower in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family in the woods near Lake Cachuma today. The deep blue/indigo colors are remarkable. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

File.Name: 8404b_8404-134.jpg
Notes: Strange flower bud of a beautiful native Larkspur (I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi) widlfower in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. The soon-to open flower parts are on the left, but it has that long nectar spur to the right. See my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

File.Name: 8404c_8404-133.jpg
Notes: Little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) working a new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the in the Rosaceae family growing near Lake Cachuma. It's interesting that there are many flowers this spring, but insects larger than gnats are scarce. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

File.Name: 8404d_8404-137.jpg
Notes:
Here are the first open flowers I've seen this year of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. As both common and scientific names suggest, they are good for photos, but not to eat! (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

I have photos of a different relative - note the colors of the glands at the base of the flowers:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32386025

File.Name: 8405a_8404-120.jpg
Notes: Another brilliant native flower of native Larkspur (I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family in the woods near Lake Cachuma. The deep blue/indigo colors are remarkable, and hard to capture. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

File.Name: 8406a_8404-122.jpg
Notes:
Here is some sacred geometry in new flowers of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family growing near Lake Cachuma. Note the pentagon around the five stamens and pistils, and the shadows. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

I've been busy this weekend - I never got outside for a photo. I'm making a database and slide show of my mushroom photos over the past 5 years for my science fair project - due at school on Friday. I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project too. This will be a busy week.

File.Name: 8406b_8404-125.jpg
Notes: There are still brilliant new flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2008)

File.Name: 8407a_8627-050.jpg
Notes:
Summer Boletes with garlic! I guess these are slices of the Queen Bolete (Boletus aereus) mushroom in the canyon. (27 June 1998)

I'm working on my science fair project (due Friday) organizing all my mushroom photos from the last ten years. It's fun finding these old photos that I don't think I've posted, at least not to this Treebeard page.
Locality: due Friday) organizing all my mushroom photos from the last ten years.  It's fun finding these old photos that I don't think I've posted

File.Name: 8408a_9424-171.jpg
Notes: I'm busy with my science fair project (due Friday!) organizing all my mushroom photos from the last 10+ years. I found this 10 year old photo in the archives of me holding a deadly poisonous white Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom in the woods on the Aliso Loop trail near the Santa Ynez River. (Santa Ynez River, 24 April 1999).

File.Name: 8409a_9204-009.jpg
Notes: Part of the fun of preparing my science fair project each year is going through decade-old photos and re-discovering old friends. Here are two former students on an after-school Hike Club adventure holding poisonous Amanita mushrooms near the second crossing of Paradise. (Santa Ynez River, 4 February 1999)

File.Name: 8409b_9211-041.jpg
Notes:
Part of the fun of preparing my science fair project each year is going through a decade of old photos and finding old friends. Here are former students on an after-school Hike Club adventure in Tequepis Canyon with mushrooms and white sage. These kids are past college now. (Santa Ynez River, 11 February 1999)

Richard and TJ - I'll see YOU at Live Oak 2008!

File.Name: 8410a_3116-104.jpg
Notes: A former student (now in college) with a handful of mushrooms on an after-school Hike Club adventure up Birabent Creek at the base of Figueroa Mountain. You can tell she doesn't like mushrooms, but anything for a photo! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 January 2003)

File.Name: 8411a_8411-017.jpg
Notes:
Here's my poster for the Dunn Middle School Science Fair tonight. I'm the science teacher, but I always do my own project. This year I went through all my 5,000+ fotolog photos for the last five years, and made a database and slideshow of mushrooms I've found near home on San Marcos Pass in themountains above Santa Barbara, Californiaa. I ended up with 143 species of mushrooms (and many unknowns) and 700+ photos in a PowerPoint slideshow. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 April 2008)

My students came through. It was a fine science fair - and fun!

File.Name: 8412a_8412-006.jpg
Notes: Cute little native Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) a few inches underwater in an old ceramic crock pot under the leaky faucet in the garden. The plants are cuttings of native Horsetail aka Smooth Scouring Rush (Equisetum hyemale ssp. affine). We keep several pots and old bathtubs around the house as water gardens for the frogs. You should do the same! The frogs have been busy making babies this spring. Note the frog eggs - and look closer for a tadpole! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2008)
CommonNames: Pacific Tree Frog; Horsetail; Smooth Scouring Rush
ScientificName: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Equisetum hyemale; affine
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Equisetum hyemale; affine

File.Name: 8413a_8413-019.jpg
Notes: This little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird has mated, and is making this bird house on the upstairs porch into a home, with a beak-full of soft grass stalks to pad the nest. The birds have been bringing sticks for several days, so I guess this is the next step to make the nest comfortable for Momma who will sit on the eggs. Of course there are two birds involved, and they are both busy. They echo their songs constantly across the woods, but I'm not sure which is which. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)

File.Name: 8414a_8413-025.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Beetles (order Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family flowering in front of our house. I guess these are little Rove Beetles in the family Staphylinidae because they have very short wing covers ("elytra") which expose the beetle's abdomen.

The individual Ceanothus flowers are tiny, but intricate - like little origami paper cups. This shrub reaches up above the upstairs roof line, and there are zillions of blue flowers this year. I'll try to remember to get a photo of the whole shrub after school tomorrow.

You won't notice many insects visiting the flowers at first. But if you hold your hand out under a flower cluster and whack the flowers, dozens or hundreds of little beetles fall out. This native shrub is full of life! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)
CommonNames: Beetles; Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn; Rove Beetles
ScientificName: Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Staphylinidae; elytra
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Staphylinidae; elytra

File.Name: 8414b_8413-023.jpg
Notes: Tiny Beetle (order Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) shrub in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family flowering in front of our house. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) working the match head-size flowers of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 8415a_8413-030.jpg
Notes: Little Beetles (order Coleoptera) - two if you look close - working a new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family) growing on the dry side of the garden at home. These perennial vines climb over and through other shrubs and trees and flower on top. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) - two if you look close - working a new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) - two if you look close - working a new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: 8415b_8415-044.jpg
Notes:
Large native shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family flowering in front of our house. The individual flowers are tiny, but there are gazillions of them on this one shrub, and I can't guess how many insects are working the flowers. See my two [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2008)

Our house is large, but simple. It's a remodeled barn. We mostly live upstairs. The wrens are nesting just around the corner of the deck on the right. It's like living in a treehouse!

File.Name: 8416a_8416-052.jpg
Notes:
The sun was still on the porch when I got home from school today. Julie was watering the potted plants when this little green Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) jumped onto the porch. I only got this one photo before it jumped away. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2008)

These frogs are color-shifters. I believe this is the same kind of frog with a dark eye-stripe as my photo last weekend:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/33725757

File.Name: 8417a_8412-010.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae family in the canyon. Even the blackberries died back during the drought last year. I'm glad they are coming back, though flowers are scarce. I'm seeing the effects of last year's drought in the woods this year, even though we've had "normal" rain. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2008)

File.Name: 8417b_8413-039.jpg
Notes: Brilliant flowers of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing near our mailbox. The red "petals" are mostly leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the slender red tubes with protruding pistil. At the very bottom you can see a single flower as open as it gets, almost like a related Penstemon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2008)

File.Name: 8418a_8404-161.jpg
Notes: At first I thought this was a bird turd on a leaflet of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family on the native side of our garden. It's a common camouflage. I guess it's a butterfly caterpillar (Lepidoptera). There's some silk webbibg on the leaf, so maybe it's ready to pupate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)

File.Name: 8418b_8412-012.jpg
Notes: Pretty little ladybug-size Beetle (order Coleoptera) resting on a leaf in the woods. This critter has ladybug colors, but the shape is all wrong - elongate rather than round. It got nervous and twitchy when I got close with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2008)

File.Name: 8419a_8413-041.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing on the flowers of a Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) shrub in the garden. I've been noticing these little flies in the woods dropping their eggs here and there on many different plants. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)

We're heading out to the desert this weekend to be part of an Audubon Society bird count on the dry bed of Owens Lake. I hope to return in a few days with some interesting photos!

File.Name: 8419b_8413-026.jpg
Notes: Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing on a leaf of horticultural Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. I've been noticing these little flies in the woods dropping their eggs here and there on different plants. The lucky eggs will become predator larvae that harvest the larvae of other insects. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2008)

File.Name: 8420a_DSC_0292.jpg
Notes:
Miles of California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family as far as the eye can see in the western corner of the Mojave Desert known as Antelope Valley. The wind was howling so hard that the poppy flowers were closing early today - no need for a tripod! (Antelope Valley, 20 April 2008)

It's been an interesting weekend from the poppy fields of OZ to the salt flats of Mordor. We participated in an Audubon Society bird count on Owens Lake east of the Sierra. The common factor was wind. I will have many photos to post when I get time to go through them. *Yipes* school tomorrow!

File.Name: 8421a_8419-010.jpg
Notes: Snow in the mountains and white alkaline Soda Ash "salt" deposits on the dry bed of Owens Lake. We participated in an Audubon Society bird count on Owens Lake east of the Sierra Nevada Mountains last weekend. Harsh as this landscape looks, we (all of us) managed to log 144 species of birds last Saturday! (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8421b_8419-058.jpg
Notes: It was a busy weekend moving from the poppy fields of OZ to the salt flats of Mordor! We participated in an Audubon Society bird count on Owens Lake east of the Sierra. Here are the alkaline Soda Ash "salt" deposits on the southern part of the dry lake bed, with the snowy Sierra Nevada mountains behind. Harsh as it looks, we found many birds in the brine pools. (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8421c_8420-246.jpg
Notes:
The road to OZ now has power lines to follow through the poppies so you don't get lost!

The color is from native California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family covering the ground in the western corner of the Mojave Desert known as Antelope Valley. It was very windy. (Antelope Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8422a_8419-091.jpg
Notes: Beautiful long-legged and long-beaked American Avocet birds with breeding colors at a brine pool on the south end of Owens Lake last weekend. Check my [Previous] photos to see how harsh this desert landscape is, yet we found many birds, and many different kinds of birds during our survey. (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8422b_8419-109.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Savannah Sparrow (Passerculus sandwichensis) bird on the alkaline shore of Cartago Springs at the south end of Owens Lake on the desert side of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8422c_8419-115.jpg
Notes: Great Egret birds on the salt flats of Owens Lake last weekend - white on white. (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8423a_8420-189.jpg
Notes:
Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense) growing at the base of a rock outcrop in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. We have many weedy thistles in California, but there's nothing commonplace about these amazing native thistles. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

After counting birds on Owens Lake last weekend, we went to our leased cabin on Lone Pine Creek. I made time to get some flower photos before the wind kicked up and we had to drive home to get ready for school on Monday. The word "desert" suggests empty, but this desert is full of life.

File.Name: 8423b_8420-223.jpg
Notes: Bright red Desert Paintbrush (Castilleja angustifolia) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family with the High Sierra mountains in the background. Just left of the highest flower, you can see Mount Whitney, the highest summit in the contiguous United States with an elevation of 14,505 feet (4,421 m). Many years ago, I camped on the summit of that mountain and watched the sun rise. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8423c_8420-201.jpg
Notes: I believe this is Desert Larkspur (Delphinium parishii ssp. parishii) growing from the dry sandy soil of the Alabama Hills at the base of Mount Whitney. It`s a shock to find this beautiful flower in the desert. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8423d_8419-097.jpg
Notes: More beautiful long-legged American Avocet birds with breeding colors at a brine pool on the south end of Owens Lake last weekend. Check my [Previous] photos. (Owens Valley, 19 April 2008)

File.Name: 8424a_8420-171.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful flower of native Mojave Aster (Xylorhiza tortifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family, growing in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. The flower seems to glow with it's own light.

Several of these flowers had a pretty moth inside that seems to be sleeping. The flowers are closed at night. This one is just opening in the early morning sunlight, and I wonder if the moth spent the night curled up inside. (And maybe also that little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera next to the moth - see my [Previous] photo.) (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

Anyone else remember Robert Heinlein's classic book "Red Planet" where the boys survive the frigid Martian night inside the cabbage-like plant that closes up to protect them against the cold?

File.Name: 8424b_8420-166.jpg
Notes: Little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) on a flower of Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. These flowers close at night. This one is just opening in the early morning sunlight. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8424c_8420-183.jpg
Notes: Another view of the beautiful native Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense) growing at the base of a rock outcrop in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. Compare my [Previous] photo. We have many introduced weedy thistles in California, but the natives are something special. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8425a_8424-329.jpg
Notes:
I noticed this butterfly caterpillar (Lepidoptera) hanging from a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family near our mailbox yesterday. I don't believe this is its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2008)

I reckon this is the first step of spinning a coccoon. It looks so vulnerable to attack hanging there with "hands" together. I thought of the old prayer:

   Now I lay me down to sleep,
   I pray the Lord my soul to keep;
   And if I die before I wake,
   I pray the Lord my soul to take.

This has real meaning for any insect about to pupate before becoming an adult - there are so many risks to growing up!

File.Name: 8425b_8419-159.jpg
Notes: Tiny Composite (Asteraceae) flower growing in the Alabama Hills desert. It looks like a Goldfields (Lasthenia) with wooly leaves, but the name escapes me right now. I put my cheap watch down on the ground for scale. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8425c_8420-195.jpg
Notes: I believe these are the flowers of native Desert Tobacco (Nicotiana obtusifolia aka N. trigonophylla) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family. The leaves have a real tobacco smell and contain nicotine. This plant was a ritual plant for Native Americans. I always get the vibes from ancient times when I find this plant flowering in the desert. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8425d_8420-229.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Desert Paintbrush (Castilleja angustifolia) flowers in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family growing against a "salt and pepper" granite rock near our cabin in the Alabama Hills at the base of Mount Whitney. (Owens Valley, 20 April 2008)

File.Name: 8426a_8426-034.jpg
Notes:
Adult Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) n the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2008)

There are a few adults on the wing, and many caterpillars of these butterflies in the woods. See my [Previous] photos.

File.Name: 8426b_8426-007.jpg
Notes:
Mature caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) n the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. There are many caterpillars and a few adult butterflies in the canyon today. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2008)

I'm not sure - are the caterpillars the same or different?

File.Name: 8426c_8424-324.jpg
Notes:
Social caterpillars (Lepidoptera) munching flowers of a native shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2008)

I've never seen this before. The caterpillars are mostly too high to reach with my tripod. They look like the larvae of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, but those caterpillars usually exclusively work plants in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. Like my [Previous] photos, I reckon this is family Nymphalidae). Whatever they are, I look forward to lots of adult butterflies in the garden!

File.Name: 8426d_8426-036.jpg
Notes:
If you didn't see my [Previous] photo that I posted yesterday, look at it now:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/34301136

This is the same creature as my [Previous] photo - then a caterpillar, now two days later a chrysalis, and soon to be an adult butterfly. I guess growing up is like that. I used flash for this photo - no choice with wind and shade today. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2008)

"It takes courage to push yourself to places that you have never been before... to test your limits... to break through barriers. And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom." - Anais Nin

File.Name: 8427a_8427-080.jpg
Notes:
Pollen-dusted Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) on a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home.

I don't think of these beetles as pollinators, but here it is dusted with pollen from young flowers and about to transfer it to this older flower. At first, the flowers hang their stamens down to dust the beetles with pollen. In time, the spent stamens rise and the female pistils move down to pisk up pollen delivered from other flowers for pollination.

This plant happens to be infested with Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera). I know the ladybird larvae are preditors of aphids, but I'm not sure of the adults. Anyway, this is what I saw today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)
CommonNames: Ladybird Beetle; Ladybug; Chaparral Death Camas; Lily; Aphids
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) on a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) on a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: 8427b_8426-083.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Beetle (order Coleoptera) working a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home. This place was cleared (and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

I should know this beetle by now, but my head is still full of mushroom and wildflower names, and I'm busy making my school slide show! It happens every year that I have to relearn all the names. When you take a walk with a naturalist, forgive them for muttering those latin names... that's the only way to remember!

File.Name: 8427c_8427-081.jpg
Notes: One single flower from a stalk of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home. These flowers have a pedestal of open male pollen-bearing stamens and still-folded-up female pistils. They remind me of a merry-go-round carousel! The green area at the base of the petals is a nectar gland to attract insect pollinators. The female pistils will drop down lower after the male stamens are done. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

File.Name: 8427d_8424-322.jpg
Notes:
Me and my shadow...

Pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) resting on a leaf in the woods. I guess it is family Pentatomidae with those 5-segmented antennae. Sorry, I don't have time to "run the key" tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

File.Name: 8428a_8427-051.jpg
Notes:
Intense colors of native the flowers of Larkspur - I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family growing in the woods along our driveway. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

The colors of the flowers are so intense that I can't capture them with my digital camera. They look deep indigo blue to the eye, but my camera always picks up the maroon violet colors that I don't see. I tried fiddling with curves and other color corrections, but I gave up. This is right from my camera. I guess If I were to post a music track with this photo, it would have to be a vintage recording of Duke Ellington's "Mood Indigo"!

Today's photos are all new wildflowers in the woods near home that I had trouble capturing the true colors of. It's better to be there!

File.Name: 8428b_8427-066.jpg
Notes:
Our real "California Poppy" on San Marcos Pass where I live is this Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae family. This poppy does NOT have a disk below the flower or seed pod like the more common E. californica does, and it's more yellow than orange in color. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

These native flowers are growing in a place that was cleared (and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break. I've been finding many "fire-follower" plants in this mechanically cleared place. I guess many of these fire-follower plants don't really need fire to sprout, but just a bit of clearing and sunshine.

This is another native flower that is so brilliant that it's hard to capture the color without overexposing like this.
Locality: and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break.  I've been finding many "fire-follower" plants in this mechanically cleared place.  I guess many of these fire-follower plants don't really need fire to sprout

File.Name: 8428c_8426-020.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - now given species rank as Styrax redivivus) - in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. They are just starting to flower in the canyon. The perfumed flowers aren't particularly showy from a distance, but they are elegant up close - maybe my favorite of all local native flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2008)

It's hard not to overexpose these pure white flowers when getting a photo. I went a bit over the limit.
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 8428d_8427-077.jpg
Notes:
One more photo of the same pollen-dusted Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) on a flower of native Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family growing near home. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

I'm not sure what species this is. The closer I looik, the more confused I get! But this is what I saw and captured with my camera yesterday.

File.Name: 8429a_8427-068.jpg
Notes:
These strange-looking flowers are the other "California Poppy" in our mountains. This is the Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae family. The flowers have many stamens, and the long tube above the flowers is the superior ovary that will develop into a seed capsule. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

These native flowers are growing in a place that was cleared (and "masticated") as part of a fire break last summer . I've been finding many "fire-follower" plants in this cleared place. I guess many of these fire-follower plants don't really need fire to sprout, but just a bit of clearing and sunshine.

File.Name: 8429b_8427-091.jpg
Notes: Beautiful yellow flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family growing in a place that was cleared for a fire break near home. The common California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica and kin like my [Next] photo) are herbs, but this head-high (or more) native shrub has flowers most of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

File.Name: 8430a_8426-042.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the in the Pea (Fabaceae) family near home - catching perfect light from behind. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2008)
